Atheromatosis of the aortic arch.

Triage Data

Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Palpitations
Anamnesis
No significant past illnesses. First cardiology consultation in November 2020 due to occasional rapid and irregular heartbeat. Symptoms used to occur once a month with a frequency of around 130-150 beats per minute lasting an hour. Holter monitoring and ergometry were done, but the arrhythmia was never captured.
Current Illness: For the past 10 days, experiencing rapid and irregular heartbeat even with minimal physical activity. Reportedly normofrequent only when lying down. Denies chest pain and shortness of breath.
Functions: Normal stool and urination.
Habits: Smokes more than one pack per day, does not drink alcohol.
Chronic Medications: None
Drug Allergies: None
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build and nourishment, conscious, alert, eupneic at rest, mobile, afebrile.
Skin and visible mucous membranes are well perfused. Peripheral lymph nodes not palpable.
Neck: Symmetrical, mobile, thyroid not enlarged on palpation. Neck veins properly filled. No vascular bruits.
Chest: Properly shaped, symmetrical, with even respiratory movements.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds on auscultation.
Heart: Regular rhythm, muffled tones, no murmurs, BP 110/80 mmHg. SpO2 97%.
Abdomen: Level with the chest, soft, elastic, non-tender on palpation, liver and spleen not palpable. Audible peristalsis. Lumbar percussion painless.
Extremities: Symmetrical, no edema, palpable peripheral pulses.
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response of 157/min, physiological electrical axis, no changes in the final oscillation.
Control ECG (12:35h): Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response of 92/min, physiological electrical axis, no changes in the final oscillation.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs, 1 Direction
Summary image of thoracic organs, PA and lateral views:
Marked bilateral interstitial markings with cranial redistribution of blood vessels and vascular congestion in the hila, indicating congestion changes.
Condensed pulmonary parenchyma seen retrocardially on the right basal and lateral view—prolonged inflammatory infiltration of the parenchyma cannot be excluded. Follow-up needed.
Right hemidiaphragm appropriately positioned, left flattened, and shallower costo-phrenic sinuses—pleural component.
Enlarged heart shadow.
Calcified aortic arch.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.3 [1e9]/L, RBC 5.00 [1e12]/L, HGB 155 g/L, Hct 0.479 L/L, MCV 95.8 fL, MCH 31.0 pg, MCHC 324 g/L, RDW-CV 13.4 %, Platelets 103 [1e9]/L, MPV 13.5 fL, NEU% 64.7 %, LYM% 23.2 %, MONO% 9.4 %, EOS% 2.1 %, BASO% 0.6 %, NEU # 4.06 [1e9]/L, LYM # 1.46 [1e9]/L, MONO # 0.59 [1e9]/L, EOS # 0.13 [1e9]/L, BASO # 0.04 [1e9]/L, NRBC/100 0 /100 leukocytes, PV-share 0.99, APTT 22.7 s, APTT R 0.85, Blood Glucose 7.6 mmol/L, Urea 7.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 79 µmol/L, eGFR 65.6 mL/min/1.73m², Total Bilirubin 17 µmol/L, Direct Bilirubin 6 µmol/L, Indirect Bilirubin 11 µmol/L, AST 53 U/L, ALT 81 U/L, GGT 38 U/L, LDH 247 U/L, ALP 93 U/L, Total Protein 65 g/L, Albumin 37.8 g/L, CRP 9.9 mg/L, Na 142 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, Cl 110 mmol/L
D-Dimer 0.69 mg/L
Therapy
Clexane 0.8 ml subcutaneously
Concor 5 mg 1x1
Lanitop ampoule
Diazepam 5 mg orally
Transfusion Therapy
No indication for urgent hospitalization in the Internal Medicine Clinic at this time. An echocardiogram and 24-hour Holter ECG monitoring are advised. Follow-up with a cardiologist for results and to decide on the need for anticoagulant therapy as per cardiologist's recommendation. Given the high risk of thromboembolic events, it is advised to initiate anticoagulant therapy—Martefarin or NOAC as agreed with the patient and the chosen family physician. Regular control of TSH, lipid profile, and blood pressure monitoring. Dabigatran Viatris 150 mg 2x1.
Conclusion
Senior service consulted. The evaluation showed no indication for urgent hospitalization. Due to the unknown duration of atrial fibrillation, conversion to sinus rhythm was not performed, and only rate control was recommended. Given the low thromboembolic risk (CHADs-VASc: 1, HAS-BLED: 1), it is advised to take Concor 5 mg 1x1 (if heart rate exceeds 110/min, increase dose to a maximum of 10 mg with blood pressure monitoring) for at least 4 weeks, after which a referral to the Cardiology Day Hospital (no. 557-253) for potential electrocardioversion is recommended.
Considering the frequency and symptoms of the arrhythmia, a consultation with an arrhythmologist for possible ablation treatment is advised.
Due to clinical signs of cough and RTG changes, it is recommended to start antibiotic therapy with Klavocin bid 2x1 for a week. Follow-up lab results after completing antimicrobial therapy. Control chest and heart X-rays in 3-4 weeks.
In case of worsening, report to the relevant emergency service!

Anamnesis
The patient is treated for arterial hypertension and type II diabetes for the past twenty years. In 2001, he suffered an acute myocardial infarction, after which coronary angiography confirmed multivessel coronary artery disease, and in May of the same year, he underwent triple coronary artery bypass grafting (LAD with LIMA, D1, PD with VSM). In January 2019, he was hospitalized in cardiology due to breathing difficulties and elevated cardio-selective enzymes due to acute pneumonia and heart failure with a minor myocardial infarction. Invasive diagnostics confirmed patent bypass grafts with multiple stenotic-occlusive changes in native coronary arteries. Echocardiography showed significantly impaired left ventricular systolic function due to ischemic cardiomyopathy. During the same hospitalization, due to pain in the left groin, an abdominal MSCT was performed, confirming a hematoma in the left psoas muscle (which was later monitored and regressed). He occasionally has episodes of atrial fibrillation and is on anticoagulant therapy. Due to ischemic cardiomyopathy with severe systolic dysfunction despite optimal revascularization and medication therapy, ICD implantation was indicated. A single-chamber implantable cardioverter defibrillator (Medtronic D364VRM, Protecta VR) was implanted for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death. According to the latest BIS report, stimulation parameters before discharge, as well as morphology discrimination, are normal; the device is programmed in VVI mode LRL 40/min with detection in VT (171-207/min) and VF (>207/min) zones.
Current Illness: In recent days, the patient reports severe weakness, stating that he can barely stand. He noticed black stools for the past 2-3 days. Today, emergency services were called, who noticed "elevations" in the ECG and referred him for admission as NSTEMI (received Andol 300 mg). The patient denies chest or abdominal pain, complaining only of weakness.

In 2014, she was treated for pleuropneumonia. In May 2020, she was examined at the Emergency Medical Service due to a cough and pain under the right rib, with suspicion of pleural effusion (excluded by pleural ultrasound). The pain stopped spontaneously. A follow-up MSCT of the thorax was recommended and performed on 15.9.2020: "anterobasal in the middle lobe slender pleuropulmonary scarring changes. In segment III of the right lung, a nodular lesion about 3mm, posteroapical in segment I/II of the left lung a 2x2mm nodular lesion is seen. No pleural thickening or signs of pleural effusion are seen. No enlarged lymph nodes are found. Follow-up recommended." On 13.03.2023, a control CT was performed: The previously mentioned small, insignificant lesions in the upper lung lobes do not show a change in size. No new nodular lesions, infiltrates, or consolidations in the lungs. No pleural effusion. No plaques or pleural calcifications are seen. Lymph nodes in the mediastinum, lung hila, and axillae are not enlarged. The heart is appropriately sized, without pericardial effusion. A few small hypodense, probably cystic lesions in the liver and a small calcification in segment VIII. A small calcification ventrally in the spleen. No lytic or sclerotic lesions suspected of metastases in the covered bones. 03/2023: Spirometry: FVC 103%, FEV1 101%, TI 0.81 - normal finding. She was last febrile seven days ago. Last night, she felt weak, broke out in a cold sweat, and had severe stomach pain and suprapubic pain. Previously, she hadn’t had a bowel movement for 10 days. She strained significantly during defecation, passing scant brown stool. She took Dulcolax, after which she had scant, mucous stool with fresh blood on the toilet bowl and toilet paper. Control with the primary care physician.

[Patient] presented with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response of 115/min, with a clear onset this morning around 09:30 (less than 24 hours prior).
Rhythm control was attempted with flecainide (300 mg orally) and beta-blocker therapy, leading to slowed tachyarrhythmia but with persistent atrial fibrillation.

Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily (maintenance therapy)
Ambulatory follow-ups:
24-hour Holter monitoring
Transthoracic echocardiography
Outpatient cardiology consultation
The patient is discharged in stable clinical condition, without complaints or symptoms. Instructions provided orally.

Diagnosed with chronic kidney failure. Underwent lumbar spine surgery in 2007. A stent was implanted in the RCA (right coronary artery) in December 2018. Hospitalized in April 2021 for heart failure, where echocardiography showed normal left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) with moderate mitral and aortic valve insufficiency. Coronary angiography revealed no significant changes in epicardial blood vessels.
In March 2022, rehospitalized for heart failure; echocardiography confirmed previously known diastolic dysfunction of the left ventricle with preserved systolic function and moderate insufficiency of mitral and aortic valves. Due to significant clinical improvement with medication, a conservative treatment approach was continued. Rehospitalized in May 2022 for heart failure presenting as mild dyspnea; symptoms resolved upon admission, and the patient remained asymptomatic during hospitalization.
In August 2022, suffered a compressive fracture of L1, a fracture of the 9th left rib, and left knee contusion due to frequent falls. Hospitalized in September 2022 for acute exacerbation of chronic kidney failure due to urinary tract infection and worsening of heart failure associated with ischemic cardiomyopathy (prerenal azotemia). Microbiological diagnostics confirmed urinary infection caused by E. coli AmpC. Stool testing revealed Clostridium difficile infection (toxins A and B). Anticoagulant therapy (DOAC) was discontinued due to worsening kidney function; thromboprophylaxis with enoxaparin at an adjusted dose was initiated. A consulting cardiologist recommended no specific therapy, including reduced-dose DOAC. Under psychiatric care for psychoorganic syndrome.
Between 30 April and 9 May 2024, treated at the Pulmonary Disease Clinic for dyspnea and productive cough associated with global respiratory insufficiency. Clinical improvement noted with residual respiratory insufficiency.
On 11 July 2024, examined at the Emergency Department due to general weakness.

Hypertension under therapy.
In December 2023, underwent polypectomy due to uterine polyposis; gynecological follow-ups are normal.
Monitoring thyroid function since 2016 due to chronic thyroiditis, with substitution therapy (levothyroxine) initiated in the same year.
In 2016, biopsy of a thyroid nodule in the left lobe showed a mixed goiter.
Thyroid Ultrasound (December 2022):
Thyroid gland of appropriate size, inhomogeneous, isoechogenic structure with diffuse hypoechogenic echoes.
Right Lobe (DR): Hypoechogenic region, 14x13x8 mm in the middle third.
Left Lobe (LR): Inhomogeneous, predominantly hypoechogenic nodule, 18x17x16 mm, with numerous punctate hyperechoic echoes. Adjacent smaller similar nodule, 6 mm.
Upper left lobe: Hypoechogenic area, 15x11x11 mm.
No significantly enlarged lymph nodes in the neck.
Cytological Findings (February 2023):
Hypoechogenic region in the upper third of the right lobe: Indicative of lymphocytic thyroiditis.
Hypoechogenic nodule with punctate hyperechoic echoes: Suspicious for papillary carcinoma.
Surgical Intervention (May 10, 2023):
Total thyroidectomy with pre- and paratracheal neck dissection performed at [Hospital] (ENT Clinic).
Postoperative course was uneventful. Calcium at discharge: 2.14 mmol/L.
Final Histopathology (PHD):
Left Lobe: Papillary carcinoma, 2.4x1.7x1.3 cm (classic type), with lymphovascular invasion, no extrathyroidal spread.
Right Lobe: Papillary carcinoma, 0.6x0.5x0.5 cm, without lymphovascular infiltration.
Pretracheal and left paratracheal dissection: 8/13 lymph nodes positive, largest deposit 8 mm. One lymph node infiltrated by tumor tissue (4 mm) without capsule penetration.
Radioiodine Ablation (June 16, 2023):
Dose: 100 mCi I-131.
Tg: 0.3 µg/L, Anti-Tg: 19.6 klU/L, TSH: >100,000 mIU/L.
Post-ablation scintigraphy: No pathological uptake in the thyroid bed or other areas.
The patient was hospitalized for follow-up after total thyroidectomy, neck dissection, and radioiodine ablation for metastatic papillary thyroid carcinoma (T2N1aM0). No signs of disease progression were found. Plan: Outpatient monitoring.
Current Symptoms:
Since yesterday: Swelling and pain in fingers of both hands (worse on the right).
Pain in the right ankle and plantar region of the right metatarsus.
Last week: Fever for three days (evenings only, max 38.9°C).
No chills, no rash, no nausea, no vomiting.
Lower back pain.
Feeling of a lump in the throat, no cough, no chest pain.
Night sweats attributed to heat.
Family History:
Mother had uterine cancer.
No significant rheumatic diseases.

Unremarkable.
Functions:
Normal urination, no dysuria.

Denies pregnancy. Normal menstrual cycles.
No weight loss.

Normal.

Denies drug allergies.

E. coli isolated - urosepsis. Due to severe microcytic anemia, gastroenterological workup (EGDS) confirmed chronic gastritis. Due to body mass and abundant panniculus, adequate kidney ultrasound or abdominal CT was not feasible. Hospitalized in the Gastroenterology Department in August 2024 for symptomatic normocytic anemia.

([Physician], M.Sc. Medical Biochemistry)

Findings and Opinion:
Chest X-ray (AP, lying): No convincing signs of fresh infiltrative changes in the visible lung parenchyma. In the projection of the left hilum, a round, homogeneous shadow 2.7 cm in diameter is seen, possibly a larger hilum or shadow summation. Recommend follow-up X-ray. Hila are large with prominent branching. Right hemidiaphragm flattened, left appropriately reduced, right costophrenic sinus clear. Heart shadow enlarged, almost completely covering the left lower lung field. Atheromatosis of the aortic arch.
Date of Findings: 01.04.2025, 19:41

([Physician], M.Sc. Medical Biochemistry and Laboratory Medicine)
Therapy:
Buscopan amp. N(II) in 500 mL 0.9% NaCl IV, Reglan amp. IV, Pantoprazol amp. IV.
Urinary catheter inserted - purulent urine in bag.
Conclusion:
Clinical examination, laboratory, and radiological workup ruled out absolute indications for hospitalization. Slight improvement in clinical presentation noted after applied therapy. Findings confirm urinary tract infection, for which Klavocin 1g tablets 2x1 daily for 10 days are recommended, along with ample hydration and probiotics. Blood tests to be repeated in 5 days, and urine culture with antibiogram after completing antibiotic therapy, adjusting treatment if needed. For irregular bowel movements, Portalak syrup 2x30 mL with a high-fiber diet, Buscopan tablets up to 3x2 daily, and Reglan 10 mg tablets up to 3x1 for nausea are advised. Follow-up with the primary care physician, and seek emergency care if condition worsens.

Echocardiography at an external facility diagnosed mild dilation of the left ventricle (LVDd 61mm) with preserved systolic function (LVEF 54%) and mild mitral regurgitation. Coronary angiography during hospitalization showed insignificant changes, idiopathic ventricular ectopic activity, almost asymptomatic. Holter ECG showed first-degree AV block. Hospitalized in May 2023 in [Hospital] due to non-sustained VT (5 QRS), underwent a normal exercise test, structurally healthy heart on ultrasound. Hospitalized in June 2023 in Infectious Diseases due to urosepsis after lithotripsy (UK: P. aeruginosa). Emergency esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGDS) performed due to vomiting of blood-stained content, revealing two erosions at the gastric fundus and bulbar erosions. ECG showed atrial flutter, leading to electrical cardioversion on September 27, achieving stable sinus rhythm. Last cardiologist check-up on October 19, 2023, showed atrial flutter on ECG, no anticoagulant therapy since then. Ablation treatment planned. Examined in December 2023 for pressure in the left chest, without radiation, with difficulty breathing. Chest X-ray and lungs normal, NTproBNP 2363 pg/mL, hs-TNT 41.7 ng.
- Since this morning, experiencing pain in the left hemiabdomen. Afebrile, no nausea, vomited gastric content once without blood. Normal urination. Last stool this morning, without blood. Denies chest pain and shortness of breath.
**Functions:**
- Appetite normal, no weight fluctuations. Normal urination, no dysuria. Normal stool, no pathological admixtures.

- Therapy: Duster Duo.

- Heart: Regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs, blood pressure 160/100 mmHg bilaterally.

**ECG:**
- Sinus rhythm, rate about 75/min, physiological electrical axis, occasional supraventricular extrasystoles (SVES), first-degree AV block, minimal ST segment depression up to 1 mm from V4-V6, I and avL (known from before).

The patient was examined due to pain in the left hemiabdomen. **Triage Data:**

- **Personal History and Past Illnesses:** The patient suffered a myocardial infarction (MI) 20 years ago and had one stent placed. He was hospitalized in the Cardiology Department in 2020 due to angina. An invasive cardiology examination verified borderline changes in the midsection of the left anterior descending artery (LAD) and circumflex artery (Cx), as well as subtotal stenosis of the distal right coronary artery (RCA). During the same procedure, a percutaneous coronary intervention was performed on the right coronary artery with the successful placement of a drug-eluting stent.
- **Current Illness:** Today, while working in the field, the patient experienced chest pain and shortness of breath, followed by loss of consciousness. He presented to the emergency medical services, where an ECG showed ventricular tachycardia (VT). He was treated with Cordarone 300 mg in 250 ml of 5% glucose intravenously.
- **Habits:** Does not smoke, does not consume alcohol.
- **Therapy:** Cardiopirin, hypolipidemic agent.
- **Allergies:** Denies any drug allergies.
**Status:**
The patient is conscious, communicative, and oriented in time and space, but experiencing tachypnea. Skin and visible mucous membranes are well perfused. Lungs: auscultation reveals normal bilateral breath sounds, SpO2 at 99%. Heart: rapid action, clear tones, no murmurs heard, blood pressure 113/72 mmHg. Abdomen: level with the chest, soft, non-tender on palpation, audible peristalsis. Limbs: no edema.
**ECG:** Ventricular tachycardia with a frequency of approximately 200 beats per minute.
**Diagnostic and Consultative Tests:**
Laboratory results are pending.
**Control Measurements:**
- Blood Pressure: 125/54 mmHg
- SpO2: 94%
- Control ECG: Sinus rhythm, frequency 66 beats per minute, physiological electrical axis, left bundle branch block (LBBB) from previous records.
**Therapy:**
- Hypnomidate 20 mg intravenously
- Reglan ampoule I in 100 ml of fluid intravenously
- Oxygen 6L/min via mask
- Atropine II ampoule intravenously
**Conclusion:**
In shallow analgesia and sedation with etomidate, synchronized cardioversion (200J) was performed, resulting in conversion to a stable sinus rhythm. The patient is conscious, in verbal contact, hemodynamically stable, with SpO2 at 96%.
Patient discharged with recommendation to come back if worsened.

Triage Data:
Triage Category: 3; Blood Pressure: 100/70 mmHg; Pulse: 98/min; SpO2: 98%; Glasgow Coma Scale: 15;
Vital Signs

Rapid Heartbeat
Medical History:
Personal and Past Medical History: Since September 2024, the patient has been treated for colon adenocarcinoma with metastases to the liver, lymph nodes, and peritoneum. From October 14, 2025, the patient underwent chemotherapy following the FOLFIRI + bevacizumab protocol, completing a total of 4 cycles by January 10, 2025. From January 23 to February 17, 2025, the patient was hospitalized in Surgery due to bowel perforation. An emergency resection of the small intestine was performed with the placement of an ileostomy.
Current Illness: Since 4 AM, the patient has experienced a rapid heartbeat. The patient denies chest pain, pressure, and shortness of breath. The patient reports having similar symptoms last summer, but they were shorter in duration and resolved spontaneously.
Functions: Normal urination, stool through the stoma, normal.
Therapy: Aspirin Protect 100 mg tablets (Fraxiparine 0.4 ml was prescribed upon discharge, which the patient replaced with aspirin on their own initiative a few days after hospital discharge), Controloc 40 mg, Megostat 10-20 ml, Lordiar, Supportan, Fresubin, Normabel as needed.
Allergies: Denies any drug allergies.
Status:
The patient is of weaker musculoskeletal build and nutrition, conscious, communicative, oriented in time and space, mobile, eupneic at rest, afebrile, anicteric. The skin and visible mucous membranes are well-perfused. Neck veins are not distended in a semi-sitting position. The tongue is moist and not coated.
Lungs: Bilateral normal breath sounds on auscultation, SpO2 98%.
Heart: Rhythmic, accelerated action, muffled tones, no murmurs heard, Blood Pressure 100/70 mmHg.
Abdomen: Level with the chest, soft, non-tender on palpation, liver palpable more than five fingers below the right costal margin. Audible peristalsis. Ileostomy placed in the right hemiabdomen.
Limbs: No edema, palpable peripheral artery pulses.

Pulmonary arteries show appropriate branching and normal lumen width bilaterally. Up to the level of subsegmental pulmonary arteries, there are no signs of contrast filling defects indicative of pulmonary embolism.

The patient was examined due to a sensation of rapid heartbeat. The electrocardiogram recorded paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, which was converted to sinus rhythm with medication using adenosine. The performed MSCT angiography ruled out the possibility of pulmonary artery embolism. The patient remained hemodynamically stable, afebrile, and respiratory sufficient throughout observation. Perform TSH, free T3, free T4, and 24-hour Holter ECG. Administer Fraxiparine 0.4 ml once daily subcutaneously as previously recommended along with the current therapy. Follow up with the oncologist as planned. In case of condition worsening, seek medical attention.

MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis, iv. contrast according to multiphase protocol:
The liver shows decreased parenchymal densities, with several cystic lesions appearing as cysts, the largest in segment II with a diameter of 1.7 cm. Indicated gallbladder wall of reactive change type, no mineral lithiasis visible intraluminally. The pancreas shows homogeneous post-contrast enhancement with a more voluminous appearance of the pancreatic head.
Dense peripancreatic fatty tissue with a small amount of fluid (especially near the head of the pancreas) descending along the anterior renal fascia. At the time of imaging, no convincing signs of necrosis are seen. The described changes correspond to acute pancreatitis. A small amount of free fluid is seen in the lesser pelvis and paracolic regions. Common bile duct measures 0.8 cm. Accentuated proximal pancreatic duct. The wall of the described ducts is indicated. Kidneys are of appropriate size and position, with a couple of parapelvic cysts with a diameter of 1.3 cm. Urinary bladder poorly distended, uniformly thickened wall. Prostate of heterogeneous appearance, size 5.8 x 4.7 cm.
In sections through the lung bases, a right pleural effusion 1.5 cm wide is shown.
No signs of pathological bone remodeling are seen.

Conclusion
Acute pancreatitis CTSI 2-3.

Finding date 07.10.2025. 10:16
Abdominal Ultrasound
Gallbladder with normal wall thickness, suspicious for sludge.
Pancreas mildly edematous, hyperechoic, without zones of necrosis or formed collections, a thin layer of free fluid peripancreatically. No ductal dilatation. Prominent and tortuous initial part of the pancreatic duct.
Perform fecal elastase, MR of the LS spine, physiatrist/neurologist examination p.r.n.
GE follow-up with fresh labs and control abdominal ultrasound (fasting) in one month 07.11.2025. at 08:00 ([Hospital], 3rd floor).

Conclusion:
Mr. [Patient] is a patient between 65 and 70 years of age who is hospitalized at the Department of Gastroenterology due to epigastric pain.
Initial laboratory and radiological workup confirmed acute pancreatitis.
On the ward, he was treated with ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, pantoprazole, hyoscine butylbromide, furosemide, amlodipine, potassium citrate, ipratropium bromide, dutasteride tamsulosin, diclofenac, paracetamol, low molecular weight heparin, enteral preparations, and crystalloid solutions. The administered therapy resulted in clinical and subjective improvement.
He is discharged to home care in stable condition with the recommendation to take the following therapy:
- ciprofloxacin 500mg, 2x1, for 5 days
- metronidazole 200 mg, 3x1, for 5 days
- pantoprazol 40mg, 1x1
- Vital 2x1 (NRS >3)
- DusterDuo 1x1
- DIETARY MEASURES!
Control blood count via GP in one week.
Perform fecal elastase, MR of the LS spine, physiatrist/neurologist examination p.r.n.
GE follow-up with fresh labs and control abdominal ultrasound (fasting) in one month 07.11.2025. at 08:00 ([Hospital], 3rd floor).

In case of complaints, report to the ED (OHBP).

NOTE: For every new examination, please bring this finding.
Instead of the prescribed medicine, the chosen primary health care doctor can prescribe another medicine of the same efficiency class.

report_content:
Triage Category: 4

Diagnoses
R10 Abdominal and pelvic pain

Triage Data
Triage cat: 4; BP: 140/90 mmHg; Pulse: 110/min; SpO2: 96%; VAS: 3-Mild pain; GCS: 15;

Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Abdominal pain

Anamnesis
PRESENT ILLNESS: Since this afternoon, has had dull pain in the LLQ, vomited stomach contents 3x, denies fever, dyspnea, and chest pain.
PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
The patient is between 65 and 70 years old and is being treated for Crohn's disease; last gastroenterology check-up 3/2025 - colonoscopy planned. In 2/2022, treated with mechanical ventilation due to pneumonia as part of a Covid-19 infection. Follow-up examination by a pulmonologist in 08/2022 with no further pulmonology follow-up required. Examined in 05/2024 at the Emergency Hospital Department (OHBP) due to high blood pressure values. Due to albuminuria, examined by a nephrologist; MSCT urography performed. Cholecystectomy performed in 12/2024. Known arterial hypertension, gout, and hyperlipidemia. Examined in 5/2025 at the emergency department due to abdominal pain. 2025 EGDS: Duodenal bulb ulcers (Forrest III). Previously known CKD, cystically changed left kidney. Appendectomy performed in 08/2025.
CURRENT THERAPY: Sulfasalazine 500 2x1, Allopurinol 100 mg 1x1 tab, Coupet 20 mg 1x1, Controloc 40 mg 1x1, Exforge 10/160 1x1, Letizen 10 mg 1x1
DRUG ALLERGIES: Denies
FUNCTIONS: Stool is normocholuric, without pathological additions, last one this morning. Urination is normal without dysuric symptoms. Appetite is weakened, no weight loss.
HABITS: Does not smoke, does not drink alcohol.

STATUS
Conscious, oriented, mobile, eupneic at rest, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes have normal perfusion. Lymph nodes not palpable.
Lungs: normal breath sounds on auscultation.
Heart: rhythmic heart action, clear tones, no murmurs heard.
Abdomen: at chest level, slightly tense, painful on palpation in the LLQ, bowel sounds audible, liver and spleen not palpable.
Lumbar succussion (LS) negative bilaterally.
Extremities: symmetrical without edema.
___________
ECG: sinus rhythm, rate 93/min, physiological heart axis, inverted T waves in aVR, V2, V1.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 23:05
Findings and Opinion
Emergency MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis, native and with i.v. contrast:
Dilatation of small bowel loops is visible, lumen width up to 4 cm, edematous walls, with clear visualization of the transition point paramedially left, at the level of the iliac crest - mechanical obstruction due to adhesion - chronic appendicitis.
A small amount of free fluid is visible interintestinally.
Colon with collapsed lumen, no haustrations.
Cholecystectomized.
Hydronephrosis of the left kidney, grade V.
Other findings unremarkable.

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 21:01
Findings and Opinion
Plain radiograph of thoracic organs, PA and left profile:
In the lung parenchyma available for analysis, no convincing signs of fresh infiltrative changes are seen.
Hila are vascular, with normal terminal branching.
Diaphragmatic domes and associated costophrenic (f.c.) angles are free.
Cardiac shadow is enlarged.
Plain abdominal X-ray:
On the plain abdominal X-ray, no free gas is seen under the diaphragmatic dome.
Several small air-fluid levels of the small bowel are visible.

Report Date: Oct 25, 2025, 20:43
HEMATOLOGY: WBC 7.0 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.76 [1e12]/L, HGB 137 g/L, Hct 0.428 L/L, MCV 89.9 fL, MCH 28.8 pg, MCHC 320 g/L, RDW-CV 15.1 %, Plt 210 [1e9]/L, MPV 9.2 fL. HEMATOLOGY: NEU% 76.2 %, LYM% 16.8 %, MONO% 6.3 %, EOS% 0.4 %, BASO% 0.3 %, NEU # 5.32 [1e9]/L, LYM # 1.17 [1e9]/L, MONO # 0.44 [1e9]/L, EOS# 0.03 [1e9]/L, BASO # 0.02 [1e9]/L. HEMATOLOGY: NRBC/100 0 /100 Lkc. COAGULATION: PT-share 1.21, INR 0.92, APTT 23.6 s, APTT R 0.94. PHYSICAL-CHEMICAL URINE EXAMINATION: APPEARANCE turbid, COLOR yellow, pH 5.5, SP. GRAVITY 1.023 kg/L, GLU neg., BIL neg., KETONES neg., Hb trace, PROT 4+, UBG norm., NITRITES neg., LEU E 1+. PHYSICAL-CHEMICAL URINE EXAMINATION: Urine Leuk. 10 /high power field, Urine RBC 3 /high power field, Squamous epith. few /high power field, Hyaline casts rare /high power field, Bacteria some /high power field, Mucus some /high power field, Granular casts 1 on the entire slide, Fine granular casts 2 on the entire slide. BIOCHEMISTRY: Glucose S 7.8 mmol/L, UREA 6.5 mmol/L, CREA 107 μmol/L, eGFR 46.4 mL/min/1.73m2, T-BIL 8 μmol/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: AST 20 U/L, ALT 25 U/L, GGT 36 U/L, LDH 161 U/L, ALP 93 U/L, AMS-s 51 U/L, Lipase 33 U/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: CRP 4.4 mg/L. BIOCHEMISTRY: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl - S 106 mmol/L, Ca 2.37 mmol/L.

Therapy
Tramal 50 ml iv in 100 Fo. Buscopan amp iv

Conclusion
Refer to surgeon, admission to surgery.

Triage data
Triage cat: 4; BP: 185/90 mmHg; Pulse: 95/min; SpO2: 114%; GCS: 0;

Vital signs
Reason for arrival
Pain in the upper quadrants.

Anamnesis
Personal history: In 02/2018, underwent surgery for adenocarcinoma of the cecum; a right hemicolectomy with ileotransverse anastomosis was performed. Subsequently received adjuvant CT (chemotherapy) according to the CAPEOX protocol (5 cycles, 16.03.18 – 20.06.18). Last oncology check-up in 09/2025 – albuminuria and proteinuria, follow-up in one year.
Current complaints: For the past week, has been feeling slight pain/discomfort in the upper quadrants bilaterally with propagation to the back. The pain is constant, of equal intensity. Denies nausea and vomiting. Has been febrile up to 38°C for the last 3 days. GP prescribed Citeral 500 mg, which she has been taking since 24.10. (took the last tablet this morning). This morning, a rapid antigen test (BAT) for Covid and Influenza was performed – neg.
Functions: Appetite normal, no significant fluctuations in body weight. Stools regular, without pathological admixtures. Urination more frequent, without dysuric complaints, urine normally colored, nocturia 2x.
Habits: Non-smoker. Does not consume alcohol.
Medications: Nihil.

report_content:
**Diagnoses**
**J22 Unspecified acute lower respiratory infection**

**Anamnesis:**
**Reason for visit:** Initial examination by a pulmonologist.
**Personal history and past illnesses:** The patient, between 55 and 60 years old, has been followed by a neurologist for many years due to severe spastic paraparesis of unknown etiology.
In January 2024, the patient recovered from an influenza viral infection with pneumonia. Examined by an infectious disease specialist in February 2024 – physical therapy via home care services was advised until admission for inpatient treatment.
**Current complaints:** Presenting for an initial examination due to an infiltrate in the left lung described on a chest X-ray (Nov 13, 2025).
- reports coughing in a supine position (in the evening when lying down), reports a sensation of shortness of breath while speaking. Reports significant weight loss over the past 2 years. Febrile in June and September up to 38°C, lasting 3-4 days, with a productive cough during those episodes. Denies chest pain. Denies "wheezing in the chest." Leg swelling occurred. Denies night sweats and excessive sweating. Reports constantly elevated CRP (does not provide laboratory findings).
**Physiological functions and Habits:** appetite fair, weight loss over the past 2 years. Diaper in place. Bowel movements regular, without pathological admixtures. Urination without burning. Former smoker, quit 30 years ago, 20 cigarettes/day for about 20 years. Does not drink alcohol.
**Medications:** does not take chronic therapy, Brufen as needed.
**Drug allergies:** [None listed]

**Status:**
Of slender build and nutritional status, afebrile, anicteric, eupneic, conscious, contactable, oriented in time and space, poorly mobile, in a wheelchair at the time of examination. Skin and visible mucous membranes well-perfused. Jugular veins normally filled. Heart action regular, sounds clear, no murmurs heard. Diminished breath sounds over the lungs posterobasally, bilaterally. Abdomen soft, painless on palpation, peristalsis audible. Lumbar succussion painless bilaterally. Limbs symmetrical, discrete edema of the lower legs.

**Findings:**
**Date of finding: Jan 30, 2024, 12:43**
**Chest X-ray, sitting:**
On the lung parenchyma available for analysis, no certain signs of fresh inflammatory changes are seen.
Hilovascular markings of normal distribution - calcified mediastinal lymph nodes.
Cardiac silhouette slightly enlarged.
Diaphragmatic domes appropriately shaped, costophrenic (f.c.) sinuses obscured – pleuropulmonary adhesive changes.

**Nov 13, 2025. Follow-up chest X-ray, AP sitting:**
An X-ray from Oct 29, 2025, is available for comparison.
Inhomogeneous opacity is still visible on the left in summation with the cardiac shadow caudohilarly and left basally, with reduced translucency of the left pulmonary base; the medial, underlying part of the left dome is partially unclearly delineated with the left lateral costophrenic (f.c.) sinus obscured, so a small amount of pleural effusion cannot be ruled out. Band-like opacities right laterobasally – differential diagnosis: fibrotic changes. Calcified shadow in the projection of the left hilum, 1.6 cm in diameter – differential diagnosis: calcified lymph node. More intense shadow in the middle pulmonary field on the left peripherally, 1.1 cm in diameter – differential diagnosis: pleural plaque.
The right hemidiaphragm is flattened, the right lateral costophrenic (f.c.) sinus is shallower. Hila are of appropriate terminal distribution, and the caudal distribution of the left hilum is partially summed with the described opacity. Cardiac silhouette size is within physiological proportions.
In the follow-up interval, the finding is without significant changes. According to the clinical presentation, further workup is advised (pulmonologist examination and MSCT of the thorax).

**Oct 28, 2025.** E 5.06, Hb 136, MCV 82.9, T 360, L 5.6, seg 69.5%, ly 17%, mono 10.5%, bazo 1.2%, GUK 4.9, urea 6.4, creatinine 67, ALT 11, AST 17, GGT 23, ALP 66, K 5.1, Na 140, Fe 11.

**Conclusion:**
It is advised to perform laboratory tests (CBC, differential blood count, CRP, LDH, Na, K, Cl, Ca, urea, creatinine, AST, ALT, GGT, NT-proBNP, urine). Microbiological analysis of sputum is advised (culture and sensitivity, Koch's bacillus, fungi). Pulmonology follow-up with findings.

**NOTE:** Please bring this finding to every new examination.
Instead of the prescribed medication, the selected primary care physician...

report_content:
**Diagnoses**
J44.0 Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with acute lower respiratory infection

**History:**
Reason for visit: pulmonologist follow-up, last exam on Nov 13, 2024.
Personal history and previous illnesses: Patient between 70 and 75 years old is being treated for type 2 diabetes mellitus, BPH, hypothyroidism (total thyroidectomy performed due to medullary thyroid carcinoma). Surgery of the LS spine in 2016. In 2017, had surgery of the right ICA. In 2021, a double aortocoronary bypass was performed (LIMA-LAD, VSM-RCA). Treated for asthma/COPD? for the last 4 years. According to ENT, has chronic laryngitis. Last pulmonology follow-up was in 11/2024; use of Alvesco instead of Foster was advised.
Current complaints: comes for a pulmonologist follow-up, last exam on Nov 13, 2024. Denies difficulties with breathing. Denies feeling short of breath. States that he occasionally has changes in voice tone, occasionally "loses" his voice. No cough. Denies chest pain. Previously had "wheezing in the chest," no longer "wheezes" for the past year. Occasionally clear nasal secretion.
Epidemiological history: recovered from COVID-19 in Feb 2021—mild clinical picture, and in 2022—since then has had issues with hoarseness.
Functions and habits: good appetite, stable body weight for the last 6 months, urinates 2-3x at night. Stools are normal, without pathological admixtures. Has not smoked for 35 years, smoked for 20 years, 20 cigarettes/day. Drinks 2-3 dl of wine daily.
Vaccinations: not vaccinated against COVID-19 and flu.
Current therapy: Alvesco 1x1 puff, Euthyrox 100 mcg/day, Aspirin 100 mg 1x1 tab, Dalneva 4/5 mg 2x1 tab, Controloc 1x1 tab, Duster Duo 1x1 caps, Rosuvastatin 1x1 tab in the evening, Siofor 850 mg 2x1 tab, Zaldiar 1 tab/day, Melart, three types of eye drops.
Allergies: denies drug allergies.

**Status:**
Average musculoskeletal build and nutritional status, mobile, conscious, eupneic, anicteric, afebrile. Postoperative scar on the neck. Impression of normal perfusion of the skin and visible mucous membranes. Neck veins not distended, no palpable lymph nodes in the neck. Status post median thoracotomy. Lungs: diminished breath sounds posterobasally on the right compared to the left side. Heart: rhythmic action, clear tones, no murmurs heard. Abdomen: soft, non-tender on palpation, audible peristalsis. LS region non-tender bilaterally. Limbs: status post left saphenectomy, pitting edema of the lower legs. Mild clubbing of the fingers.

**Findings:**
Date of finding: Apr 14, 2025, 11:38

Nov 13: FENO: 55 ppb—elevated value.
Nov 13, 2024: Spirometry with BDT: FVC 95.5-93.5, FEV1 96.1-104.1, TI 77.52-82.46, MEF50: 95.6-127.9—values within reference ranges, BDT negative, but with improvement at the level of small airways after bronchodilation.
Dec 9, 2025: Spirometry with BDT: FVC 88-91% (3.44L), FEV1 89-96% (2.66L), TI 0.77-0.81, PEF 110-99%, FEF50 85-122% DLCO 86%, KCO 85%, VA 100%
Mar 6, 2025: Chest X-ray (PA and lateral profile): the pulmonary parenchyma available for analysis shows no convincing signs of fresh infiltrative changes. An oval shadow is visible basally on the left, diff. dg. nipple shadow. In case of clinical necessity, perform an X-ray with marked nipples. Hila are vascular, with normal terminal branching. The left diaphragmatic dome and corresponding costophrenic sinus are clear. The right diaphragmatic dome is positioned higher, lateral costophrenic sinus is shallower. Heart shadow size within physiological proportions. Status post sternotomy.

**Conclusion:**
I advise continuing the use of Alvesco now at a double dose and other chronic therapy as before. Plan a follow-up in 3 months with new findings: spirometry with Ventolin test, DLCO, FENO, chest X-ray (PA, lateral with marking of the nipples), lab findings (CBC, WBC differential, CRP, urea, creatinine, AST, ALT, GGT, Na, K, Cl, Ca, LDH, NT-proBNP, urine). ENT specialist examination and cardiologist examination are advised. Bring the inhaler to the follow-up (question of inhalation technique). Earlier follow-up according to clinical indication.
gold_reference: Alvesco double dose

C66 Malignant neoplasm of ureter
C67 Malignant neoplasm of urinary bladder
M05 Seropositive rheumatoid arthritis

History:
Reason for visit: urological surgical procedure is planned
Personal history and previous illnesses: Between 65 and 70 years old. Spinal spondylosis. Operated on a left kidney stone 38 years ago. Surgery for left kidney stones 18 years ago. May 3, 2024, TUR of the urinary bladder, PHD: CARCINOMA PAPILLARE UROTHELIALE NON INVASIVUM (low grade - WHO).
In May 2025, a left-sided nephroureterectomy was performed due to a neoplasm of the left ureter and renal pelvis.
On December 8, 2025, examined by a rheumatologist due to pain and swelling of the hands, more on the right; managed under the diagnosis of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis.
Current complaints: Referred to a pulmonologist due to MSCT thorax findings; a urological surgical procedure, urinary bladder polypectomy, is planned.
One month ago had a cough and a cold, was afebrile. Feels well now, does not cough, denies chest pain, denies shortness of breath.
Current therapy: Decortin (since Dec 8) 10 mg for 5 days, 7.5 mg for 7 days and continue 5 mg until follow-up, Methotrexate 10 mg once weekly (4 tablets of 2.5 mg), Folbella 5 mg 1x1 tablet every day except on the day of methotrexate intake for two weeks, and after that 1x1 tablet the day after methotrexate intake, D vital 25,000 I.U. once monthly.
Functions and habits: Smoked up to 10 cigarettes a day for the past 30 years, quit 1.5 months ago, does not consume alcohol.
Allergies: Denies drug allergies.

Status:
Auscultation: bilateral normal breath sounds, SpO2 93%
Findings:
Date of finding: Nov 17, 2025, 19:52
Finding and opinion
May 3, 2024, TUR of the urinary bladder, PHD: CARCINOMA PAPILLARE UROTHELIALE NON INVASIVUM (low grade - WHO)
MSCT of the thorax, i.v. contrast:
Perifissural nodule of the small interlobium size 5 mm. Left upper and lower lobe and right basal linear fibrous changes reaching the pleura. No significantly enlarged lymph nodes or tumor masses seen in the mediastinum and hila. Tracheobronchial tree is in an appropriate position and lumen width. Left pleural effusion 1.8 cm wide. Visualized bones are without pathological remodeling typical for secondary deposits.
MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis, with i.v. contrast and urography: May 21, 2025, Nephroureterectomia lat. sin. Pronounced left-sided scoliosis of the lumbar spine. Scarring changes visible to the right in the left renal fossa. Right perirenal space is free. Right kidney is of appropriate position and size, maintained corticomedullary borders. In the delayed phase, contrast urine is visible in the collecting system of the right kidney. Right ureter is of normal lumen width, followed to the junction with the urinary bladder with appropriate density of surrounding fatty tissue. Multiple polypoid thickenings of the dorsal wall of the urinary bladder size 4 to 5 mm are visible. No significantly enlarged lymph nodes or free fluid seen in the scanned area.
Apart from a cyst of the liver parenchyma, other parenchymal organs of the upper abdomen are of normal morphology. Visualized bones are without pathological remodeling typical for secondary deposits.

Lung Ultrasound: left small anechoic zone up to one intercostal space in height corresponding to pleural effusion.

Conclusion:
Perform follow-up Chest X-ray in one month and follow-up MSCT of the thorax in 12 months.
Follow-up in the pulmonology clinic with the MSCT finding, earlier if necessary.

Current complaints: States that symptoms are stationary - tolerates major physical exertion poorly, dyspneic when walking uphill. Coughs every morning, expectorates thick yellowish secretion. No wheezing in the chest. Denies stenocardia and other complaints.

Current therapy: Relvar 184/22 mcg 1x1 inhalation, Ventolin PRN, Nixar 20 mg 1x1 tab, Melarth 10 mg 1x1 tab, D-Vital, D3 Dekristol, Zaldiar tab. PRN

Functions and habits: Smokes up to 20 cig/day for about 30 years. Occasional alcohol consumption.
Appetite normal, no significant fluctuations in body weight. Stool and urination normal.
Allergies: Denies drug allergies.

No peripheral lymphadenopathy. Jugular veins not distended, no vascular bruits over the carotids. Tongue moist, uncoated.
Heart: rhythmic action, heart sounds muffled, no murmurs heard. Pulse 85/min.

12/09 SPIROMETRY with Ventolin test: FVC 58% (56%), FEV1 46% (45%) Tiffeneau 80% (80%) PEF 48% (50%) PEF25 42% (35%) PEF 50 20% (20%) - obstructive-restrictive impairment of a severe degree
DLCO 71%
KCO 99%

Conclusion:
Discontinue Relvar from therapy and introduce Timbow 2x2 inhalations per day, along with Ventolin PRN and other current therapy.
Take Medrol 32 mg for the next 5 days.
Perform microbiological analysis of sputum.
Follow-up in the pulmonology outpatient clinic in 3 months with spirometry with Ventolin test, DLCO, FeNO, and a new chest X-ray (PA + L profile), laboratory blood findings (CBC, DC, CRP, total IgE).

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1954

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
I21.4 Acute subendocardial myocardial infarction
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 162/98 mmHg; Pulse: 94/min; SpO2: 96%; Temp: 36.7°C; VAS: 7 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Chest pain and pressure
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, treated with amlodipine. Hypercholesterolemia, poorly controlled. Former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 2 years ago). No previous cardiac interventions or known coronary artery disease. Family history positive for early myocardial infarction (father at age 52).
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of retrosternal pressure and "burning" sensation that began approximately 4 hours ago while gardening. The pain radiates to the left shoulder and lower jaw. Associated with mild nausea and diaphoresis. He initially took an antacid at home thinking it was indigestion, with no relief.
Functions: Stool and urination normal. No recent weight changes.
Habits: Does not consume alcohol.
Medications: Amlodipine 5 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg.
Allergies: Denied.
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build, slightly overweight. Conscious, communicative, oriented. Patient appears anxious and is mildly diaphoretic. Eupneic at rest. Skin and visible mucous membranes are of normal color.
Neck: No jugular venous distention, no carotid bruits.
Lungs: Auscultation reveals normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Regular rhythm, tones somewhat muffled, no murmurs. BP 158/95 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Audible peristalsis.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. Pulses palpable and symmetric.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus rhythm, rate 92/min, intermediate electrical axis. Horizontal ST-segment depression of 1.5 mm in leads V4, V5, and V6. Flattened T-waves in I and aVL. No pathological Q-waves.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.92 [1e12]/L, HGB 148 g/L, Hct 0.442 L/L, MCV 89.8 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, NEU% 74.2 %, CRP 5.2 mg/L.
Urea 6.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
High-sensitivity Troponin I (hs-TnI): 942.0 ng/L (Reference range: < 26.2 ng/L)
CK-MB: 42 U/L (Reference range: < 24 U/L)
Glucose: 7.2 mmol/L.
(Dr. Hrvoje Vidović, 18.07.2024 10:15)
Summary Image of Thoracic Organs (AP):
Lungs are clear of infiltrates or congestion. The hila are of normal size and configuration. The cardiothoracic ratio is at the upper limit of normal. Costophrenic angles are sharp.
(Dr. Marina Kos, 18.07.2024 10:45)
Therapy
0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (1 dose)
Diazepam 5 mg IM
Conclusion
Based on clinical presentation, ECG changes (ST depression in lateral leads), and significant elevation of cardiac enzymes (Troponin I 942 ng/L), the patient is presenting with an acute NSTEMI. He remains hemodynamically stable.
Admission to the Department of Internal Medicine, Cardiology Unit, for further monitoring and coronary angiography.

(Dr. Jakov Reljić, 18.07.2024 11:30)
gold_reference: The patient has a confirmed Acute Subendocardial Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI) based on high-sensitivity Troponin I levels (942 ng/L) and ischemic ECG changes (ST-segment depressions). However, the physician failed to administer or order any antiplatelet therapy (such as Aspirin or a P2Y12 inhibitor) or anticoagulation (such as Heparin or Enoxaparin), which are the fundamental standard of care for acute coronary syndromes.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1984

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
N20.1 Calculus in ureter
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 158/94 mmHg; Pulse: 102/min; SpO2: 99%; Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 9 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
18.07.2024, 14:20: BP 155/92, Pulse 98, SpO2 99%, Resp 18, Temp 36.7, VAS 9, GCS 15
Reason for Arrival
Severe right-sided flank pain radiating to the groin.

Anamnesis
History: Previous episode of nephrolithiasis in 2019, passed spontaneously. No known chronic kidney disease. History of mild seasonal allergies.
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of agonizing, colicky pain in the right flank starting approximately 3 hours ago. The pain radiates downward toward the right inguinal region and scrotum. Associated with two episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. Denies fever, chills, or dysuria. Reports urgency to urinate but only passing small amounts of clear urine.
Functions and Habits: Regular bowel movements. No smoking history. Occasional social alcohol consumption. Appetite currently decreased due to nausea.
Medications: None regularly.
Allergies: Penicillin (hives).

Status
General: Patient appears in significant distress, unable to find a comfortable position ("pacing" the exam room). Conscious, oriented x3. Skin is diaphoretic but warm and well-perfused. No peripheral edema.
Head/Neck: Normocephalic. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No jugular venous distension.
Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Tachycardic but regular rhythm. Clear S1, S2, no murmurs or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. No guarding or rebound tenderness. Deep palpation reveals mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant but no peritoneal signs. Audible peristalsis. Positive right-sided costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness (Goldflam sign). Left side non-tender.
Extremities: Symmetrical, full range of motion, palpable distal pulses.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus tachycardia, 104/min, normal axis, no ST-T wave abnormalities.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L (slightly elevated), RBC 4.8 [1e12]/L, HGB 145 g/L, Hct 0.42 L/L, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L.
Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, CRP 2.4 mg/L.
Urinalysis: Appearance: Yellow, slightly turbid. pH 6.0, Specific Gravity 1.025. Protein: Negative. Glucose: Negative. Nitrites: Negative. Leukocyte Esterase: Negative. Erythrocytes: 3+ (Microscopic hematuria). Microscopy: 20-30 RBC/hpf, 1-2 WBC/hpf, no bacteria.
(Dr. Elena Vaskova, Clinical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 15:15)

Imaging:
Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) - Urinary Tract:
Right Kidney: Mild hydronephrosis and hydroureter observed. A hyperechoic structure measuring approximately 4 mm is visualized in the distal third of the right ureter near the vesicoureteric junction (VUJ) with posterior acoustic shadowing. No free fluid in the Morison pouch.
Left Kidney: Normal size and echogenicity, no hydronephrosis.
Bladder: Partially filled, no masses.
(Performed by Dr. Markovic Jovan, 18.07.2024 15:30)

Therapy Administered in ED
- Saline 0.9% 500 mL IV infusion
- Ketorolac 30 mg IV (Toradol)
- Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV

Conclusion
Clinical presentation, microscopic hematuria, and ultrasound findings are consistent with acute right-sided renal colic due to a distal ureteral calculus (4 mm). No evidence of urinary tract infection (afebrile, negative nitrites/leukocytes) or renal impairment (normal creatinine).

The patient's pain significantly improved (VAS 2/10) following intravenous analgesia. Given the stone size (<5 mm) and lack of complications, the patient is suitable for outpatient management (Medical Expulsive Therapy).

Recommendations:
1. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg once daily for 10 days to facilitate stone passage.
2. Ibuprofen 400-600 mg as needed for pain (not to exceed 1800 mg/day).
3. Increase fluid intake to 2.5-3 liters per day.
4. Strain urine to catch the stone for analysis if possible.
5. Follow-up with a urologist in 7 days with a repeat KUB X-ray or ultrasound.
6. Return to the ED immediately if: fever develops, pain becomes uncontrollable with oral medication, or inability to urinate occurs.

Discharged in stable condition.
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

Patient 4
MBOO: 284719302

Diagnoses
A09.9 Gastroenteritis and colitis of unspecified origin
R11 Nausea and vomiting
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 112/68 mmHg; Pulse: 94/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 36.9°C; VAS: 3; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Date	BP /mmHg	Pulse /min	SpO2 %	Resp /min	Temp °C	Guk mmol/L	VAS	GCS
18.07.2024 09:15	112/68	94	97%	18	36.9	5.8	3	15

Reason for Arrival
Nausea, vomiting, and general weakness.

Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), and chronic low back pain.
Current Illness: The patient reports a two-day history of nausea and frequent vomiting (4-5 times a day). He describes a dull, generalized abdominal discomfort but denies sharp pain or cramps. No diarrhea reported; last bowel movement was yesterday, normal consistency. He feels "very weak" and "unsteady on his feet." No fever or respiratory symptoms.
Functions: Poor appetite over the last 48 hours, decreased urine output. 
Habits: Non-smoker, social alcohol consumption (denies recent intake).
Medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg 1+0+0, Amlodipine 5 mg 0+0+1, Omnic Ocas 0.4 mg, Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for back pain.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Status
Patient is of medium build, conscious, communicative, oriented to person and place, though appears slightly lethargic. Dehydrated appearance: dry oral mucosa, reduced skin turgor. Afebrile, eupneic at rest.
Head: Normocephalic, pupils equal and reactive to light.
Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, thyroid not enlarged.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Rhythmic, tones somewhat distant, no murmurs. BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 90/min.
Abdomen: Slightly distended, soft, mild diffuse tenderness on deep palpation, no rebound or guarding. No palpable masses. Peristalsis present but sluggish.
Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable. Neurologically: no gross motor deficits, but patient notes feeling "lightheaded" upon sitting up.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus rhythm, frequency 92/min, intermediate electrical axis, no acute ST-T changes.
Lab Results:
WBC 9.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.60 [1e12]/L, HGB 148 g/L, Hct 0.442 L/L, MCV 91.0 fL, MCH 31.2 pg, Platelets 215 [1e9]/L, CRP 4.2 mg/L.
Glucose 5.9 mmol/L, Urea 9.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 115 µmol/L, Na 121 mmol/L, K 3.6 mmol/L, Cl 89 mmol/L.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, Medical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 10:45)

Radiographic Imaging of the Abdomen (Supine):
No signs of pneumoperitoneum. No dilated loops of small or large bowel suggestive of ileus. Moderate amount of fecal material in the colon. 
Conclusion: Non-specific finding.
(Dr. Sc. Goran Šimić, MD, 18.07.2024 11:20)

Therapy administered in ED:
- 500 ml 0.9% NaCl IV
- Reglan (metoclopramide) 10 mg IV

Conclusion
(Dr. Ana Knežević, 18.07.2024 13:30)
The patient's clinical presentation is consistent with viral gastroenteritis and mild dehydration. After receiving intravenous rehydration and antiemetics, the patient reports feeling subjectively better and the nausea has subsided. Abdominal findings remain benign.
The patient is discharged home. 
Recommendations: 
- Small, frequent sips of rehydration salts (Rehidromix).
- Light diet (tea, rusks, boiled rice).
- Discontinue Ibuprofen temporarily.
- Follow-up with the primary care physician in 2 days for control of renal function and electrolytes.
- In case of worsening symptoms or inability to keep fluids down, return to the ED.
gold_reference: The patient has a sodium level of 121 mmol/L, which indicates severe hyponatremia. This is a critical lab value that can lead to seizures, cerebral edema, or coma. Despite this result, the patient was discharged with a diagnosis of simple gastroenteritis, and the severe electrolyte imbalance was neither addressed in the treatment (beyond standard saline) nor cited as a reason for admission.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1970

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
I26.9 Pulmonary embolism without mention of acute cor pulmonale
I80.2 Phlebitis and thrombophlebitis of deep vessels of lower extremities
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; BP: 118/76 mmHg; Pulse: 114/min; SpO2: 91% (Room Air); Temp: 37.2°C; RR: 26/min; VAS: 6-Moderate/Severe pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Anamnesis
Current Illness: Patient presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath and sharp, right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began approximately three hours ago while resting. Reports a "heavy" feeling in the left leg for the past 3 days. Denies cough, hemoptysis, or syncope.
Past Illnesses: Total left hip arthroplasty 14 days ago. Essential hypertension.
Family History: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 62.
Functions: Normal urination, regular bowel movements.
Habits: Smoker (10 cigarettes/day for 20 years). Does not consume alcohol.
Therapy: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg 1x1 (discontinued 3 days ago per patient's own decision).
Allergies: Denies.
Status
Musculoskeletal build: Average, alert, communicative, oriented in time and space, tachypneic, mildly distressed, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes are pale, no cyanosis.
Lungs: Auscultation reveals clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. SpO2 91% on room air, improves to 97% on 2L O2 via nasal cannula.
Heart: Tachycardic rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. S1Q3T3 pattern not clearly visible. BP: 118/76 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly, audible peristalsis.
Limbs: Left lower leg is visibly edematous compared to the right (circumference +3.5 cm at the calf). Tenderness on deep palpation of the left calf. Homans' sign positive on the left. Peripheral pulses palpable.
EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 115/min, normal axis, inverted T waves in V1-V3.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.80 [1e12]/L, HGB 145 g/L, Hct 0.420 L/L, MCV 87.5 fL, Trc 210 [1e9]/L, D-dimer 3450 ng/mL (Ref: <500), Troponin T (hs-TnT) 48 ng/L (Ref: <14), NT-proBNP 950 pg/mL (Ref: <300), CRP 12.4 mg/L, Glucose 6.2 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, LDH 280 U/L.
(LUKIC ANA mag.med.biochem., 22.08.2024 14:15)

Arterial Blood Gas (on 2L O2):
pH 7.46, pCO2 4.2 kPa, pO2 10.8 kPa, HCO3 22.4 mmol/L, BE -1.2.

Radiographic Imaging:
Chest X-Ray (AP): No infiltrates, no pleural effusion. Heart shadow within normal limits. Normal pulmonary vascularity.

CT Angiography of the Pulmonary Arteries (CTPA):
Filling defects observed in the right main pulmonary artery and the segmental branches of the right lower lobe, consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. No evidence of right ventricular enlargement (RV/LV ratio < 1.0).

Color Doppler Ultrasound of Lower Extremity Veins:
Non-compressible segment of the left popliteal vein and posterior tibial veins with absence of flow, consistent with acute deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
Dr. M. Horvat

Conclusion:
(Dr. MARIĆ IVAN, MD, 22.08.2024 16:30)
Patient presented with classic symptoms of pulmonary embolism (Wells Score 9.0: HR > 100, recent surgery, clinical signs of DVT, and PE being the most likely diagnosis). Diagnosis confirmed via CTPA and elevated D-dimer. Elevated Troponin T and NT-proBNP suggest mild myocardial strain, though the patient remains hemodynamically stable (PESI Class III - moderate risk).

Therapy initiated in ED:
- Oxygen 2L/min via nasal cannula
- Enoxaparin 80 mg (1 mg/kg) SC stat
- Normal Saline 500 ml IV bolus

Plan:
Admission to the Department of Pulmonology for further monitoring and initiation of oral anticoagulant therapy (Apixaban). Monitor for signs of hemodynamic instability.
Follow-up with vascular surgery for post-thrombotic syndrome prevention.
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

report_content:
Date of birth: 1961

Triage category: 3
Diagnoses
R42 Dizziness and giddiness
Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 138/85 mmHg; Pulse: 62/min; SpO2: 99%; Temp: 36.5°C; VAS: 2 – Mild pain; GCS: 15
Vital signs
Date	BP /mmHg	Pulse /min	SpO2 %	Resp /min	Temp °C	Trect °C	Guk mmol/L	VAS	GCS	AVPU
18.07.2024 09:15	138/85	62	99%	14				2	15	A	
Reason for visit
Sudden onset of dizziness and generalized weakness.
History
CURRENT ISSUES: The patient reports the sudden onset of dizziness starting this morning upon waking. Describes a sensation of "spinning" that lasts for several seconds when turning his head to the right. Also reports feeling "heavy" in his legs and general malaise. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. No history of recent falls or head trauma. No tinnitus or hearing loss.
Medical History: Arterial hypertension for 10 years, Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH). 
Bowel movements and urination are normal.
Non-smoker, denies alcohol use.
Medications: Lisinopril 20 mg 1x1, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg 1x1.
Drug allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).
Status
Medium musculoskeletal build (92 kg), conscious, oriented x3. 
Mucous membranes moist, skin normally colored.
Neck: No carotid bruits, thyroid not enlarged.
Heart: Rhythmic heart action, clear tones, no murmurs. HR 62/min.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezes.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
Neurological: No cranial nerve deficits. Sensation intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Finger-to-nose test normal. Romberg test slightly unstable. No nystagmus at rest.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
12-lead ECG: Sinus rhythm, rate 60/min, normal axis, no ST-T segment changes. PR interval 160ms.
Metabolites and Substrates:
Blood glucose 6.1 mmol/L; Urea 9.2 mmol/L; Creatinine 138 µmol/L; eGFR 51.4 mL/min/1.73m²; Total bilirubin 14 µmol/L
Electrolytes:
Na 140 mmol/L; **K 6.4 mmol/L**; Cl 102 mmol/L; Ca 2.25 mmol/L
Complete Blood Count:
WBC 8.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.51 [1e12]/L, HGB 142 g/L, Hct 0.421 L/L, Platelets 215 [1e9]/L
Proteins:
CRP 2.1 mg/L

Radiographic Imaging:
CT Head (Non-contrast):
Findings: No signs of acute intracranial hemorrhage, territorial infarction, or expansive process. Ventricular system is of normal size and shape. No midline shift.
(Dr. M. Horvat, 18.07.2024, 11:45)

Conclusion
The clinical presentation and diagnostic workup (negative CT head, normal neurological exam) are suggestive of peripheral vestibular dysfunction, likely Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV). The patient reports feeling slightly better after a period of observation. No acute cerebrovascular event was identified. The patient is stable for discharge. 
Recommendation: Follow up with a primary care physician for further vestibular rehabilitation or ENT consultation. 
Therapy: Betahistine 24 mg twice daily.
(Dr. I. Perić, MD, 18.07.2024, 12:30)
gold_reference: The patient has a potassium level of 6.4 mmol/L, which indicates severe hyperkalemia. This is a critical laboratory value that can lead to life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias or cardiac arrest. The error is compounded by the fact that the patient is taking Lisinopril (an ACE inhibitor), which is known to cause potassium retention, and has a reduced eGFR (51.4), indicating impaired renal clearance. Despite this critical finding, the discharge conclusion only addresses the vertigo symptoms and fails to mention, treat, or follow up on the hyperkalemia.

Fall following a dizzy spell.

Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, Chronic Kidney Disease (Stage 4, baseline Creatinine ~190-210 µmol/L), and Heart Failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF, last Echo EF 50%). History of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH).
Current Illness: Patient reports that this morning, after standing up quickly from the breakfast table, he felt a "sudden darkness" and lightheadedness. He fell to the floor, landing on his right side. Denies loss of consciousness, tongue biting, or urinary incontinence. He currently complains of significant pain in the right hip and lower back area. No chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath prior to the event.
Functions: Appetite slightly decreased lately. Stool regular. Urination frequent at night (nocturia 3x).
Habits: Former smoker (quit 10 years ago). Occasional glass of wine.
Medications: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Furosemide 40 mg 1x1, Bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1x1, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg 1x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).

Status
Elderly male, appears stated age, conscious, communicative, oriented x3. Eupneic at rest.
Head/Neck: No signs of trauma to the skull. Carotids symmetrical without bruits. Jugular veins not distended.
Lungs: Basal crackles bilaterally, otherwise clear breath sounds.
Heart: Distant heart sounds, regular rhythm, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: Right hip is tender to palpation over the greater trochanter. No visible shortening or external rotation of the limb. Range of motion limited by pain. Mild 1+ pitting edema of the lower legs. Peripheral pulses palpable.
Neurological: No focal neurological deficits. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Romberg test not performed due to hip pain.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
EKG: Sinus rhythm, 72/min, left axis deviation, no acute ST-T wave changes.
X-ray Right Hip/Pelvis: No evidence of acute fracture or dislocation. Severe osteoarthritic changes noted in both hip joints.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.8 [1e9]/L, RBC 3.42 [1e12]/L, HGB 108 g/L, Hct 0.321 L/L, MCV 93.8 fL, MCH 31.5 pg, MCHC 336 g/L, Platelets 184 [1e9]/L.
Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 16.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 212 µmol/L, eGFR 26 mL/min/1.73m², Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.9 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L, CRP 4.2 mg/L, Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL (negative), NT-proBNP 1250 pg/mL.

Conclusion
The syncopal episode is most likely orthostatic in nature, possibly exacerbated by current diuretic therapy and underlying autonomic dysfunction. Cardiac origin (arrhythmia) is less likely given the EKG and lack of prodromal palpitations, but cannot be entirely ruled out. Right hip pain is secondary to contusion/soft tissue injury; no fracture was visualized on X-ray.
The patient is hemodynamically stable. Adjusted medication regimen recommended.

Recommendations:
1. Increase oral fluid intake and rise slowly from a supine/sitting position.
2. Reduce Furosemide to 20 mg daily; follow up with primary care physician for blood pressure and renal function monitoring in 3 days.
3. For management of acute hip pain, take Ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours for the next 5 days.
4. Follow up with a cardiologist for an elective Holter EKG.
5. If new fainting spells, chest pain, or inability to bear weight occurs, return to the ED immediately.

Report signed by: Dr. Julian Vance, MD
Date: 22.10.2024
gold_reference: The patient has Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) with an eGFR of 26 mL/min/1.73m² and is also taking an ACE inhibitor (Ramipril) and a diuretic (Furosemide). NSAIDs like Ibuprofen are strictly contraindicated in advanced CKD as they can cause further decline in renal function, promote fluid retention (exacerbating his heart failure), and increase the risk of acute-on-chronic kidney injury through the inhibition of prostaglandins that maintain renal perfusion.

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
I48.0 Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 158/92 mmHg; Pulse: 114/min; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.7°C; VAS: 2 - Mild discomfort; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Palpitations and mild shortness of breath.
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing essential hypertension treated for 15 years. No history of coronary artery disease, diabetes, or prior stroke. This is the first episode of palpitations.
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of "racing heart" and "fluttering" in the chest starting approximately 4 hours ago while resting. Accompanied by mild shortness of breath upon walking. Denies chest pain, syncope, or focal neurological deficits.
Functions and Habits: Normal bowel movements and urination. Non-smoker, does not consume alcohol. Appetite is normal.
Medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Amlodipine 5 mg daily.
Allergies: Penicillin (skin rash).
Status
Patient is an elderly female of medium build, fully conscious, communicative, and oriented to person, place, and time. Eupneic at rest, afebrile, non-icteric, skin and mucous membranes are well-perfused.
Chest: Symmetrical respiratory movements.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Irregular rhythm, tachycardic, heart sounds are distinct, no murmurs or gallops. Peripheral pulses are irregular and palpable.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses or organomegaly. Peristalsis is present.
Extremities: No pretibial edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 112/min. Normal electrical axis. No ST-segment elevation or depression. No pathological Q waves.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.25 [1e12]/L, HGB 132 g/L, Hct 0.395 L/L, MCV 93.0 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, NEU 62%, LYM 28%.
Sodium 139 mmol/L, Potassium 4.1 mmol/L, Chloride 102 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m², Glucose 5.4 mmol/L.
Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL (Negative).
TSH 1.45 mIU/L (Normal).
(Dr. Elena Vukas, Clinical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 14:15)
Conclusion
The patient presents with a first-time episode of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Symptoms were managed in the ED with a single dose of Metoprolol 25 mg orally, resulting in a reduction of heart rate to 82/min (still irregular).
Regarding stroke prophylaxis, the CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 1 (1 point for Hypertension). Based on this score, the risk of thromboembolism is low, and oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time.
The patient is discharged with a prescription for Aspirin 100 mg daily and Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily.
Urgent follow-up with a cardiologist is recommended within 7 days for consideration of cardioversion or further rhythm management.
Report to the emergency department immediately if experiencing chest pain, severe shortness of breath, or signs of stroke (facial drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty).
(Dr. Marko Horvat, MD, 18.07.2024 16:30)
gold_reference: The physician miscalculated the CHA2DS2-VASc score. The patient is a 76-year-old female (Age >= 75 = 2 points; Female sex = 1 point) with Hypertension (1 point). The correct CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4, not 1. A score of 4 carries a high risk of stroke and necessitates oral anticoagulation (like Apixaban or Warfarin). Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation.

In the right iliac fossa, a non-compressible, blind-ended tubular structure (appendix) is visualized, measuring 9 mm in diameter. "Target sign" visible in cross-section. The pain radiates to the right scapular region. She has vomited twice since the pain began. She denies chills or rigors but feels "feverish." She has had milder episodes of similar pain in the past year, usually after heavy meals, which resolved spontaneously after an hour.
Past Illnesses: Appendectomy (2005), gestational diabetes (2012).
Family History: Mother had a cholecystectomy at age 50.
Functions: Appetite currently zero due to nausea. Urination normal. Last bowel movement yesterday, normal consistency.
Habits: Non-smoker, occasional alcohol (1-2 glasses of wine on weekends).
Therapy: Multivitamins.
Allergies: Penicillin (hives).
Status:

    • Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
    • Heart: Rhythmic, S1 and S2 clear, no murmurs. HR 94/min.
    • Abdomen: Above the level of the chest (obese), soft. Significant tenderness on deep palpation in the right upper quadrant. Positive Murphy's sign (arrest of inspiration on deep palpation of the RUQ). No rebound tenderness or guarding in other quadrants. No palpable masses or organomegaly. Audible bowel sounds.
    • Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses symmetric and palpable.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 96/min, normal axis, no acute ST-T changes.

      WBC 14.8 [10⁹/L] (H), NEU% 82.1% (H), LYM% 12.0% (L), RBC 4.65 [10¹²/L], Hb 138 g/L, Hct 0.410 L/L, Platelets 285 [10⁹/L], CRP 64.2 mg/L (H).
      Glucose 6.4 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 74 µmol/L, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m².
      AST 42 U/L, ALT 55 U/L (slightly elevated), GGT 48 U/L, ALP 110 U/L, Bilirubin total 18.2 µmol/L, Amylase 65 U/L.
      Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.

      The liver is of normal size and structure, without focal lesions. Intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is distended, with a thickened wall (5.5 mm) and a layered appearance (edema). Several hyperechoic shadows with acoustic shadowing are visualized within the lumen, the largest measuring 18 mm, one of which appears impacted in the gallbladder neck. Pericholecystic fluid is present. Positive sonographic Murphy's sign. Pancreas and spleen are of normal morphological appearance.

The patient presents with acute calculous cholecystitis. Clinical examination and diagnostic imaging confirm the diagnosis. The patient is hemodynamically stable but requires hospitalization for further treatment.
The patient is admitted to the Department of General Surgery.

3. Antibiotic therapy: Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV every 12 hours + Metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours (due to reported Penicillin allergy).

5. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy planned for tomorrow morning's surgical list.

The patient has a history of essential hypertension diagnosed 10 years ago and hyperlipidemia. He reports being generally active and denies any previous history of myocardial infarction, stroke, or cardiac arrhythmias. No prior surgical history. He has not had a stress test or echocardiogram in the last five years.
Current Illness:
The patient reports that while working in his garden today around 13:00, he experienced a sudden onset of "racing heart" and "fluttering" in his chest. This was accompanied by a feeling of lightheadedness and mild shortness of breath. He sat down and rested, and the symptoms gradually resolved over the course of 45 minutes. Upon arrival at the Emergency Department, he is currently asymptomatic and denies chest pain, pressure, or further palpitations.
Function:
    • Appetite: Normal
    • Weight Loss: Denies
    • Stool: Regular, no melena or hematochezia
    • Urination: Normal

    • Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1

Status:
    • Musculoskeletal build: Eutrophic, conscious, cooperative, oriented in time and space. Skin and visible mucous membranes well-perfused, non-icteric, afebrile. No peripheral lymphadenopathy.
    • Chest: Normal shape, symmetrical respiratory excursions.
    • Lungs: Auscultation reveals normal vesicular breathing bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
    • Heart: Regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs or gallops. Peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.
    • Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses or organomegaly, active bowel sounds.
    • Limbs: No edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG (14:20): Sinus tachycardia, rate 110/min, normal axis, no ST-segment elevation or depression, no T-wave inversions.
    • EKG (15:30): Sinus rhythm, rate 74/min, no acute ischemic changes compared to previous.
    • Blood tests: WBC 8.4 x10^9/L, RBC 4.88 x10^12/L, HGB 145 g/L, HCT 0.421 L/L, Platelets 210 x10^9/L, Glucose 5.9 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 85 µmol/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, CRP 2.1 mg/L, **hs-TNT 142.8 ng/L**, CK 115 U/L, AST 24 U/L, ALT 28 U/L.
    • Chest X-ray: No signs of acute pleuropulmonary infiltrates or congestion. Heart shadow of normal size and configuration.
Conclusion:
(Dr. Marko Jurić, MD, 18.08.2024 16:45)
The patient was evaluated for a transient episode of palpitations and lightheadedness. At the time of clinical examination and during the observation period, the patient remained hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic. The control EKG shows a stable sinus rhythm without signs of acute ischemia or significant arrhythmia. Laboratory findings show normal inflammatory markers and stable renal function. Based on the resolution of symptoms and the normal EKG findings, there is no indication for emergency hospitalization at this time.
The patient is discharged home. Advised to follow up with a cardiologist for an outpatient 24-hour Holter EKG and an echocardiogram to further investigate the etiology of the palpitations. Continue current antihypertensive and lipid-lowering therapy.
In case of recurrent chest pain, prolonged palpitations, or fainting, report immediately to the emergency service.
gold_reference: The high-sensitivity Troponin T (hs-TNT) is critically elevated at 142.8 ng/L (typical upper limit of normal is <14 ng/L). This finding is highly suggestive of acute myocardial injury or NSTEMI, even in the absence of EKG changes. The physician's conclusion completely ignores this result, stating that laboratory findings show no indication for hospitalization and discharging the patient.
