Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Rhythmic action, clear tones, no murmurs. BP: 140/85 mmHg. Pulse: 94/min.
Abdomen: Above the level of the chest, soft, significantly tender on deep palpation in the right hypochondrium. Positive Murphy's sign. No rebound tenderness (Blumberg sign negative). No palpable masses or organomegaly. Bowel sounds are present but diminished.
Limbs: Symmetrical, no edema, peripheral pulses palpable and equal.

WBC 13.8 [10⁹/L], RBC 4.62 [10¹²/L], Hb 138 g/L, Hct 0.410 L/L, MCV 88.7 fL, Platelets 245 [10⁹/L], NEU% 78.2%, LYM% 14.5%, MONO% 6.8%, EOS% 0.3%, BASO% 0.2%, NEU # 10.79 [10⁹/L], Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 4.9 mmol/L, Creatinine 72 µmol/L, eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73m², AST 24 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, GGT 35 U/L, ALP 92 U/L, Total Bilirubin 15.2 µmol/L, Amylase - serum 48 U/L, Lipase 32 U/L, CRP 32.4 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
(Ivan Horvat, M.Sc. Biochemist, 18.07.2024 20:45)

Abdominal Ultrasound:
The liver is of normal size and structure, without focal lesions. The gallbladder is distended, with a thickened and edematous wall (thickness 4.8 mm), showing a "double-contour" sign. Several calculus shadows are visualized within the lumen, the largest measuring 18 mm, one of which appears impacted in the gallbladder neck. Positive sonographic Murphy's sign. The common bile duct (CBD) is not dilated (4 mm). No pericholecystic fluid collection seen. The pancreas and spleen appear normal. No free fluid in the abdominal cavity.

The patient presents with clinical, laboratory (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and ultrasonographic signs of acute calculous cholecystitis. Liver enzymes and lipase are within reference ranges, making choledocholithiasis and biliary pancreatitis less likely. After initial analgesic and antiemetic therapy, the patient reports moderate improvement in pain (VAS 4). 
A surgical consultation was performed (Dr. Petrović). Based on the findings, the patient is indicated for admission to the Department of General Surgery for further monitoring and laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

Risk assessment for thromboembolism was performed. CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 1 point (female sex). Based on this low risk score, chronic oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time. The patient is stable for discharge.

gold_reference: The clinician miscalculated the CHA2DS2-VASc score. The patient is 74 years old (1 point), female (1 point), has hypertension (1 point), diabetes mellitus (1 point), and heart failure (1 point). The correct score is 5, not 1. A score of 5 carries a high risk of stroke (approx. 6.7% per year), necessitating oral anticoagulation (e.g., Apixaban or Warfarin).

The patient presents with clinical and laboratory signs of systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) likely secondary to acute pyelonephritis. gold_reference: The report fails to order or perform an urgent renal ultrasound or CT scan. In a patient with acute pyelonephritis and significant Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), it is mandatory to rule out obstructive uropathy (e.g., an infected stone causing hydronephrosis). If an obstruction is present, antibiotics and fluids alone are insufficient; the patient would require urgent urological intervention (stenting or nephrostomy) to drain the infection.

WBC 11.8 [1e9]/L, Erc 4.90 [1e12]/L, HGB 152 g/L, Hct 0.448 L/L, MCV 91.4 fL, MCH 31.0 pg, MCHC 339 g/L, RDW-CV 13.1 %, Trc 245 [1e9]/L, NEU% 78.2 %, LYM% 14.5 %, MONO% 6.3 %, EOS% 0.5 %, BASO% 0.5 %, PV ratio 1.05, APTT 25.1 s, Blood Glucose 5.4 mmol/L, Urea 9.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 118 µmol/L, eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m², AST 24 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, GGT 35 U/L, CRP 8.2 mg/L, Na 136 mmol/L, **K 2.8 mmol/L**, Cl - S 102 mmol/L.

The patient presented with symptoms of acute gastroenteritis and clinical signs of dehydration. Laboratory findings show mild leukocytosis and pre-renal azotemia (elevated urea and creatinine) consistent with volume depletion. After the administration of 1000 ml of saline solution and antiemetic therapy, the patient reports feeling significantly better, the nausea has subsided, and she was able to tolerate a small amount of water orally.
The patient is stable for discharge.

gold_reference: The patient has a potassium level of 2.8 mmol/L, which is moderate-to-severe hypokalemia. This is a critical value that was not addressed in the therapy (only 0.9% NaCl was given, which contains no potassium) or in the discharge conclusion. Hypokalemia of this degree can lead to cardiac arrhythmias and explains the patient's profound weakness and the 'U waves' noted on the EKG.

gold_reference: The physician prescribed Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) to a patient on chronic Warfarin therapy without adjusting the Warfarin dose or scheduling immediate INR monitoring. •	Post-operative status: Arthroscopic ACL reconstruction of the right knee 12 days ago. Currently mobilizing with crutches.
•	History of localized superficial thrombophlebitis in the left leg 3 years ago.
•	No known chronic respiratory or cardiac diseases.
•	Non-smoker.
Medications:
•	Andol 100 mg (discontinued 2 days ago).
•	Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for post-op pain.
Drug Allergies:
•	Penicillin (skin rash).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General: Alert, oriented, appears in moderate discomfort during deep inspiration. Slightly tachypneic.
•	Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. Right-sided pleuritic rub not definitively heard.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, rhythmic, clear tones, no murmurs.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
•	Extremities: Right lower extremity in a hinged knee brace. Right calf is slightly more edematous than the left (+1.5 cm circumference at mid-calf), with mild tenderness on deep palpation of the posterior compartment. No redness or increased warmth.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Sinus tachycardia, heart rate 109 bpm. Normal axis. No ST-segment or T-wave changes. No S1Q3T3 pattern observed.
Laboratory Results:
Hematology:
•	WBC: 9.4 (10^9/L)
•	Hb: 142 (g/L)
•	Platelets: 285 (10^9/L)
Coagulation:
•	PV: 0.98
•	INR: 1.05
•	D-dimer: 3.45 mg/L FEU (Reference range: < 0.50) [Elevated]
Biochemistry:
•	Urea: 5.2 mmol/L
•	Creatinine: 82 µmol/L
•	CRP: 12.4 mg/L (Reference: < 5.0) [Elevated]
•	Troponin T (hs-TnT): 9 ng/L (Reference: < 14)
Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (AP view):
•	Findings: Lung parenchyma shows no signs of consolidation, pneumothorax, or pleural effusion. Heart shadow is normal in size and shape. Diaphragmatic domes are sharp.
(Performed by Dr. Goran Babić, 22.08.2024, 15:45)

Therapy
•	Ketoprofen 100 mg IM (administered in ED)
•	0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV infusion
________________________________________
Conclusion
The patient’s chest pain is likely musculoskeletal/pleuritic in nature, possibly related to altered gait and use of crutches following knee surgery. Clinical examination of the lungs and chest X-ray are unremarkable. Laboratory findings show a non-specific elevation of D-dimer and CRP, which can be expected in the early post-operative period.

Recommendations:
•	Continue with Ibuprofen 400 mg 3x1 for pain management.
•	Follow-up with the orthopedic surgeon as scheduled.
•	Local application of warm compresses to the chest wall.
•	Return to the ED if severe dyspnea, syncope, or hemoptysis occurs.

Discharged in stable condition with improvement in pain after analgesia.

gold_reference: The physician failed to order a CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) or V/Q scan despite the patient having a high clinical probability for Pulmonary Embolism (PE). Discharging the patient with only NSAIDs without ruling out PE is a critical failure that could lead to fatal outcomes.

Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 148/92 mmHg; Pulse: 52/min; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.5°C; VAS: 2 - Mild pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Anamnesis
Patient is a 67-year-old male with a significant medical history including Stage 3 Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD), arterial hypertension, and chronic heart failure (NYHA Class II). 
Current Illness: Presents to the Emergency Department complaining of progressive generalized muscle weakness and a sensation of "heaviness" in his legs over the last 2 days. He reports mild nausea but denies vomiting. He denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath at rest. He mentions he has been strictly following his medication regimen but has recently started using a salt substitute for his meals.
Therapy: Lisinopril 20 mg, Furosemide 40 mg, Carvedilol 6.25 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (reports hives).
Status
Medium musculoskeletal build, alert and oriented x3. Skin is pale and dry, no visible cyanosis or icterus. 
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no rales or wheezing heard. 
Heart: Bradycardic, regular rhythm, S1/S2 clear, no murmurs. 
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender to palpation, bowel sounds present in all four quadrants. Liver and spleen not palpable. 
Extremities: No peripheral edema, symmetrical pulses, slightly decreased strength in lower extremities (4/5).
ECG: Sinus bradycardia, rate 50/min, PR interval 205ms, QRS duration 110ms. Notable for high-amplitude, tented T-waves in precordial leads V2, V3, and V4.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Laboratory Results:
WBC 8.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 3.91 [1e12]/L, HGB 121 g/L, Hct 0.36 L/L, MCV 92.1 fL, Platelets 215 [1e9]/L, NEU% 68.2 %, LYM% 22.1 %, 
Urea 19.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 272 µmol/L, eGFR 21.4 mL/min/1.73m², 
Glucose 5.9 mmol/L, Na 137 mmol/L, K 6.8 mmol/L, Cl 104 mmol/L, Total Bilirubin 11 µmol/L, AST 28 U/L, ALT 24 U/L, CRP 8.4 mg/L.
(Dr. Goran Marković, 18.07.2024 14:22)

No acute cardiopulmonary infiltrates. Heart size is at the upper limit of normal. No pleural effusions.
(Dr. Ivana Kovač, 18.07.2024 15:05)
Therapy
0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV infusion
Conclusion
The patient’s symptoms of weakness and bradycardia were attributed to mild dehydration and his known chronic renal condition. Following the administration of 500 mL of Normal Saline, the patient reported a subjective improvement in his energy levels. Vital signs remained stable throughout the observation period. 
The patient is discharged home with instructions to discontinue the use of salt substitutes and to adhere to a low-potassium diet. He is advised to follow up with his primary care physician or nephrologist within 48 hours for a repeat metabolic panel to monitor renal function and electrolytes. 
If he experiences chest pain, fainting, or worsening weakness, he should return to the emergency department immediately.

R10.1 Pain localized to upper abdomen
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 108/64 mmHg; Pulse: 55/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Anamnesis
Past Medical History: Arterial hypertension for 10 years, treated with Amlodipine. Hypercholesterolemia. No history of coronary artery disease. Chronic smoker (25 pack-years).
Current Illness: Patient presented to the emergency department complaining of sudden onset, severe "burning" pain in the epigastrium that began approximately 90 minutes ago after eating a spicy meal. The pain radiates slightly to the lower chest and neck. He reports significant nausea and one episode of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. He denies fever, cough, or changes in bowel habits.
Therapy: Amlodipine 5 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
General Status: Middle-aged male, appearing acutely ill, moderately diaphoretic (sweating), pale. Alert and oriented x3.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Bradycardic, regular rhythm, S1 and S2 clear, no murmurs or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, deep tenderness on palpation in the epigastric region. No guarding or rebound tenderness. Liver and spleen not palpable. Normal bowel sounds.
Extremities: Cool to the touch peripherally, no edema, pulses palpable but weak.
ECG: Sinus bradycardia, rate 54/min. 1.5mm ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Subtle ST-segment depression in leads I and aVL.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.90 [1e12]/L, HGB 151 g/L, Hct 0.44 L/L, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, Glucose 7.8 mmol/L, Urea 6.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, CRP 2.1 mg/L.
Troponin T (hs-cTnT): 0.048 ng/mL (Reference range: <0.014 ng/mL).
AST 48 U/L, ALT 35 U/L, GGT 22 U/L, ALP 65 U/L, Amylase 58 U/L.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 22.10.2024 19:45)

Summary Image of Thoracic Organs:
No evidence of acute pulmonary infiltrates or congestion. Heart size at the upper limit of normal. Mediastinum appears normal.
(Dr. Marko Ivić, 22.10.2024 20:10)

Abdominal Ultrasound:
No evidence of cholecystitis or cholelithiasis. Pancreas appears normal in size and echogenicity. No free intraperitoneal fluid visualized.
(Dr. Ana Babić, 22.10.2024 20:30)
Therapy
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
"GI Cocktail" (30mL Antacid + 10mL Lidocaine 2%) administered orally
Conclusion
Patient reports significant improvement in epigastric pain (VAS reduced from 8 to 2) following the administration of the GI cocktail and IV pantoprazole. The clinical picture is highly suggestive of acute gastritis exacerbated by dietary triggers. ECG shows sinus bradycardia and minor repolarization changes, likely chronic or secondary to the acute abdominal distress. Minimal troponin elevation is noted, possibly due to demand or analytical variation given the resolution of symptoms with GI therapy.
Discharged home.
Recommendations:
- Control with primary care physician in 2 days.
- Pantoprazole 40 mg once daily for 14 days.
- Bland diet.
- Return to ED if chest pain recurs or worsens.
(Dr. Stjepan Radić, MD, 22.10.2024 21:15)
gold_reference: The physician missed an Inferior Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). The patient had classic 'red flags': epigastric pain in a smoker with hypertension, diaphoresis, bradycardia, and an ECG showing ST elevation in II, III, and aVF with reciprocal changes in I and aVL. Additionally, the high-sensitivity Troponin T was elevated above the reference range. The physician incorrectly attributed the pain relief from a 'GI cocktail' as confirmation of a gastrointestinal cause, a common cognitive bias (premature closure).

Symptoms began shortly after returning from a transatlantic flight (10 hours). This morning, the patient experienced a sudden onset of mild chest tightness and shortness of breath while walking to the bathroom. No cough, no hemoptysis, no syncope.
Functions: Appetite normal, urination regular, last stool yesterday.
Habits: Does not smoke, social alcohol consumption.
Medications: None.
Allergies: Denies.
Status
Medium musculoskeletal build, alert, communicative, oriented. Patient appears mildly tachypneic at rest. Skin and visible mucous membranes are of normal color.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. SpO2 91% on room air.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Peristalsis present.
Extremities: Right lower leg is visibly swollen from the mid-calf to the ankle, erythematous and warm to the touch. Calf circumference is 4 cm greater on the right than the left. Pitting edema present on the right. Homans' sign positive on the right. Peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.
ECG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 115/min, normal axis, S1Q3T3 pattern noted.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 5.10 [1e12]/L, HGB 155 g/L, Hct 0.460 L/L, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, D-Dimer 4.12 mg/L FEU (Ref: <0.50), Troponin T 14 ng/L (Ref: <14), CRP 8.2 mg/L, Na 140 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, Urea 5.4 mmol/L.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 22.05.2024 09:15)
Color Doppler Ultrasound of Lower Extremity Veins:
The common femoral, superficial femoral, and popliteal veins of the right leg are dilated and non-compressible, filled with hypoechoic thrombotic masses. No flow detected upon provocation. Findings consistent with acute deep vein thrombosis (DVT) of the right leg. Left leg veins show normal compressibility and flow.
(Dr. Elena Radić, 22.05.2024 10:30)
Summary Image of Thoracic Organs:
No visible pneumothorax. No obvious infiltrative changes. Heart shadow size within physiological limits.
(Dr. Elena Radić, 22.05.2024 10:45)

Enoxaparin (Clexane) 80 mg SC
Analgesic (Paracetamol 1g IV)
O2 2 L/min via nasal cannula
Conclusion
Confirmed acute deep vein thrombosis of the right lower extremity. Patient is hemodynamically stable but remains mildly tachycardic and hypoxic. Admission to the Department of Internal Medicine (Vascular ward) for further anticoagulation therapy and leg elevation.
(Dr. Marko Kovač, 22.05.2024 11:30)
gold_reference: The patient presents with high-risk clinical features for Pulmonary Embolism (PE), including a confirmed DVT, sudden onset shortness of breath, tachycardia (114/min), hypoxia (SpO2 91%), and an S1Q3T3 pattern on ECG. Despite a Wells Score indicating a high probability of PE and clinical signs of respiratory compromise, the physician failed to order a CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) or V/Q scan to confirm/rule out PE before transferring the patient to a standard ward.

Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
General weakness and mild confusion
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), and benign prostatic hyperplasia.
Current Illness: Patient's daughter reports that for the last 4-5 days the patient has been increasingly lethargic and "not himself." He complains of a mild headache and significant generalized weakness. No fever, no cough, no urinary symptoms. The daughter notes he has been taking his "water pill" (furosemide) daily despite the recent heatwave and decreased appetite.
Functions: Reduced appetite over the last week, decreased fluid intake. Stool regular. Urination reported as decreased in volume.
Habits: Non-smoker, occasional alcohol use (one glass of wine with lunch).
Medications: Fursemid 40 mg 1x1, Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1x1, Pantoprazole 20 mg 1x1, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
Elderly male, appears mildly dehydrated and fatigued. Conscious, responds to commands, but exhibits slow mentation and is disoriented to exact date. Afebrile, eupneic at rest. Skin turgor slightly reduced, mucous membranes dry. No peripheral lymphadenopathy.
Neck: No jugular venous distention, no carotid bruits.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Regular rhythm, S1 and S2 clear, no murmurs. BP 108/64 mmHg (sitting), Pulse 90/min.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses or organomegaly. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: No pretibial edema. Peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric.

Laboratory Results:
WBC 6.8 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.42 [1e12]/L, HGB 138 g/L, Hct 0.412 L/L, MCV 93.2 fL, MCH 31.2 pg, Platelets 224 [1e9]/L.
Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 10.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 118 µmol/L (Baseline 90), eGFR 54 mL/min/1.73m², CRP 3.5 mg/L.
Na 119 mmol/L, K 3.7 mmol/L, Cl 85 mmol/L, Ca 2.22 mmol/L.
(Dr. Goran Babić, Medical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 11:30)
Radiographic Imaging of Lungs (AP):
No signs of pleural effusion or pulmonary congestion. No acute infiltrates.
Conclusion
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 18.07.2024 13:45)
The clinical picture is suggestive of mild dehydration and exhaustion, likely exacerbated by the recent high ambient temperatures and ongoing diuretic therapy. The patient is hemodynamically stable.
Recommendations:
1. Temporary discontinuation of Fursemid for the next 3 days.
2. Increase oral fluid intake (water, rehydration salts, soups).
3. Follow-up with primary care physician in 48 hours for control of renal function and electrolytes.
4. If symptoms of confusion worsen or fainting occurs, return to the ED.
Patient discharged home in the company of his daughter.
gold_reference: The patient has a sodium level of 119 mmol/L, which represents severe, life-threatening hyponatremia. This level is associated with high risks of cerebral edema, seizures, and permanent neurological damage. Despite this critical value, the physician attributed the symptoms to 'mild dehydration' and discharged the patient with advice to increase oral water intake, which could further dilute the serum sodium and worsen the condition.

The patient is currently hemodynamically stable after spontaneous cardioversion. Risk of thromboembolism was assessed using the CHA2DS2-VASc score.

The patient presented with a first episode of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation which spontaneously converted to sinus rhythm in the emergency department. He remains hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic.

Recommendations:

gold_reference: The doctor incorrectly calculated the CHA2DS2-VASc score. The patient is 72 years old (+1 point), has Hypertension (+1 point), and has Diabetes Mellitus (+1 point). Lungs: Tachypneic, bilateral clear breath sounds, no rales or wheezing. SpO2 92% on room air, improves to 97% with 3L oxygen via nasal cannula.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. S1Q3T3 pattern not clearly seen, but sinus tachycardia present. BP 120/80 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly, bowel sounds active.
Extremities: Left hip surgical incision is clean, dry, and intact. No significant calf swelling, no redness or warmth. Peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric. Wells Score for PE: 4.5 (High probability).

EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 115/min, normal axis, prominent S wave in lead I, Q wave and inverted T wave in lead III (S1Q3T3 pattern).

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.10 [1e12]/L, HGB 128 g/L, Hct 0.380 L/L, MCV 92.7 fL, Trc 245 [1e9]/L, NEU% 78.2%, LYM% 15.4%.
PV ratio 0.85, INR 1.15, APTT 28.4 s.
D-dimer: 3.42 mg/L FEU (Ref: <0.50) - Elevated.
Troponin T (hs): 18 ng/L (Ref: <14) - Mildly elevated.
NT-proBNP: 420 pg/mL (Ref: <125) - Elevated.
Blood Glucose: 7.2 mmol/L, Urea: 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine: 74 µmol/L (eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m²), Na: 139 mmol/L, K: 4.1 mmol/L, CRP: 12.5 mg/L.
(MARIĆ IVANA mag.med.biochem., 20.07.2024 10:15)

Radiology:
Chest X-ray (AP, Bedside): No focal consolidations, no pneumothorax. Heart size within normal limits. Small right-sided pleural effusion.
CT Angiography of the Chest (CTPA):
Findings: Multiple filling defects identified within the segmental and subsegmental branches of the right lower lobe pulmonary artery, consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. No evidence of central saddle embolism. Right ventricle to left ventricle (RV/LV) ratio is 0.9 (no significant right heart strain). No signs of pulmonary infarction.
(Dr. S. Horvat, Radiologist, 20.07.2024 11:30)

Therapy administered in ED:
- Oxygen 3L/min via nasal cannula
- Sodium Chloride 0.9% 500 ml i.v.
- Enoxaparin (Clexane) 80 mg s.c. (therapeutic dose for 80kg patient)
- Morphine 2 mg i.v. for pain/anxiety

Conclusion
(Dr. MARKO PERIĆ, MD, 20.07.2024 12:45)
65-year-old patient with a recent history of major orthopedic surgery presents with clinical and radiological evidence of acute segmental pulmonary embolism. Despite being on low-dose prophylactic rivaroxaban, the patient developed a breakthrough thromboembolic event.
The patient is currently hemodynamically stable. CTPA shows no significant right ventricular strain, but biomarkers (Troponin and NT-proBNP) are mildly elevated, categorizing this as an intermediate-low risk PE (PESI Class III).
The patient is admitted to the Department of Pulmonology for further monitoring and initiation of therapeutic anticoagulation.

Plan:
1. Transfer to Pulmonology ward.
2. Continue therapeutic Enoxaparin 1mg/kg every 12 hours.
3. Transition to therapeutic dose Apixaban or Rivaroxaban after 24-48 hours of observation.
4. Monitor vital signs and oxygen saturation.
5. Physical therapy consultation for gradual mobilization.
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

Anamnesis
Current Illness: A 72-year-old patient presents to the emergency department complaining of nausea, vomiting, and non-bloody diarrhea persisting for the last three days. He estimates 6-8 watery stools per day. He reports significant fatigue and a "shaky" feeling in his muscles. He has been unable to maintain adequate oral intake.
Past Illnesses: Arterial hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Chronic Kidney Disease (Stage G3a).
Family History: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 60.
Functions: Reduced urine output over the last 24 hours. Chronic constipation usually, now with diarrhea.

Therapy: Ramipril 10 mg 1x1, Metformin 1000 mg 2x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Status
Patient is of medium musculoskeletal build, appears fatigued and mildly ill. Oriented to person, place, and time.
Skin and Mucosa: Mucous membranes are dry, skin turgor is reduced. No peripheral edema.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Rhythmic action, heart sounds are clear, no murmurs. BP 105/65 mmHg.
Abdomen: Level with the chest, soft, diffusely tender on deep palpation, no signs of peritoneal irritation. Peristalsis is hyperactive.
Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.

EKG: Sinus rhythm, frequency 88/min, normal electrical axis, no ST-segment deviations, no T-wave abnormalities noted.

CRP 12.5 mg/L.
AST 22 U/L, ALT 25 U/L, GGT 30 U/L.
(STANIŠIĆ LADA mag.med.biochem., 18.08.2024 10:45)

Radiographic Imaging:
Abdominal X-ray (Erect):
No free intraperitoneal air. Multiple fluid-filled loops of small bowel without evidence of mechanical obstruction. Significant amount of fecal material in the colon.
Dr. M. Horvat

Therapy in ED:
- Sol. 0.9% NaCl 1000 ml i.v.
- Reglan 10 mg i.v.

Conclusion:
(Dr. P. VUKOVIĆ, MD, 18.08.2024 12:30)
The patient presented with symptoms of viral gastroenteritis and associated dehydration. During his stay in the emergency department, he received parenteral rehydration and antiemetic therapy, after which he reported feeling significantly better and was able to tolerate a small amount of oral fluids. Laboratory findings show a mild elevation in inflammatory markers and signs of pre-renal kidney injury likely secondary to volume depletion. Electrolytes show minor fluctuations consistent with diarrhea. 

The patient is hemodynamically stable and is discharged home.
Recommendations:
- Continue oral rehydration with electrolyte solutions (Rehydromix).
- Light diet (boiled rice, toast, bananas).
- Temporarily discontinue Metformin and Ramipril for 48 hours until oral intake improves.
- Follow-up with the primary care physician in 2 days for repeat lab work (urea, creatinine, electrolytes).
- In case of recurrence of vomiting or decreased consciousness, return to the ED immediately.
gold_reference: The patient has a potassium level of 6.6 mmol/L, which constitutes severe, life-threatening hyperkalemia. This was dismissed in the conclusion as 'minor fluctuations' and the patient was discharged. High potassium levels can lead to fatal cardiac arrhythmias.

Vital Signs
Date: 18.07.2024 09:15; BP: 138/84 mmHg; Pulse: 72/min; SpO2: 96%; Resp: 16/min; Temp: 36.7°C; Guk: 8.4 mmol/L

Anamnesis
Current Illness: The patient is a 72-year-old male who presents to the ED after a brief loss of consciousness while standing at a grocery store this morning. Witnesses state he "slumped to the floor" and was unconscious for approximately 30 seconds. No tonic-clonic movements observed. No tongue biting or urinary incontinence. Upon regaining consciousness, the patient felt slightly confused but quickly returned to baseline. Currently complains of mild lightheadedness when standing. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath.
Past Illnesses: Arterial hypertension for 15 years, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, Stage 3 Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD).
Family History: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 65.
Functions: Normal urination, chronic constipation.
Habits: Former smoker (quit 10 years ago), denies alcohol use.
Therapy: Lisinopril 20 mg 1x1, Metformin 1000 mg 2x1, Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Status
Alert, oriented x3, communicative, eupneic, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes dry, reduced skin turgor. 
Neck: No carotid bruits, no jugular venous distension.
Lungs: Auscultation reveals clear breath sounds bilaterally.
Heart: Regular rhythm, S1 and S2 clear, no murmurs or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly, bowel sounds present.
Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetrical. 
Neurological: No focal deficits, cranial nerves II-XII intact, negative Romberg test.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
EKG: Sinus rhythm, rate 70/min, normal axis, no ST-segment or T-wave changes, PR interval 160ms, QRS 90ms.

Laboratory Findings:
WBC 8.2 x10^9/L, RBC 3.90 x10^12/L, HGB 118 g/L, HCT 0.35 L/L, Platelets 210 x10^9/L.
Glucose 8.4 mmol/L, Urea 12.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 162 µmol/L (Baseline 145 µmol/L), eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m².
Sodium 139 mmol/L, **Potassium 6.4 mmol/L**, Chloride 104 mmol/L.
CRP 4.2 mg/L, hs-TNT 11.4 ng/L (Normal <14).

Radiographic Imaging:
Chest X-ray (PA): No acute cardiopulmonary infiltrates. Heart size within normal limits. No pleural effusions.

Conclusion:
(Dr. M. Horvat, MD, 18.07.2024 13:45)
The patient was evaluated for a syncopal episode. Based on the history and clinical presentation, the event is most likely vasovagal or orthostatic in nature, possibly exacerbated by mild dehydration and antihypertensive medication. Acute coronary syndrome has been ruled out by negative cardiac enzymes and a non-ischemic EKG. Neurological examination is unremarkable. The patient remained stable during the observation period and reported feeling better after resting.

The patient is discharged home in stable condition.
Advised to increase oral fluid intake and move slowly when transitioning from a sitting to a standing position.
Continue current home medications.
Follow-up with the primary care physician in 2-3 days for blood pressure monitoring.
In case of recurrent syncope or chest pain, return to the emergency department immediately.
gold_reference: The patient has a potassium level of 6.4 mmol/L, which indicates severe hyperkalemia. This is a potentially life-threatening condition that can lead to cardiac arrhythmias or arrest. Despite this critical value, the physician's conclusion attributes the syncope to dehydration, fails to mention the hyperkalemia, and instructs the patient to continue 'current home medications' (which includes Lisinopril, an ACE inhibitor that further increases potassium levels).

Anamnesis
Personal Anamnesis: Chronic arterial hypertension. Dyslipidemia. Diagnosed with bronchial asthma 15 years ago, with several emergency visits in the past for exacerbations; currently managed with daily maintenance therapy. No history of coronary artery disease or previous strokes.
Current Illness: Patient presents to the emergency department complaining of sudden onset palpitations and a "racing heart" that began this morning around 07:00 while eating breakfast. The palpitations are accompanied by mild retrosternal pressure and a sensation of shortness of breath. Denies syncope or near-syncope. Reports that her breathing has been slightly "tight" over the last two days due to a mild upper respiratory infection, for which she has been using her rescue inhaler more frequently.
Functions: Normal appetite, no dysuria, regular bowel movements.
Habits: Ex-smoker (quit 10 years ago), denies alcohol consumption.
Medications: Ramipril 5 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg, Symbicort (Budesonide/Formoterol) 160/4.5 mcg 2 puffs twice daily, Ventolin (Salbutamol) as needed.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
Elderly female, alert, oriented x3, appears mildly distressed by palpitations. Eupneic at rest but uses accessory muscles slightly during speech.
Skin: No cyanosis, no diaphoresis.
Lungs: Bilaterally decreased breath sounds with occasional expiratory wheezing noted in the lower fields.
Heart: Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic. Clear tones, no S3 or S4. No murmurs. BP 120/75 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly, peristalsis present.
Extremities: No peripheral edema, peripheral pulses palpable but irregular.
EKG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, heart rate 145/min. Normal axis. No ST-segment elevation or depression. No Q waves.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Laboratory Results:
WBC 9.4 [1e9]/L, Erc 4.45 [1e12]/L, HGB 138 g/L, Hct 0.41 L/L, MCV 92.1 fL, Trc 245 [1e9]/L, NEU% 68.2 %, LYM% 22.1 %, Blood Glucose 6.2 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m², T-BIL 12 µmol/L, AST 28 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, GGT 24 U/L, LDH 195 U/L, CK 110 U/L, Troponin T (hs-TnT) 12 ng/L (Normal <14), CRP 12.5 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 3.7 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L, Mg 0.78 mmol/L.
(DR. M. HORVAT, 22.10.2024 10:30)

Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (PA):
No signs of acute pulmonary infiltrates or congestion. Heart shadow appears mildly enlarged. Costophrenic angles are sharp.

Conclusion
(DR. L. KOVAČ, MD, 22.10.2024 11:15)
Patient presents with new-onset symptomatic atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. Laboratory findings show no significant electrolyte imbalance or evidence of myocardial infarction (Troponin negative). Lung auscultation reveals mild wheezing consistent with the patient's history of asthma and recent respiratory infection.

Therapy administered in ED:
- 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion
- Propranolol 40 mg p.o. for heart rate control
- Pantoprazole 40 mg IV

Disposition:
The patient remains in the observation unit for rate control monitoring. Cardiology consultation requested for possible elective cardioversion or long-term rhythm management.
gold_reference: The patient has a documented history of bronchial asthma and is currently presenting with mild expiratory wheezing. Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that blocks both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors. Blocking beta-2 receptors in the bronchial smooth muscle can precipitate severe bronchospasm or an acute asthma attack in susceptible patients.

Reason for Visit
Shortness of breath and worsening leg swelling over the last 4 days.
________________________________________
Medical History (Anamnesis)
Current Complaints: 
The patient presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion, which has worsened to orthopnea (requires 3 pillows to sleep). Reports waking up twice last night gasping for air (PND). Noted significant swelling in both lower extremities and a weight gain of 4 kg in the past week. Denies chest pain, fever, or productive cough.
Medical History:

•	Chronic Kidney Disease (Stage G3a).

•	General: Alert, mildly distressed, sitting upright to breathe.

•	Heart: Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic. S3 gallop present. No murmurs.
•	Neck: Jugular venous distension (JVD) present, approximately 8 cm above the sternal angle.

________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate 108 bpm. Left axis deviation. No acute ST-T wave changes.
Laboratory Results:
Hematology:
•	WBC: 8.4 (10^9/L)
•	Hb: 118 (g/L) (slightly low)

Electrolytes:

Conclusion
The patient is experiencing an acute exacerbation of chronic heart failure (ADHF) with signs of volume overload and mild respiratory distress. There is a concomitant acute-on-chronic kidney injury, likely cardiorenal in nature. After IV diuretic administration, the patient reports subjective improvement in breathing.

Recommendations:
•	Discharge home with instructions to increase oral Furosemide to 80 mg daily for the next 5 days.
•	Restrict fluid intake to 1.5L per day.
•	Follow-up with primary care physician or cardiologist within 48-72 hours for electrolyte and renal function monitoring.
•	Return to ED if shortness of breath worsens or if unable to lie flat.
Discharged in stable condition.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 20.08.2024, 13:45)
gold_reference: The patient presents with Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (ADHF) characterized by hypoxia (SpO2 91% on RA), significant volume overload (JVD, 3+ edema, PND/orthopnea), and a significantly elevated NT-proBNP. Furthermore, the patient has an Acute Kidney Injury (Creatinine 184 vs baseline 125) and hyponatremia (Na 132). According to clinical guidelines, patients with new-onset hypoxia, significant congestion, and worsening renal function require inpatient admission for IV diuresis, careful monitoring of renal function/electrolytes, and optimization of heart failure therapy.

Anamnesis
From history: Patient has a history of Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 12 years ago), essential hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Chronic smoker (1 pack/day for 30 years). Previous surgical history includes laparoscopic cholecystectomy in 2015.
CURRENT ILLNESS: Patient presents to the ED complaining of sudden onset of severe epigastric pain and "burning" sensation in the upper abdomen that started approximately 3 hours ago after eating a spicy meal. The pain is associated with significant nausea and two episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. He describes the pain as a heavy pressure in the pit of his stomach. He denies substernal chest pain, radiation to the arms, or shortness of breath. He took an over-the-counter antacid at home with minimal relief.
Functions: Appetite normally good, regular stools, no melena or hematochezia. Urination regular.
Habits: Smoker (20/day), occasional alcohol consumption.
Medications: Metformin 1000 mg 1+0+1, Lisinopril 10 mg 1+0+0, Atorvastatin 20 mg 0+0+1.
Drug Allergies: Denied.
Status
Patient is a 62-year-old male, appearing moderately distressed and diaphoretic (mildly sweaty). Oriented x3.
Head and Neck: Mucous membranes slightly dry. No jugular venous distention. No carotid bruits.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles or wheezes.
Heart: Regular rhythm, relatively slow rate (bradycardia), heart sounds S1 and S2 are present, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, significant tenderness localized to the epigastric region. No rebound or guarding. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds present and active in all four quadrants.
Extremities: Warm and well-perfused. No peripheral edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetrical.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus bradycardia, frequency 56/min, normal axis. Presence of Q-waves in leads III and aVF. No acute ST-segment elevation noted. Non-specific ST-T wave changes in the precordial leads.
Lab Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.90 [1e12]/L, HGB 152 g/L, Hct 0.455 L/L, Platelets 240 [1e9]/L, NEU% 72.0%.
Glucose 12.8 mmol/L (elevated), Urea 7.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 95 µmol/L (eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m²), Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
AST 45 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, GGT 42 U/L, LDH 290 U/L, Amylase 65 U/L, Lipase 40 U/L.
hs-TNT (High-sensitivity Troponin T): 52.4 ng/L (Reference: <14.0 ng/L).
CRP: 8.5 mg/L.
(Lab tech: Goran Marković, 17.07.2024 19:45)
Radiographic Imaging
Abdominal X-ray (Upright): No free air under the diaphragm. No dilated loops of bowel or air-fluid levels.
Chest X-ray (PA): No acute infiltrates. Heart size within upper limits of normal. Costophrenic angles sharp.
Therapy
20 mg Pantoprazole IV.
10 mg Metoclopramide IV.
"GI Cocktail" (30 ml Antacid + 10 ml Lidocaine viscous) orally.
Conclusion
(Dr. Hrvoje Babić, 17.07.2024 21:00)
Patient reports significant improvement in epigastric pain following the administration of the GI cocktail and pantoprazole. The clinical presentation is highly suggestive of acute gastritis or peptic ulcer disease exacerbated by dietary intake. The mild elevation in hs-TNT is noted but likely represents chronic elevation in the setting of long-standing diabetes and hypertension, as the patient lacks typical anginal symptoms and the ECG shows no acute ST-segment elevations.
Patient is stable for discharge.
Recommendations:
- Follow up with primary care physician in 2-3 days.
- Start Pantoprazole 40 mg once daily for 14 days.
- Bland diet; avoid spicy foods and NSAIDs.
- Return to ED if pain worsens, or if new symptoms like black stools or shortness of breath occur.
gold_reference: The physician missed an Acute Coronary Syndrome (specifically an inferior myocardial infarction). The patient has several high-risk features: diabetes (which often causes atypical/non-chest pain presentations of MI), hypertension, smoking history, and diaphoresis. The epigastric pain, sinus bradycardia, and Q-waves in leads III and aVF are classic signs of an inferior MI. Most importantly, the hs-Troponin T (52.4 ng/L) is significantly above the upper reference limit (14 ng/L), which should never be dismissed as 'chronic' without serial testing or comparison to a baseline.

Blood Glucose: 6.4 mmol/L.
Imaging:
CT Angiography of the Chest (PE Protocol): Multiple filling defects identified within the segmental and subsegmental branches of the right lower lobe pulmonary artery, consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. No evidence of right ventricular strain (RV/LV ratio < 1.0). No pulmonary opacities or pleural effusions.
Venous Doppler Ultrasound (Left Leg): Non-compressible common femoral and popliteal veins, confirming acute deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
(Dr. Marek Horvat, Radiologist, 20.08.2024 14:15)
Conclusion
The patient presents with acute pulmonary embolism and concurrent DVT three weeks post-orthopedic surgery. Wells Score for PE is 9.0 (High Risk: Clinical signs of DVT +3, Heart rate >100 +1.5, Recent surgery +1.5, Alternative diagnosis less likely +3). PESI (Pulmonary Embolism Severity Index) score is 91 (Class III - Moderate risk).
Given the presence of the embolism despite prophylactic anticoagulation (Rivaroxaban 10mg), the patient is transitioned to therapeutic anticoagulation.
Therapy:
- Oxygen via nasal cannula at 2L/min (SpO2 improved to 97%).
- Enoxaparin 80 mg SC (1mg/kg for 80kg weight).
Patient is hemodynamically stable. Admitted to the Department of Internal Medicine for further monitoring and titration of oral anticoagulation therapy.
(Dr. Elena Kovac, MD, 20.08.2024 15:30)
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

Vital Signs

18.08.2024, 09:15	142/88	114	93	22	36.8	5.8	4	15	A
18.08.2024, 11:30	135/82	108	94	18	-	-	2	15	A
________________________________________
Reason for Visit
Sudden onset of shortness of breath and chest tightness.
________________________________________
Medical History (Anamnesis)
Current Complaints: 
The patient presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath that started approximately three hours ago while sitting. Reports a "smothering" sensation and mild retrosternal tightness. Denies cough, fever, or hemoptysis. Patient appears anxious and states she feels like "something is very wrong."

Medical History:
•	Status post total knee arthroplasty (right) 12 days ago; currently in rehabilitation phase, limited mobility.
•	Breast cancer (Stage II), status post lumpectomy and radiation (2022). Currently on maintenance hormonal therapy.
•	Hypertension, well-controlled.
•	Hypercholesterolemia.

Medications:
•	Tamoxifen 20 mg daily.
•	Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
•	Atorvastatin 20 mg daily.
•	Paracetamol 500 mg as needed for surgical pain.

Drug Allergies:
•	Penicillin (rash).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General: Patient is tachypneic and appears visibly distressed/anxious. Well-nourished, no cyanosis.
•	Lungs: Bilaterally clear breath sounds. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi. Chest wall expansion symmetrical.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. S1, S2 clear. No murmurs or gallops.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
•	Extremities: Right knee surgical site is healing well with mild residual post-operative edema. No calf tenderness or redness noted bilaterally. Peripheral pulses 2+ and equal.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Sinus tachycardia, heart rate 112 bpm. Normal axis. No ST-segment or T-wave changes. No S1Q3T3 pattern observed.

Laboratory Results:
Hematology - Complete Blood Count (CBC):
•	WBC: 9.4 (10^9/L)
•	RBC: 4.15 (10^12/L)
•	Hb: 128 (g/L)
•	Hct: 0.382 (L/L)
•	MCV: 91.2 (fL)
•	Trc: 245 (10^9/L)

Coagulation:
•	PV: 0.98
•	INR: 1.02
•	APTT: 26.4 s
•	D-dimer: 3.45 mg/L FEU (elevated, Ref: < 0.50)

Biochemistry:
•	Glucose: 5.8 mmol/L
•	Urea: 5.2 mmol/L
•	Creatinine: 72 µmol/L
•	hs-Troponin I: 12 ng/L (Ref: < 26)
•	CRP: 14.2 mg/L (mildly elevated)

Radiology:
Chest X-ray (PA view):
•	Lungs are clear. No evidence of pneumothorax, pleural effusion, or focal infiltrates. Heart size is within normal limits.
(Performed by Dr. Marinović Ivan, 18.08.2024, 10:45)
________________________________________
Therapy
•	Diazepam 5 mg orally (administered at 10:15)
•	Oxygen 2L/min via nasal cannula (discontinued after 30 mins as SpO2 stabilized at 94-95%)
________________________________________
Conclusion
Clinical evaluation and imaging did not reveal acute pulmonary or cardiac pathology. Chest X-ray is unremarkable. Troponin is negative, ruling out acute myocardial infarction. The patient's symptoms of shortness of breath and tachycardia improved following the administration of an anxiolytic. The elevated D-dimer is likely non-specific, potentially related to recent major orthopedic surgery (knee arthroplasty) and the post-operative inflammatory state.

Recommendations:
•	Follow up with primary care physician for anxiety management.
•	Continue current post-operative rehabilitation.
•	Return to ED if symptoms worsen or if unilateral leg swelling develops.

Discharged in stable condition.

(Dr. Horvat Marko, MD, 18.08.2024, 12:30)
gold_reference: The clinician failed to work up a Pulmonary Embolism (PE) in a high-risk patient. The patient has multiple major risk factors (recent major orthopedic surgery 12 days ago, active hormonal therapy for cancer/Tamoxifen, and malignancy history). She presented with sudden onset dyspnea, tachycardia, and hypoxia (SpO2 93%), and had a significantly elevated D-dimer (3.45). While D-dimer can be elevated post-surgery, the clinical presentation and high Wells score (3.0 for tachycardia, 1.5 for recent surgery, 1.5 for cancer = 6.0, indicating high probability) mandate a CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA) or V/Q scan. Attributing these findings to 'anxiety' without definitive imaging is a critical diagnostic failure.

Reason for Visit
Sudden, severe left-sided flank pain radiating to the groin.
________________________________________
Medical History (Anamnesis)
Current Complaints: Patient reports sudden onset of agonizing left flank pain starting approximately 2 hours ago. The pain is colicky in nature and radiates towards the left inguinal region. Associated with significant nausea and two episodes of non-bilious vomiting. Denies fever, chills, or dysuria. Noticed slightly darker urine this morning.
Medical History:
•	Arterial hypertension, diagnosed 2 years ago.
•	Hyperuricemia.
•	No previous history of nephrolithiasis.
•	No prior abdominal surgeries.
Medications:
•	Ramipril 5 mg 1x1.
•	Allopurinol 100 mg 1x1.
Drug Allergies:
•	Penicillin (reports rash).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General: Patient appears in acute distress, restless, unable to find a comfortable position. Eupneic, afebrile, skin is pale and slightly diaphoretic.
•	Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
•	Heart: Rhythmic, slightly tachycardic (92 bpm), clear tones, no murmurs.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. No rebound tenderness or guarding. Bowel sounds present. No palpable masses or organomegaly.
•	Lumbar Region: Significant tenderness to percussion over the left costovertebral angle (CVA). Right side is painless.
•	Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Sinus rhythm, heart rate 89 bpm, normal axis, no ST-T segment abnormalities.
Laboratory Results:
Hematology - Complete Blood Count (CBC):
•	WBC: 9.4 (10^9/L)
•	RBC: 5.12 (10^12/L)
•	Hb: 152 (g/L)
•	Hct: 0.452 (L/L)
•	Platelets: 245 (10^9/L)
Biochemistry:
•	Glucose: 5.4 mmol/L
•	Urea: 5.8 mmol/L
•	Creatinine: 92 µmol/L (eGFR 88.4 mL/min/1.73m²)
•	CRP: 3.2 mg/L
•	Na: 140 mmol/L
•	K: 4.2 mmol/L
•	Uric Acid: 410 µmol/L (elevated)
Urinalysis (Sediment):
•	Appearance: Slightly cloudy, yellow.
•	Erythrocytes: 40-50 /hpf (Microhematuria).
•	Leukocytes: 2-4 /hpf.
•	Nitrites: Negative.
•	Protein: Trace.
Imaging:
Ultrasound of the Abdomen and Urinary Tract:
•	Right kidney: Normal size and parenchymal thickness, no signs of hydronephrosis or stones.
•	Left kidney: 11.5 cm, shows mild dilatation of the pyelocaliceal system (Grade 1 hydronephrosis).
•	Ureter: Proximal left ureter is slightly dilated. A hyperechoic structure measuring approximately 5 mm is visualized in the mid-ureter with posterior acoustic shadowing.
•	Bladder: Partially filled, thin walls, no intraluminal masses.
(Performed by Dr. Aras Kozlevac, 22.10.2024, 04:20)
________________________________________
Therapy in ED
•	Saline 0.9% 500 mL IV infusion.
•	Ketorolac 30 mg IV.
•	Pantoprazole 40 mg IV.
•	Metoclopramide 10 mg IV.
________________________________________
Conclusion
Clinical presentation and ultrasound findings are consistent with acute renal colic due to a 5 mm calculus in the left ureter. Acute kidney injury and urinary tract infection (pyelonephritis) have been ruled out based on normal creatinine, low CRP, and absence of leukocyturia/fever. Pain was successfully managed in the ED.

Recommendations:
•	Increase fluid intake (2.5 - 3 liters per day).
•	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg once daily for 10 days (to facilitate stone passage).
•	Ibuprofen 400 mg or Naproxen 500 mg as needed for pain (up to 3 times daily).
•	Strain urine to catch the stone for analysis if it passes.
•	Urology follow-up in 7 days with a repeat ultrasound.
•	Immediate return to the ED if high fever, uncontrollable pain, or inability to urinate occurs.

Discharged in stable condition, VAS 2/10.

(Dr. Elena Vrančić, MD, 22.10.2024, 06:15)

Diagnoses
N10 Acute tubulo-interstitial nephritis (Acute pyelonephritis)

R30.0 Dysuria
Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 112/70 mmHg; Pulse: 112/min; SpO2: 98%; Temp: 39.2°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15
Reason for visit
Right-sided flank pain, high fever, and shivering.
History
Personal history: The patient has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections, with the last episode occurring six months ago, treated successfully with nitrofurantoin. No history of nephrolithiasis or congenital urinary tract abnormalities. No previous surgeries.
Current illness: Symptoms began two days ago with increased urinary frequency and burning during urination (dysuria). This morning, the patient developed sharp, constant pain in the right flank radiating toward the groin. Since noon, she has experienced high fever (up to 39.5°C at home), rigors, and has vomited twice. She took 1000 mg of paracetamol with minimal effect on the pain.
Functions: Decreased appetite due to nausea, frequent urination of small amounts, last bowel movement yesterday (normal consistency).
Habits: Non-smoker, does not consume alcohol.
Medications: None.
Drug allergies: Denies.
Status
General appearance: The patient appears acutely ill, shivering, communicative, and oriented. Eupneic at rest, febrile. Skin is warm and dry, no rashes. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No peripheral edema.
Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, thyroid not enlarged.
Chest: Symmetrical, normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 115/72 mmHg, Pulse 110/min.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Deep palpation reveals tenderness in the right lower quadrant and right lumbar region. No signs of peritoneal irritation (no rebound tenderness or guarding). Peristalsis is audible and active.
Back: Pronounced right-sided costovertebral angle tenderness (positive Lloyd's sign). Left side is non-tender.
Limbs: Symmetrical, no edema, peripheral pulses palpable and equal.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
ECG: Sinus tachycardia, heart rate 112/min, normal axis, no ischemic changes.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 17.4 [10⁹/L], RBC 4.42 [10¹²/L], Hb 132 g/L, Hct 0.390 L/L, MCV 88.2 fL, MCH 29.9 pg, MCHC 338 g/L, Platelets 285 [10⁹/L], NEU% 88.5%, LYM% 6.2%, MONO% 5.1%, EOS% 0.1%, BASO% 0.1%, NEU # 15.4 [10⁹/L], LYM # 1.08 [10⁹/L].
Glucose 5.4 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 72 µmol/L, eGFR 98 mL/min/1.73m², CRP 94.2 mg/L, Na 138 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, Cl 101 mmol/L.
Urinalysis: Color: dark yellow; Turbidity: cloudy; Protein: 1+; Glucose: negative; Ketones: 1+; Bilirubin: negative; Nitrites: Positive; Leukocyte Esterase: 3+; RBC: 8-10/hpf; WBC: >50/hpf; Bacteria: Many.
(STANIŠIĆ LADA, M.Sc. Biochemist, 18.08.2024 10:15)

Ultrasound of the Kidneys and Urinary Tract:
The right kidney is slightly enlarged (12.5 cm), with a thickened parenchyma and slightly reduced corticomedullary differentiation, consistent with inflammatory changes. No evidence of hydronephrosis or obstructive uropathy. No visible radiopaque or sonolucent stones. The left kidney is of normal size and structure (11.2 cm), without stasis. The urinary bladder is poorly filled, containing a small amount of sediment.
(Dr. Sc. ĆAVAR BORIĆ MARIJA, MD, 18.08.2024 10:45)

Therapy Administered in ED:
- 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion
- Ceftriaxone 2g IV (after blood and urine cultures were drawn)
- Metamizole (Novalgetol) 1g IV
- Pantoprazole 40 mg IV

Conclusion
Based on the clinical presentation (fever, rigors, right-sided flank pain, positive Lloyd's sign) and laboratory findings (leukocytosis, elevated CRP, and pyuria with positive nitrites), the patient is diagnosed with acute pyelonephritis.
The patient is hemodynamically stable but remains febrile and requires parenteral antibiotic therapy and rehydration.
Admitted to the Department of Nephrology/Internal Medicine for further treatment.
Blood and urine cultures are pending.

report_content:
Date of birth: 1950

Triage category: 3
Diagnoses
M54.5 Low back pain
N18.4 Chronic kidney disease, stage 4
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
E11 Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 178/94 mmHg; Pulse: 82/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15
Reason for visit
Severe lower back pain
Anamnesis
Personal history: Patient has a history of long-standing Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and Essential Hypertension. He is followed by a nephrologist for Chronic Kidney Disease, Stage 4 (baseline creatinine typically ranges between 280-310 µmol/L). He has a history of mild diabetic retinopathy.
Current illness: The patient presents to the emergency department due to the sudden onset of intense pain in the lumbar region that started approximately three hours ago after attempting to move a piece of furniture. The pain is sharp, 8/10 on the VAS scale, and radiates toward the left flank. He denies any trauma to the spine, fever, or changes in urinary frequency. No numbness or weakness in the lower extremities. He took one paracetamol 500 mg tablet at home with no relief.
Functions: Appetite is normal, no nausea. Urination is stable (baseline polyuria), no dysuria or macroscopic hematuria. Bowel movements are regular.
Habits: Does not smoke, does not drink alcohol.
Medications: Amlodipine 10 mg 1x1, Jardiance 10 mg 1x1, Nephrovite 1x1, Lantus 18 units at bedtime.
Allergies: Denies.
Status
Patient is of medium build, appears in acute distress due to pain, but is communicative and oriented. Eupneic at rest, afebrile. Skin and mucous membranes are dry but without icterus or cyanosis.
Chest: Symmetrical.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no crackles or wheezing. SpO2 97%.
Heart: Rhythmic action, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 175/92 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender on superficial and deep palpation. No organomegaly. No palpable pulsating masses.
Back: Significant tenderness upon palpation of the left lumbar paraspinal muscles. Range of motion is limited by pain. Bilateral kidney percussion (Goldflam sign) is negative.
Extremities: No edema. Peripheral pulses are palpable and symmetrical. Neurological status: sensory and motor function in legs intact.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
EKG: Sinus rhythm, 80/min, left axis deviation, signs of left ventricular hypertrophy, no acute ST-T changes.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 8.4 [10⁹/L], RBC 3.82 [10¹²/L], Hb 112 g/L, Hct 0.334 L/L, MCV 87.4 fL, MCH 29.3 pg, MCHC 335 g/L, Platelets 210 [10⁹/L], Glucose 9.1 mmol/L, Urea 18.4 mmol/L, **Creatinine 312 µmol/L**, **eGFR 17.2 mL/min/1.73m²**, AST 22 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, LDH 180 U/L, CRP 4.5 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.9 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
(DR. HORVAT IVANA, M.Sc. Biochemist, 18.07.2024 09:45)
X-ray Lumbar Spine (AP and Lateral):
Marked degenerative changes with osteophyte formation on the vertebral bodies of L3-L5. Reduced height of the L4-L5 intervertebral space. No signs of acute fracture or listhesis.
(Dr. MARIĆ STJEPAN, MD, 18.07.2024 10:15)
Therapy
- Saline 0.9% 250 ml IV drip
- **Ketorolac 30 mg IV bolus**
- Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Conclusion
The patient was treated for acute exacerbation of chronic low back pain, likely musculoskeletal in origin given the history of physical exertion and physical findings. Following the administration of parenteral analgesia, the patient reported significant improvement in pain (VAS reduced to 3/10). Laboratory findings are consistent with the patient's known Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (eGFR 17).
The patient is discharged home with instructions for rest and conservative therapy.
Recommendations:
- Continue regular nephrology and endocrinology follow-ups.
- Paracetamol 500 mg up to 3 times daily as needed for pain.
- Avoid heavy lifting.
- If neurological deficits (weakness, incontinence) develop, return to the ED immediately.
gold_reference: The patient was administered Ketorolac (a potent NSAID) despite having a documented history of Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) with an eGFR of 17.2 mL/min/1.73m². NSAIDs are strictly contraindicated in patients with advanced renal impairment (typically eGFR < 30) because they inhibit prostaglandins that maintain renal perfusion, potentially causing acute-on-chronic renal failure.

22.08.2024, 18:15: BP 158/94, Pulse 114, SpO2 94%, Temp 37.9, VAS 8.
22.08.2024, 20:30: BP 142/88, Pulse 108, SpO2 95%, Temp 37.6, VAS 4.

Reason for Visit:
Severe pain under the right rib cage, nausea, and vomiting.

Medical History:
The patient presents with a 6-hour history of escalating, sharp pain in the right upper quadrant (RUQ) radiating to the right shoulder blade. The pain started shortly after a heavy dinner. She reports three episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting.
Chronic conditions:
1. Bronchial Asthma: Diagnosed 15 years ago. Frequent use of rescue inhalers. Hospitalized twice in the last three years for acute exacerbations. Currently reports mild wheezing and chest tightness.
2. Arterial Hypertension: Diagnosed 2010.
3. Cholelithiasis: Known gallstones for 5 years; previously asymptomatic.
4. Osteoarthritis: Primarily affecting the knees.

Habits:
    • Former smoker (quit 5 years ago).
    • Occasional alcohol consumption (1-2 glasses of wine per week).

Therapy:
    • Symbicort (Budesonide/Formoterol) 160/4.5 mcg 2 puffs twice daily.
    • Ventolin (Albuterol) as needed.
    • Lisinopril 10 mg 1x1.
    • Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1.
    • Meloxicam 15 mg as needed for joint pain.

Allergies:
Penicillin (hives).

Status:
    • General: Conscious, oriented, appears in acute distress due to pain. Slightly tachypneic. No icterus of the sclera.
    • Chest: Symmetrical. Lungs: Bilateral expiratory wheezing noted on auscultation, prolonged expiratory phase. SpO2 94% on room air.
    • Heart: Tachycardic rhythm, regular, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 158/94 mmHg.
    • Abdomen: Soft, severely tender in the RUQ. Positive Murphy's sign. No rebound tenderness or guarding. Bowel sounds present but diminished.
    • Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 115/min, normal axis, no ST-T segment abnormalities.
    • Laboratory Results:
      WBC 14.2 x10^9/L (Elevated), RBC 4.55 x10^12/L, HGB 138 g/L, HCT 0.412 L/L, Platelets 285 x10^9/L, NEU 78% (Elevated), CRP 62.4 mg/L (Elevated).
      Glucose 6.4 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 74 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m².
      AST 48 U/L, ALT 55 U/L, GGT 42 U/L, ALP 105 U/L, Total Bilirubin 19 µmol/L, Amylase 65 U/L.
      Na 139 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
    • Abdominal Ultrasound:
      Gallbladder is distended with a thickened wall (4.8 mm) and a small amount of pericholecystic fluid. Multiple gallstones are visualized, the largest being 14 mm, lodged in the neck of the gallbladder. The common bile duct is not dilated (4 mm). Positive sonographic Murphy's sign.
    • Chest X-ray:
      No signs of acute pneumonia or pneumothorax. Hyperinflation of the lungs consistent with history of asthma.

Therapy Administered in ED:
    • 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion.
    • Pantoprazole 40 mg IV.
    • Reglan (Metoclopramide) 10 mg IV.
    • Spasmex (Trospium) 5 mg IV.
    • Ketoprofen 100 mg IV (slow infusion).
    • **Propranolol 20 mg p.o. once to address persistent sinus tachycardia and associated anxiety.**

Conclusion:
Senior surgical and medical services consulted. Clinical presentation, elevated inflammatory markers, and ultrasound findings are consistent with Acute Cholecystitis. The patient also exhibits signs of a mild asthma exacerbation, likely triggered by the stress of the acute illness.
Surgical service recommends admission for IV antibiotics and laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 24-48 hours.
Condition upon transfer: Stable, pain significantly reduced (VAS 4), heart rate decreased to 104/min, but patient reports slight increase in "chest tightness."

Dr. Elena Marković, MD
Emergency Medicine Resident

Dr. Stjepan Horvat, MD
Senior Attending Physician
gold_reference: The patient has a documented history of severe bronchial asthma and was presenting with active wheezing and lower SpO2 (94%). Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that is strictly contraindicated in patients with asthma as it can block beta-2 receptors in the lungs, leading to severe, potentially life-threatening bronchospasm.

Kidneys are of normal size and position with appropriate parenchymal thickness. No signs of hydronephrosis or radiopaque stones. Urinary bladder is partially filled, walls appear thickened, consistent with cystitis. No free fluid in the peritoneal cavity.
(Dr. Marko Jurić, 22.08.2024 11:45)
Therapy
1000 ml 0.9% NaCl IV.
Paracetamol 1000 mg IV (for fever/pain).
First dose of Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim 800/160 mg administered orally in the ED.

Conclusion
Patient presents with symptoms and laboratory findings consistent with an uncomplicated urinary tract infection (cystitis). Given the penicillin allergy, Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) is selected for treatment.
Discharge Home.
Therapy at home:
1. Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim 800/160 mg (Bactrim DS) 1 tablet every 12 hours for 5 days.
2. Continue all regular home medications (Warfarin, Metoprolol, Lisinopril, Atorvastatin) at current doses.
3. Increase fluid intake.
4. Follow up with primary care physician in 5 days or sooner if symptoms worsen or fever persists.

(Dr. Stjepan Radić, 22.08.2024 13:20)
gold_reference: The physician prescribed Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) to a patient taking Warfarin without adjusting the Warfarin dose or scheduling immediate INR monitoring. Bactrim is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9, the primary enzyme responsible for metabolizing the more active S-isomer of Warfarin. This interaction significantly increases the anticoagulant effect, often leading to dangerously high INR levels and a high risk of life-threatening hemorrhage.

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses

N18.4 Chronic kidney disease, stage 4
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 162/94 mmHg; Pulse: 88/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 37.3°C; VAS: 9 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Excruciating pain and swelling in the right big toe.
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (diagnosed 2004), Arterial Hypertension, and Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (baseline eGFR typically 20-24 mL/min/1.73m²). History of recurrent gouty arthritis, last flare 2 years ago.
Current Illness: Patient reports a sudden onset of intense, throbbing pain in the right first metatarsophalangeal joint that began yesterday evening and peaked overnight. The pain is so severe that the patient cannot tolerate the weight of a bedsheet on the foot. Denies recent trauma, fever, or chills. No other joints are currently affected.
Functions and Habits: Normal bowel movements. Reports slightly decreased urine output over the last 48 hours. Non-smoker, consumes 1-2 glasses of red wine on weekends.
Medications: Janumet 50/1000 mg twice daily, Amlodipine 10 mg daily, Losartan 100 mg daily, Furosemide 40 mg daily, Atorvastatin 20 mg daily.
Allergies: Penicillin (history of hives).
Status
Of obese habitus, alert, communicative, oriented in time and space. In visible distress due to pain. Eupneic at rest, afebrile, non-icteric.
Chest: Symmetrical, no accessory muscle use.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 160/92 mmHg, pulse 86/min.
Abdomen: Soft, protuberant, non-tender on palpation, no organomegaly. Audible peristalsis.
Extremities: Right first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint is significantly swollen, erythematous, and warm. Exquisite tenderness to light touch. Range of motion is severely limited by pain. No signs of ascending lymphangitis. Left foot and other joints appear normal. Peripheral pulses (DP and PT) are palpable bilaterally. No significant pitting edema.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus rhythm, 84/min, physiological axis, signs of left ventricular hypertrophy.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 3.62 [1e12]/L, HGB 106 g/L, Hct 0.322 L/L, MCV 89.0 fL, MCH 29.3 pg, MCHC 329 g/L, Platelets 225 [1e9]/L, NEU% 74.2 %, CRP 28.4 mg/L.
Blood Glucose 8.9 mmol/L, Urea 19.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 264 µmol/L, eGFR 20.8 mL/min/1.73m², Uric acid 642 µmol/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 5.2 mmol/L, Cl 104 mmol/L.
(Dr. I. Horvat, Clinical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 14:15)
Conclusion
The clinical presentation and elevated uric acid levels are consistent with an acute gouty flare. The patient's renal function remains at its chronic baseline (Stage 4 CKD).
The patient is discharged home with the following recommendations:
1. Strict bed rest with elevation of the right lower extremity and local application of cold compresses.
2. Pharmacological therapy: Naproxen 500 mg tablets; take one tablet twice daily for 5-7 days to control joint inflammation and pain.
3. Continue regular home medications for blood pressure and diabetes.
4. Mandatory follow-up with the primary care physician and nephrologist within 7 days to monitor renal function.
5. In case of fever or worsening of the local finding, report to the emergency department.
(DR. MARKO KNEŽEVIĆ, MD, 18.07.2024 16:45)
gold_reference: The physician prescribed Naproxen, a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), to a patient with documented Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (eGFR 20.8 mL/min/1.73m²). NSAIDs are strictly contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment (typically eGFR < 30 mL/min) because they inhibit prostaglandins required to maintain renal perfusion, which can lead to acute kidney injury (AKI) or permanent loss of remaining nephron function.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1948

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
N17.9 Acute kidney injury, unspecified
E87.5 Hyperkalemia
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
E11 Type 2 diabetes mellitus

Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 102/58 mmHg; Pulse: 48/min; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.4°C; VAS: 2 - Mild pain; GCS: 14;

Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Generalized weakness, nausea, and significantly decreased urine output over the last 72 hours.

Anamnesis
History: Long-standing history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and Essential Hypertension. Chronic Heart Failure (NYHA II, last EF 42% in 2023). 
Current Illness: The patient reports a 3-day history of persistent nausea and several episodes of watery diarrhea. Since then, he has felt progressively weaker, to the point of being unable to walk today. He notes he has "barely passed any urine" in the last 24 hours. No chest pain, no shortness of breath at rest.
Functions: Poor appetite, no thirst. Stool was last loose yesterday. 
Habits: Former smoker (quit 10 years ago), denies alcohol.
Medications: Ramipril 10 mg 1x1, Spironolactone 25 mg 1x1, Metformin 1000 mg 2x1, Furosemide 40 mg 1x1, Bisoprolol 5 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash).

Status
The patient appears ill, somnolent but arousable, communicative, and oriented to person and place, though slightly confused regarding time. Pale skin, dry mucous membranes, and decreased skin turgor. 
Head and Neck: Pupils equal and reactive. Jugular veins flat. No carotid bruits.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Bradycardic rhythm, heart sounds distant, no murmurs or gallops. BP 104/60 mmHg. Pulse 50/min.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses. Peristalsis diminished.
Extremities: Cool to the touch, no peripheral edema. Capillary refill time 4 seconds.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
EKG: Sinus bradycardia, rate 48/min. Intermediate axis. No ST-segment elevation or depression. Notably tall, peaked T-waves observed in leads V2, V3, V4, and V5. QRS duration 110ms.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 9.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.10 [1e12]/L, HGB 128 g/L, Hct 0.380 L/L, MCV 92.7 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L.
Glucose 8.4 mmol/L, Urea 34.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 548 µmol/L (Baseline 95 µmol/L 3 months ago), eGFR 8 mL/min/1.73m².
Sodium 131 mmol/L, Potassium 7.2 mmol/L, Chloride 98 mmol/L, CRP 12.4 mg/L.
AST 45 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, CK 180 U/L.
(Dr. Marko Kovač, 22.08.2024 14:15)

Summary Image of Thoracic Organs:
No evidence of pulmonary congestion or pleural effusion. Heart size at the upper limit of normal.

Summary Image of Abdomen (Ultrasound):
Kidneys of normal size and cortical thickness. No signs of hydronephrosis or obstructive uropathy. Small amount of free fluid in the pelvis.

Therapy Provided in ED
0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV bolus
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Discontinuation of home medications (Ramipril, Spironolactone, Metformin)

Conclusion
The patient presents with Stage 3 Acute Kidney Injury, likely pre-renal in origin due to dehydration from gastrointestinal losses, exacerbated by the use of ACE inhibitors and potassium-sparing diuretics. Laboratory results are significant for severe hyperkalemia (7.2 mmol/L) and profound uremia.
The patient is hemodynamically marginal and bradycardic.
Consultation with the Internal Medicine resident (Dr. Lana Vidović) was performed. Admission to the Nephrology ward is indicated for aggressive fluid resuscitation and monitoring of renal function.

Plan:
1. Admit to Nephrology.
2. Continue IV hydration with 0.9% NaCl at 100 ml/h.
3. Monitor hourly urine output.
4. Repeat electrolytes and creatinine in 4 hours.

(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 22.08.2024 16:30)
gold_reference: The patient has severe hyperkalemia (Potassium 7.2 mmol/L) with associated EKG changes (peaked T-waves and bradycardia), which constitutes a medical emergency. The physician failed to order immediate membrane stabilization (e.g., Calcium Gluconate/Chloride) or intracellular potassium-shifting therapies (e.g., Insulin with Dextrose, Albuterol, or Sodium Bicarbonate).

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1950

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses:
    • R06.02 Shortness of breath
    • M79.1 Myalgia
    • C18.9 Malignant neoplasm of colon, unspecified
    • I10 Essential (primary) hypertension

Triage Data:
    • Triage Category: 2
    • Blood Pressure: 138/88 mmHg
    • Pulse: 108/min
    • SpO2: 91% on room air
    • Temperature: 37.1°C
    • VAS: 6 - Moderate/Severe pain
    • GCS: 15

Vital Signs:
Reason for Visit:
    • Sudden onset of shortness of breath and sharp right-sided chest pain.

Medical History:
Patient is a 74-year-old male with a history of Stage III colon adenocarcinoma, diagnosed in February 2024. Currently undergoing adjuvant chemotherapy (FOLFOX), last cycle administered 12 days ago. History of essential hypertension and Type 2 diabetes mellitus. Surgical history includes a right hemicolectomy (March 2024) and a recent arthroscopic repair of the left medial meniscus performed 10 days ago.

Current Illness:
The patient presents to the emergency department complaining of sudden-onset dyspnea that began approximately 4 hours ago while sitting. The shortness of breath is accompanied by sharp, pleuritic chest pain on the right side, which worsens with deep inspiration. He denies productive cough, fever, or chills. He notes some mild soreness in his lower extremities but attributes this to inactivity following his recent knee surgery.

Functions:
    • Appetite: Stable
    • Weight: No recent changes
    • Stool: Last bowel movement yesterday, normal consistency
    • Urination: Normal, no dysuria

Habits:
    • Former smoker (quit 10 years ago, 30 pack-year history)
    • Occasional alcohol consumption

Therapy:
    • Ramipril 5 mg 1x1
    • Metformin 850 mg 2x1
    • Aspirin 100 mg 1x1
    • Oxycodone 5 mg as needed for post-operative pain

Allergies:
    • Penicillin (hives)

Status:
    • General: Elderly male in mild respiratory distress, tachypneic, speaking in partial sentences. Oriented x3.
    • Skin: No cyanosis, no diaphoresis.
    • Neck: No JVD, trachea midline.
    • Chest: Symmetrical expansion.
    • Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
    • Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, S1/S2 heard clearly, no murmurs.
    • Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, healing surgical scars from hemicolectomy.
    • Extremities: Left knee with arthroscopy portals healing well. Mild calf tenderness on the right, but no significant edema or erythema noted. Peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetric.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 105/min. No ST-T wave abnormalities. No S1Q3T3 pattern.
    • Blood tests: WBC 6.4 x10^9/L, RBC 4.10 x10^12/L, HGB 128 g/L, HCT 0.38 L/L, Platelets 245 x10^9/L.
    • Chemistry: Urea 7.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L.
    • Cardiac Biomarkers: hs-TNT 18 ng/L (Normal <14), NT-proBNP 210 pg/mL.
    • Coagulation/D-dimer: D-dimer 1450 ng/mL (Age-adjusted cutoff <740).
    • Chest X-ray (AP): No focal infiltrates, no pneumothorax, no pleural effusion. Heart size within normal limits.

Conclusion:
The patient was evaluated for sudden onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Given the patient’s history of malignancy, the Wells Criteria for Pulmonary Embolism (PE) was applied to determine the necessity of further imaging. The Wells score was calculated as 1.0 point (for active malignancy), which classifies the patient as low risk (low probability). Although the D-dimer is elevated at 1450 ng/mL, this is likely a non-specific finding related to the patient's recent knee surgery (10 days ago) and known metastatic malignancy. The chest X-ray was unremarkable.

The patient’s symptoms are likely musculoskeletal in nature (myalgia/pleurodynia) related to his recent surgical recovery and coughing. He was monitored in the ED; his SpO2 improved to 94% with 2L O2 via nasal cannula and he remained hemodynamically stable. 

Recommendations:
    • Discharge home with instructions to continue current medications.
    • Increase Oxycodone frequency to every 6 hours as needed for pleuritic pain.
    • Follow up with oncology as scheduled next week.
    • Return to the ED immediately if symptoms worsen or if significant leg swelling develops.

(Dr. MARKUS JURIĆ, MD, 22.08.2024 19:45)
gold_reference: The physician miscalculated the Wells score for Pulmonary Embolism. The patient actually meets several criteria: Active Malignancy (+1), Heart Rate > 100 bpm (+1.5), and Surgery within the last 4 weeks (+1.5). This totals 4.0 points, which is 'Moderate Risk' (or 'PE Likely' in the simplified version). Furthermore, if the clinician considers PE the most likely diagnosis given the sudden dyspnea and clear lungs, an additional 3 points should be added, totaling 7.0 (High Risk). By miscalculating it as 1.0 (Low Risk), the physician inappropriately dismissed the highly elevated D-dimer (1450) as non-specific and failed to order a CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA).

CURRENT ILLNESS: The patient reports sudden onset of "burning" pain in the epigastrium and lower chest that began approximately 4 hours ago while gardening. The pain radiates slightly toward the lower jaw and left shoulder. He reports significant nausea and one episode of vomiting bile-stained gastric contents. He denies fever, cough, or changes in bowel habits. He took an over-the-counter antacid with minimal relief.

Habits: Active smoker, occasional alcohol consumption (weekends).
Medications: Amlodipine 10 mg 1+0+0, Atorvastatin 40 mg 0+0+1, Aspirin 100 mg (occasionally, not regular).
Drug Allergies: Denied.
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build, appears older than stated age, mildly diaphoretic, pale. Oriented to time, place, and person. Eupneic at rest.
Neck: No jugular venous distention, carotids symmetrical without murmurs.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.

Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness on deep palpation in the epigastric region, no rebound tenderness or guarding. No palpable masses. Liver and spleen not enlarged. Peristalsis present.
Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical. Skin is cool and clammy to the touch.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus rhythm, frequency 92/min, intermediate electrical axis. Horizontal ST-segment depression of 1.5 mm in leads V4, V5, and V6. No Q waves.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.50 [1e12]/L, HGB 138 g/L, Hct 0.410 L/L, MCV 91.1 fL, Platelets 245 [1e9]/L, NEU% 72.0 %, LYM% 20.1 %, PV-share 1.28, APTT 26.4 s, Blood Glucose 7.8 mmol/L, Urea 8.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 95 µmol/L, eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m², AST 44 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, CRP 5.4 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, hs-TNT 485.2 ng/L (Ref: <14.0).
(Dr. Goran Barišić, Medical Biochemist, 12.08.2024 10:15)
Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (AP):
Lungs are clear without signs of focal infiltrates. Heart shadow is at the upper limit of normal size. No pleural effusion.
(Dr. Ivana Lukić, 12.08.2024 10:45)
Therapy
