**Triage Category:** 3  

---

## Diagnoses
- **J18.9** Pneumonia, unspecified organism  
- **R06.02** Shortness of breath (symptom)  

---

## Triage Data
**Triage Cat:** 3  
**Blood Pressure:** 146/88 mmHg  
**Pulse:** 104/min  
**SpO₂:** 92% (room air)  
**Resp:** 22/min  
**Temp:** 38.2 °C  
**VAS:** 3/10 (pleuritic chest discomfort with coughing)  
**GCS:** 15  

---

## Reason for Arrival
Fever, productive cough, and shortness of breath for 3 days.

---

## Anamnesis (History)
**Present Illness:**  
Patient reports 3 days of fever and chills, productive cough with yellow sputum, progressive dyspnea on exertion, and mild right-sided pleuritic chest pain when coughing. No syncope. Denies hemoptysis. No recent travel. Wife had “a cold” one week ago.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Hypertension  
- Hyperlipidemia  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet + metformin)  
- No known chronic lung disease

**Medications (home):**  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1×1  
- Metformin 1000 mg 2×1  
- **Simvastatin 40 mg 1×1 (evening)**  
- Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg 1×1  

**Allergies:** Denies drug allergies.

**Habits:** Former smoker (25 pack-years), quit 6 years ago. Alcohol occasionally.

**Functions:** Reduced appetite. Urination normal. No vomiting/diarrhea.

---

## Physical Examination
**General:** Ill-appearing but cooperative, oriented, speaking in full sentences, mildly tachypneic at rest. Skin warm, well perfused.

**HEENT:** Oropharynx mildly injected, no exudate. No neck stiffness.

**Chest/Lungs:** Chest expansion symmetric. Auscultation: decreased breath sounds and crackles at right lower lung field; no wheeze.  
SpO₂ improves to 95% on 2 L/min nasal cannula.

**Heart:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic. No murmurs.

**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.

**Extremities:** No peripheral edema. Calves soft, non-tender.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG
- Sinus tachycardia ~102/min  
- No acute ST elevation/depression, no dynamic ischemic changes

### Imaging
**Chest X-ray (PA + lateral):**  
- Patchy alveolar opacity/consolidation in **right lower lobe**  
- No pleural effusion  
- Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits  
**Impression:** Findings consistent with right lower lobe pneumonia.

### Laboratory Results (06.02.2026 19:10)
**CBC:**  
- WBC **13.8 ×10⁹/L** (↑)  
- RBC 4.72 ×10¹²/L  
- HGB 141 g/L  
- Hct 0.417 L/L  
- MCV 88.3 fL  
- Platelets 232 ×10⁹/L  
- Neutrophils 82% (absolute 11.3 ×10⁹/L) (↑)  

**Inflammation:**  
- CRP **96 mg/L** (↑)  
- Procalcitonin **0.42 ng/mL** (mildly ↑)

**Chemistry:**  
- Glucose 9.8 mmol/L (↑)  
- Urea 7.2 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 92 µmol/L (eGFR 79 mL/min/1.73m²)  
- Na 137 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, Cl 101 mmol/L  
- AST 24 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, GGT 22 U/L  
- Lactate 1.4 mmol/L  

**Cardiac markers:**  
- hs-Troponin T 7 ng/L (within reference, no rise on repeat at 2h)  

**Microbiology:**  
- SARS‑CoV‑2/Influenza A/B rapid PCR: negative  
- Blood cultures drawn ×2 (pending)

---

## ED Course / Observation
Oxygen via nasal cannula 2 L/min with improvement in SpO₂ to 95–96%. After antipyretic and fluids, patient reports easier breathing. Hemodynamically stable throughout observation. CURB‑65 calculated as 1 (age ≥65), suitable for outpatient treatment with close follow-up given clinical stability and good support at home.

---

## Therapy in ED
- Oxygen 2 L/min via nasal cannula (weaned off before discharge; SpO₂ 94–95% on room air)  
- Paracetamol 1 g PO  
- 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV  
- First antibiotic dose given in ED: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV

---

## Discharge Therapy / Prescriptions
- **Clarithromycin 500 mg PO 2×1 for 7 days**  
- Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125 mg PO 2×1 for 7 days  
- Paracetamol 1 g PO as needed for fever (max 3 g/day)  
- Encourage oral hydration, rest

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture, labs (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and chest X-ray are consistent with community-acquired right lower lobe pneumonia. No evidence of acute coronary syndrome. Patient improved during ED observation, maintains adequate oxygenation on room air, and is safe for discharge with oral antibiotics and strict return precautions.

**Recommendations:**
- Follow up with family physician within 24–48 hours.
- Return immediately for worsening shortness of breath, persistent fever >48 hours after antibiotics, chest pain at rest, confusion, or SpO₂ <92%.
- Review blood culture results with primary care when available.

**Physician:** Dr. I. Novak  
**Time:** 06.02.2026, 22:05  

---
gold_reference: Clarithromycin is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor and markedly increases simvastatin levels, raising the risk of severe myopathy/rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury. The report prescribes clarithromycin without recognizing or addressing this dangerous interaction.

- IV fluids: **500 mL 0.9% NaCl** over 60 minutes (oral intake reduced; no signs of overload)  
- Antipyretic: **Paracetamol 1 g PO** (fever and discomfort)  
- Empiric antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia with penicillin allergy history (non-anaphylactic):  
  - **Ceftriaxone 2 g IV** (administered in ED; tolerated without reaction)  
  - **Azithromycin 500 mg PO** (first dose in ED)

Reassessment after therapy (approx. 2 hours):
- Temp 37.8 °C, HR 98/min, BP 116/72 mmHg  
- RR 20/min  
- SpO₂ 95–96% on 2 L/min; drops to 92–93% when trialed off oxygen  
- Dyspnea improved but still present with exertion

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture, labs (leukocytosis with neutrophilia, high CRP), hypoxemia, and CXR findings are consistent with **right lower lobe community-acquired pneumonia**. Due to **ongoing oxygen requirement** and systemic features (fever, tachypnea), patient requires **inpatient treatment and observation**.

**Plan:**
- **Admit to Internal Medicine/Pulmonology ward** for continued oxygen therapy, IV/PO antibiotics, and monitoring.  
- Continue ceftriaxone + azithromycin per local CAP protocol; reassess after 48–72 h or earlier if deterioration.  
- Encourage oral fluids as tolerated; IV fluids as needed based on intake/output and vitals.  
- Consider sputum culture if adequate sample; blood cultures if recurrent fever/clinical worsening.  
- DVT prophylaxis per ward protocol if reduced mobility.

**Return precautions communicated:** worsening dyspnea, chest pain at rest, confusion, persistent hypotension, inability to maintain SpO₂, or any allergic reaction signs.

*(ED Physician: Dr. Marko Jurić, 06.02.2026 21:02)*

Pain intensity initially 7/10, associated with **mild diaphoresis** and **nausea**, no vomiting. Pain radiated to the **left shoulder and jaw**. Symptoms partially improved with rest but did not fully resolve. At presentation pain 3–4/10.

**HEENT:** No icterus, mucosa moist.  
**Neck:** No JVD, no carotid bruits.  
**Lungs:** Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles.  
**Cardiac:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic initially; normal S1/S2, no murmurs/rubs.

### Laboratory Results (repeat troponin)

**Findings:**

*(Radiologist: Dr. N. Kovač, 06.02.2026 10:12)*

## ED Treatment / Therapy
**Monitoring:** Continuous ECG monitoring, IV access x1, oxygen not required (SpO₂ stable on room air).

**Medications administered in ED:**  
- **Aspirin 300 mg PO (chewed) once**  
- **Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL**, repeated once after 5 minutes (pain reduced to 1–2/10; BP remained stable)  
- **Metoprolol 5 mg IV** (given for tachycardia and ischemia; HR decreased to 80–90/min; no hypotension)

## Clinical Course / Assessment
Presentation and ECG changes are consistent with **non–ST-elevation acute coronary syndrome**. Serial hs-troponin values show **no dynamic rise**, supporting **unstable angina** rather than NSTEMI at this time; however, due to ischemic ECG changes and typical symptoms with risk factors, patient requires inpatient cardiology evaluation and further management (possible early invasive strategy).

## Conclusion / Disposition
**Final ED Impression:** **Acute coronary syndrome without ST elevation – likely unstable angina.**  
**Disposition:** **Hospital admission to Cardiology (monitored bed).**  
**Plan on admission:** Continue anticoagulation and anti-ischemic therapy, repeat ECGs and troponins as per protocol, echocardiography, consider coronary angiography based on cardiology assessment.

**Patient informed** about suspected ACS, need for admission and monitoring; agrees.

report_content:
**Patient:** 2  
**MBOO:** 247531906

**Date of Birth:** 1988 (36 years)

---

## Triage Category: 3

## Diagnoses (ED)
- R07.4 Chest pain, unspecified  
- F41.1 Generalized anxiety disorder (suspected/panic component)  
- M94.0 Tietze syndrome (costochondritis), suspected  

---

## Triage Data
**Triage Cat:** 3  
**Blood Pressure:** 126/78 mmHg  
**Pulse:** 112/min  
**Respiratory Rate:** 22/min  
**SpO₂:** 93% on room air (improves to 97% on 2 L/min O₂ via nasal cannula)  
**Temperature:** 36.8 °C  
**VAS:** 6/10 (moderate–severe)  
**GCS:** 15  

---

## Anamnesis

**History:**  
No prior myocardial infarction. No known thrombophilia. Mild asthma in childhood (no inhalers currently). No prior surgeries.

**Current illness:**  
Sudden onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain starting ~6 hours prior to arrival, worse with deep inspiration and coughing. Associated shortness of breath and a sense of “heart racing.” Denies fever, productive cough, hemoptysis, syncope. Reports mild right calf soreness for 2–3 days but “thought it was a strain.”

**Risk factors / context:**  
Returned 2 days ago from a long-haul flight (approximately 10 hours). Uses combined oral contraceptive pill. No pregnancy. No recent trauma.

**Functions:**  
Appetite reduced due to pain. No nausea/vomiting. Normal bowel movements. Urination normal.

**Habits:**  
Non-smoker. Alcohol occasionally. No illicit drug use.

**Medications:**  
Combined oral contraceptive (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel). Occasional ibuprofen.

**Drug allergies:**  
Penicillin (urticarial rash).

---

## Status (Physical Exam)
**General:** Alert, anxious-appearing, speaking in full sentences but mildly dyspneic. Afebrile.  
**Skin/mucosa:** Warm, well perfused. No cyanosis.  
**Neck:** No JVD.  
**Lungs:** Breath sounds vesicular bilaterally; no wheeze, no crackles. Pain increased on deep inspiration.  
**Heart:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic; no murmurs.  
**Chest wall:** Localized tenderness over right costochondral junctions (3rd–4th ribs), pain reproduced by palpation.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender, no guarding.  
**Extremities:** No visible edema. Right calf mildly tender to deep palpation, no erythema/warmth noted; peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric.  
**Neuro:** No focal deficit.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG
**Sinus tachycardia** 110–115/min, normal axis, no ST elevation/depression, QTc 432 ms.

### Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (Chest X-ray), 1 Direction
**Findings and Opinion:**  
AP portable: Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits. No focal consolidation, no pleural effusion, no pneumothorax.  
**Conclusion:** No acute cardiopulmonary radiographic abnormality.  
(Dr. Ivana Kovač, 06.02.2026 19:42)

---

## Laboratory Results (06.02.2026 19:20)
**CBC:**  
- WBC 9.8 [1e9]/L  
- RBC 4.62 [1e12]/L  
- HGB 139 g/L  
- Hct 0.412 L/L  
- MCV 89.2 fL  
- Platelets 238 [1e9]/L  

**Inflammation:**  
- CRP 2.1 mg/L  

**Biochemistry:**  
- Urea 5.4 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 74 µmol/L (eGFR 103 mL/min/1.73m²)  
- Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L  
- Glucose 5.6 mmol/L  

**Cardiac markers:**  
- hs-Troponin T 6 ng/L (within reference)  

**Coagulation / thrombosis marker:**  
- **D-dimer 1.86 mg/L FEU (elevated)**  

**Blood gas (venous):**  
- pH 7.43, pCO₂ 35 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 23 mmol/L, lactate 1.2 mmol/L

(Lab signed: Medical biochemist, 06.02.2026 19:35)

---

## Therapy (ED)
- Oxygen 2 L/min via nasal cannula (target SpO₂ > 95%)  
- Ibuprofen 600 mg PO  
- Pantoprazole 40 mg PO (gastric protection with NSAID)  
- Diazepam 2 mg PO (for anxiety component, single dose)  
- Observation with repeat vitals over ~2.5 hours  

---

## Course in ED / Reassessment
After analgesia, pain decreased to VAS 3–4/10. SpO₂ stable 96–97% on room air at rest, HR ~96/min. Patient reports improved breathing.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
**Assessment:** Chest pain is most consistent with **costochondritis with anxiety-related hyperventilation**, given reproducible chest wall tenderness, normal ECG, normal troponin, and unremarkable chest X-ray.  

**Plan:** Discharge home. Continue ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours as needed with food for 3 days. Rest, hydration. Follow up with primary care physician within 2–3 days.

**Return precautions:** Worsening shortness of breath, persistent tachycardia, syncope, hemoptysis, increasing chest pain, new leg swelling.

**Discharge statement (contains the clinical error):**  
> “Despite elevated D-dimer, there is no indication for further imaging; pulmonary embolism is unlikely and does not require CT pulmonary angiography at this time.”

(ED physician: 06.02.2026 22:05)

---
gold_reference: The patient has multiple PE risk features (recent long-haul flight, estrogen-containing OCP), symptoms compatible with PE (sudden pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, initial borderline hypoxemia), and an elevated D-dimer. Declaring PE unlikely without applying a validated pretest probability tool (e.g., Wells/YEARS/PERC) and without appropriate imaging can miss a potentially life-threatening diagnosis.

- Reports unsteadiness and “pulling to the left” when trying to walk.

Laboratory Findings (reported 06.02.2026, 13:42):

  - WBC: 8.9 ×10⁹/L
  - Hemoglobin: 132 g/L
  - Platelets: 238 ×10⁹/L

  - Urea: 6.8 mmol/L
  - Creatinine: 78 µmol/L
  - eGFR: 72 mL/min/1.73 m²
- Glucose (serum): 10.6 mmol/L
- Liver enzymes: within normal limits
- Coagulation:
  - INR: 1.0
  - aPTT: 29 s
- hs-Troponin T: 8 ng/L (no dynamic rise planned due to non-cardiac presentation)

Imaging:
1) Non-contrast CT head (06.02.2026, 14:25)  
   - No acute intracranial hemorrhage.  
   - No mass effect or midline shift.  
   - Chronic microangiopathic changes.  
   Radiology impression: No acute intracranial bleeding; early ischemic changes may not be visible.

2) Chest X-ray (AP)  
   - No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality.

No vascular imaging (CTA/MRA) performed.

________________________________________  
Therapy in ED
- IV access, monitoring
- 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV
- Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
- Ketorolac 15 mg IV
- Labetalol 10 mg IV (for severe hypertension), with BP improvement
- Observation ~4 hours: nausea improved, headache decreased to VAS 3/10; vertigo persisted but patient able to sit up

________________________________________  
Conclusion / Disposition
- Working diagnosis documented as migraine/vestibular syndrome with vomiting and hypertensive urgency; CT head negative for hemorrhage.
- Discharged home with spouse.
- Discharge medications: ondansetron ODT PRN, advised acetaminophen PRN.

**ED Documentation Note (clinical error text):**  
> “Neurological exam without focal deficit; symptoms most consistent with vestibular migraine. No indication for stroke pathway given normal CT head.”

________________________________________
gold_reference: This presentation (sudden onset vertigo + severe occipital headache + vomiting + gait inability + nystagmus + left dysmetria, with major vascular risk factors and possible transient slurred speech) is concerning for posterior circulation stroke (e.g., cerebellar infarct) or TIA. A normal non-contrast CT head does not exclude early ischemic stroke, especially in the posterior fossa. Documenting 'no indication for stroke pathway' based on CT alone overlooks an important diagnosis.

**Emergency Department Report (Synthetic, Realistic)**

## Triage

---

## Reason for Admission
Suspected acute pyelonephritis with systemic inflammatory response.

---

## Anamnesis

**History of Present Illness:**  
2 days of burning urination and urinary frequency. Since last night, chills and fever, with increasing right-sided flank pain radiating to the groin. Nausea without vomiting. No chest pain or shortness of breath. No known kidney stones in the past. Took paracetamol at home with partial relief.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Mechanical mitral valve replacement (2016)  
- Chronic atrial fibrillation  
- Hypertension  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus  
- Stage 3 chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine ~120 µmol/L per patient card)  
- Hyperlipidemia

**Surgical History:** Mechanical valve (2016), cholecystectomy (2008)

**Epidemiological Anamnesis:** Sporadic, no travel, no known sick contacts.

**Habits:** Non-smoker, rare alcohol.

**Home Medications:**  
- **Warfarin** variable dosing (target INR 2.5–3.5 for mechanical valve per patient)  
- Bisoprolol 5 mg 1×1  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1×1  
- Metformin 500 mg 2×1  
- Atorvastatin 20 mg 1×1  
- Pantoprazole 20 mg 1×1

**Allergies:** Denies drug allergies.

---

## Physical Examination (11:05)

**General:** Ill-appearing, febrile, oriented, mildly dehydrated.  
**Skin/Mucosa:** Warm, dry mucosa.  
**Cardio:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic; mechanical click audible; no new murmurs. Peripheral pulses present.  
**Respiratory:** Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles/wheezes.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness, no guarding.  
**Renal angle:** Marked right costovertebral angle tenderness.  
**Extremities:** No edema, no calf tenderness.  
**Neuro:** GCS 15, no focal deficit.

---

## Vital Signs (ED course)

| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | RR (/min) | Temp (°C) | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 06.02.2026 10:46 | 104/62 | 112 | 95 | 20 | 38.6 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 12:10 | 110/66 | 104 | 96 | 18 | 38.0 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 14:30 | 116/70 | 92 | 97 | 16 | 37.6 | 15 |

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### Point-of-care / Urinalysis (11:20)
- Leukocyte esterase: **+++**
- Nitrite: **positive**
- Blood: **+**
- Protein: trace
- Ketones: negative
- Glucose: negative
- Specific gravity: 1.020

### Bloodwork (11:25)
**CBC**
- WBC **15.8 x10⁹/L** (NEU 88%)  
- HGB 122 g/L  
- Hct 0.368 L/L  
- Platelets 214 x10⁹/L

**Inflammation / Sepsis**
- CRP **164 mg/L**

- Urea **12.8 mmol/L**

- K 4.6 mmol/L  
- Cl 101 mmol/L  
- HCO₃⁻ 21 mmol/L  
- Glucose 11.2 mmol/L

**Liver**
- AST 28 U/L, ALT 21 U/L, ALP 102 U/L, Bilirubin total 14 µmol/L

**Coagulation**
- **INR 3.1**  
- APTT 31 s

**Blood cultures:** 2 sets drawn prior to antibiotics (11:35)

### ECG (11:10)
Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response ~105/min, no acute ischemic changes.

---

## Imaging

### Renal/Bladder Ultrasound (12:40)
**Findings:**  
- Right kidney: mild pelvic dilatation, no clear obstructing stone visualized  
- Left kidney: no hydronephrosis  
- Bladder: moderate residual urine, no mass seen

**Impression:** Mild right-sided pelviectasis—cannot exclude early/partial obstruction; correlate clinically.

### CT Abdomen/Pelvis without contrast (14:05) *(performed due to CKD and to evaluate obstruction/stone)*
**Findings:**  
- No ureteral calculus identified  
- Mild right perinephric fat stranding  
- No hydronephrosis of significance  
- No intra-abdominal abscess

**Impression:** Imaging supports **right acute pyelonephritis** without obstructing stone.

---

## ED Treatment / Therapy

- IV access x2, monitoring  
- **0.9% NaCl 1,000 mL IV** over 2 hours, then reassess  
- **Paracetamol 1 g IV**  
- **Ondansetron 4 mg IV**  
- **Ceftriaxone 2 g IV** (first dose in ED at 12:00)  
- Pain control: **Metamizole 1 g IV** (13:10)  
- Discharge antibiotic plan documented: **Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800 mg PO, 1 tablet twice daily for 10 days** (start next morning), pending culture results  
- Continue home medications as usual (including **warfarin**)

---

## Clinical Course
After fluids and antipyretics, temperature and tachycardia improved. Blood pressure remained stable. Pain reduced to 3/10. No vomiting; tolerating oral intake. CT excluded obstruction/stone requiring urgent urology intervention.

---

## Disposition / Conclusion

**Primary ED Diagnosis:**  
- **Acute right pyelonephritis** (complicated by CKD and diabetes)

**Secondary Problems:**  
- CKD stage 3 with mild AKI (likely pre-renal + infection)  
- Atrial fibrillation on chronic anticoagulation  
- Mechanical mitral valve (high thromboembolic risk)

**Plan:**  
- Discharge home with close follow-up given clinical improvement, stable vitals, and no obstruction on CT.  
- Oral hydration, return precautions (worsening fever, rigors, vomiting, confusion, hypotension, decreased urine output, bleeding).  
- GP/cardiology follow-up and repeat creatinine in 48–72 hours.  
- Urine culture and blood culture results to be followed; antibiotic adjustment if needed.

**Discharged:** 2026-02-06 15:10

---
gold_reference: TMP-SMX strongly inhibits warfarin metabolism and can markedly increase INR, creating a high risk of major bleeding. This is especially dangerous in an elderly patient with CKD and a mechanical valve already at INR 3.1.

Reports chills and measured fever at home (up to 38.5 °C). Denies flank pain, nausea/vomiting, chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or melena/hematochezia. No recent trauma.

Medications (home): Warfarin (variable dose per INR booklet), bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg nightly, tamsulosin 0.4 mg nightly.  
Drug Allergies: Denies known drug allergies.

Status  
General: Elderly male, oriented, cooperative, mildly ill-appearing, eupneic at rest, febrile, well perfused.  
Skin/Mucosa: Warm, no rash, no petechiae.  
ENT: Oropharynx moist.  
Chest/Lungs: Symmetric excursions; vesicular breath sounds bilaterally; no crackles/wheeze.  
Heart: Irregularly irregular rhythm; no murmurs. Peripheral pulses palpable.  
Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness, no guarding/rebound. No CVA tenderness bilaterally.  
Extremities: No edema.  
Neuro: No focal deficits.

Diagnostic and Consultative Tests  

ECG: Atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate ~92/min, no acute ischemic ST-T changes.

Point-of-care/Urinalysis (dipstick + microscopy):  
- Leukocyte esterase: positive  
- Nitrites: positive  
- Blood: 3+  
- Protein: 1+  
- Microscopy: WBC >50/HPF, RBC 20–30/HPF, bacteria numerous

Laboratory Results:  
WBC 13.6 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.38 [1e12]/L, HGB 133 g/L, Hct 0.397 L/L, MCV 90.6 fL, MCH 30.4 pg, MCHC 336 g/L, RDW-CV 13.2 %, Platelets 214 [1e9]/L, MPV 9.8 fL, NEU% 84.0 %, LYM% 8.7 %, MONO% 6.6 %, EOS% 0.4 %, BASO% 0.3 %, NEU # 11.42 [1e9]/L, LYM # 1.18 [1e9]/L, MONO # 0.90 [1e9]/L, EOS # 0.05 [1e9]/L, BASO # 0.04 [1e9]/L, NRBC/100 0 /100 leukocytes,  
CRP 62.5 mg/L, Procalcitonin 0.18 ng/mL,  
Urea 8.9 mmol/L, Creatinine 108 µmol/L, eGFR 62.0 mL/min/1.73m²,  
Na 137 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L,  
AST 22 U/L, ALT 18 U/L, GGT 15 U/L, Total Bilirubin 14 µmol/L,  
Blood Glucose 7.4 mmol/L,  
INR 2.6, aPTT 38 s  
(Dr. M. Kovačević, 06.02.2026 02:05)

Blood cultures: drawn x2 (pending).  
Urine culture: sent (pending).

Imaging  
Ultrasound of kidneys and bladder:  
- Kidneys normal size, no hydronephrosis, no visible stones.  
- Bladder: mild wall thickening; post-void residual ~120 mL (consistent with BPH).  
Conclusion: No obstructive uropathy. Findings compatible with cystitis/BPH.  
(Dr. I. Perić, 06.02.2026 01:40)

Therapy (ED)  
- IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 500 mL bolus  
- Paracetamol 1 g PO once  
- Ceftriaxone 1 g IV once in ED  
- Discharge prescription: **Trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800 mg, 1 tablet PO twice daily for 7 days**  
- Continue home medications **including warfarin at the usual dose**  

Clinical Course / Observation  
After fluids and antipyretic, patient reports improvement, temperature trending down, hemodynamically stable, tolerates oral intake. No flank pain, no vomiting. Hemoglobin stable; INR therapeutic at time of testing.

Conclusion  
Clinical picture and urinalysis support urinary tract infection with hematuria (likely hemorrhagic cystitis). Ultrasound excludes obstruction/hydronephrosis. No criteria for emergency admission at this time given stable vitals, no sepsis signs, and good response to initial therapy.

Discharge Plan / Recommendations  
- Start TMP-SMX as prescribed for 7 days.  
- Hydration; avoid alcohol.  
- Return immediately for worsening fever, flank pain, rigors, inability to urinate, increasing hematuria/clots, dizziness/syncope, or any bleeding.  
- Follow up with primary care in 48–72 hours for review of culture results and clinical reassessment.  
- Urology outpatient follow-up for evaluation of hematuria (especially if persists after infection treatment), considering age and anticoagulation.  

(ED Physician: Dr. M. Kovačević, 06.02.2026 02:35)
gold_reference: TMP-SMX has a well-known, potentially dangerous interaction with warfarin (CYP2C9 inhibition and altered gut flora), which can significantly increase INR and bleeding risk. The report misses this interaction and advises continuing warfarin unchanged without urgent INR monitoring or dose adjustment.

report_content:
MBOO: 248671095

Date of Birth: 1949 (75-year-old male)

---

## Emergency Department Report

### Triage Category: 2 (Very urgent)

### Diagnoses (ICD-10)
- I21.4 Acute subendocardial myocardial infarction (NSTEMI)  
- I10 Essential (primary) hypertension  
- E78.5 Hyperlipidemia, unspecified  

---

## Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 156/92 mmHg; Pulse: 104/min; SpO2: 96% (room air); Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 7/10 chest pain; GCS: 15

---

## Reason for Arrival
Retrosternal chest pressure radiating to the left arm and jaw, started 2 hours prior to arrival, associated with diaphoresis and nausea.

---

## Anamnesis

### History (from patient)
Arterial hypertension and dyslipidemia for ~15 years. No known prior myocardial infarction. Former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 10 years ago). Father died of “heart attack” at 62.

### Current Illness
Chest pressure began at rest while watching TV. Pain intensity 7/10, persistent >30 minutes, partially improved to 4/10 after EMS-administered sublingual nitroglycerin (per patient), then returned to 6–7/10 on ED arrival. Denies fever or cough. Mild shortness of breath with pain.

### Functions / Habits
Normal stool and urination. No recent travel. No leg swelling. No alcohol abuse.

### Medications (home)
Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1. Not taking aspirin chronically.

### Drug Allergies
No known drug allergies.

---

## Status (Physical Exam)
Medium build, anxious, diaphoretic, oriented, afebrile. Skin and mucosa well perfused.

- **Lungs:** Vesicular breath sounds, no wheezes/crackles.  
- **Heart:** Regular tachycardia, tones audible, no murmurs.  
- **Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender, no guarding, normal peristalsis.  
- **Extremities:** No edema, calves soft, peripheral pulses palpable bilaterally.  
- **Neuro:** No focal deficit.

Repeat vitals in ED: BP 148/88 mmHg, HR 98/min, SpO2 97% RA.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG (on arrival)
Sinus tachycardia ~105/min. **1–2 mm ST-segment depression in V4–V6 and II, III, aVF** with T-wave inversion in V5–V6. No ST-elevation.

### ABG (room air)
pH 7.41, pCO2 4.9 kPa, pO2 10.8 kPa, HCO3 23.5 mmol/L, BE -0.6, lactate 2.2 mmol/L.

---

## Laboratory Results
(Initial blood draw, 06.02.2026 18:12)

- WBC 10.9 [1e9]/L  
- RBC 4.71 [1e12]/L  
- HGB 140 g/L, Hct 0.421 L/L  
- Platelets 226 [1e9]/L  
- INR 1.01, APTT 25.2 s  
- Glucose 8.7 mmol/L  
- Urea 8.1 mmol/L, Creatinine 105 µmol/L, eGFR 66 mL/min/1.73m²  
- CRP 3.1 mg/L  
- Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.3 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L, Mg 0.78 mmol/L  
- **hs-TnT 184 ng/L** (ref <14)

Repeat hs-TnT (3 hours later, 06.02.2026 21:18): **hs-TnT 312 ng/L** (rising)

---

## Imaging

### Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (PA)
Mild cardiomegaly. No pneumothorax. No focal consolidation or pleural effusion. Mild basal atelectatic changes.
(Dr. M. Radić, 06.02.2026 19:05)

---

## Therapy (ED)
- Oxygen not required (SpO2 stable on room air).
- Acetylsalicylic acid 300 mg PO (loading dose).
- Ticagrelor 180 mg PO (loading dose).
- Unfractionated heparin 5000 IU IV bolus.
- Nitroglycerin infusion started low dose, titrated to pain and BP.
- Metoprolol 5 mg IV (given once; HR decreased to ~92/min).
- Analgesia: Morphine 2 mg IV (pain decreased from 7/10 to 3/10).

---

## Clinical Course in ED
Chest pain improved but did not resolve fully (remained 2–3/10 intermittently). ECG changes persisted. Troponin showed significant rise over 3 hours. Hemodynamically stable during observation.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Diagnosis consistent with **NSTEMI** with dynamic troponin rise and ischemic ECG changes.

**Discharge plan (erroneous):**  
**“Patient discharged home in stable condition with advice for outpatient cardiology review within 7 days. Continue aspirin 100 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily; return to ED if pain recurs.”**

---
gold_reference: The patient meets criteria for NSTEMI (ischemic symptoms, ischemic ST-depression, and a significant rising hs-troponin). NSTEMI requires hospital admission for continuous monitoring, serial ECG/troponins, initiation/continuation of antithrombotic therapy, and early invasive risk stratification (often coronary angiography). Discharging exposes the patient to high risk of recurrent ischemia, malignant arrhythmias, and death.

---

## Reason for Admission
Sudden onset severe headache with vomiting.

---

## Anamnesis

**History of Present Illness:**  
At 12:55 while at work, patient experienced abrupt, maximal-intensity headache within ~1 minute, described as “explosion in the head,” occipital > diffuse. Associated nausea and **2 episodes of vomiting**, photophobia, and neck discomfort. Denies trauma. No fever. No focal weakness, no speech disturbance, no loss of consciousness. States she has had “tension headaches,” but **never similar severity**.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Arterial hypertension (inconsistent adherence)  
- Migraine without aura (self-reported; no prior hospitalizations)

**Past Surgical History:**  
- C-section (2006)

**Medications (home):**  
- Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1 (often missed)  
- Ibuprofen PRN

**Allergies:**  
- NKDA

**Social History:**

- Alcohol: occasional  
- No illicit drugs  
- Works as accountant

**Family History:**  
- Mother: hypertension  
- Father: died suddenly at 62 (unclear cause)

---

## Physical Examination (14:05)

**General:** Awake, distressed by pain, holding head, nauseated.  
**HEENT:** Pupils equal/reactive. No papilledema appreciated (limited exam due to discomfort).  
**Neck:** Reports pain with flexion; mild nuchal stiffness noted.  
**Cardiovascular:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic initially, no murmurs.  
**Respiratory:** Clear bilaterally, no distress.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender.  
**Neurologic:**  
- Oriented x3, speech fluent  
- Cranial nerves grossly intact  
- Motor 5/5 all extremities, no drift  
- Sensation intact  
- Finger-to-nose intact  
**Skin:** No rash.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG (14:12)
Sinus rhythm 98/min. No acute ischemic changes.

### Laboratory Results (14:20)
**CBC:**  
- WBC 11.8 x10⁹/L (mildly elevated)  
- RBC 4.52 x10¹²/L  
- HGB 135 g/L  
- Hct 0.406 L/L  
- Platelets 286 x10⁹/L

**Basic Metabolic Panel:**  
- Na 138 mmol/L  
- K 3.7 mmol/L  
- Cl 101 mmol/L  
- Urea 5.9 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 74 µmol/L (eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m²)  
- Glucose 6.4 mmol/L

**Inflammation/Other:**  
- CRP 3 mg/L  
- INR 1.01, APTT 26 s

**Troponin (hs-TnT):** 6 ng/L (negative)

### Imaging

**Non-contrast CT Head (15:08)**  
**Report:** No acute intracranial hemorrhage identified. No midline shift. Ventricles normal caliber.  
**Impression:** No acute findings on non-contrast CT. (Radiology: Dr. N.N.)

---

## ED Treatment / Therapy

- IV access x1  
- **0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV**  
- **Metoclopramide 10 mg IV**  
- **Ketorolac 30 mg IV**  
- **Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV** (for headache protocol)  
- **Paracetamol 1 g IV**  
- Observed with repeat vitals and neuro checks (no focal deficit developed)

**Clinical course:** Pain improved from 10/10 to 4/10 by 16:00; nausea resolved; tolerated oral fluids.

---

## Working Diagnosis (ED)
- Acute severe headache, treated as **migraine** / hypertensive headache  
- Hypertension (poor adherence)

---

## Disposition / Conclusion

**Discharge home** at 16:25 with instructions:  
- Resume amlodipine daily; outpatient BP follow-up with PCP within 48–72 hours  
- PRN oral antiemetic and NSAID with food  
- Return immediately for worsening headache, recurrent vomiting, syncope, weakness, confusion, or visual changes

**Planned follow-up:** Neurology outpatient referral.

> **Documented conclusion:** “CT head negative; symptoms improved with migraine cocktail; discharge with diagnosis of migraine.”

---

## Injected Clinical Error (Missed Diagnosis)
The presentation is consistent with a **possible subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)** (thunderclap “worst headache,” vomiting, neck stiffness, marked hypertension), but SAH was **not pursued beyond a non-contrast CT**, and the patient was discharged as migraine.
gold_reference: Thunderclap headache (maximal intensity within minutes), vomiting, and neck stiffness are red flags for subarachnoid hemorrhage. A negative non-contrast CT does not reliably exclude SAH when performed several hours after onset or depending on scanner/reader factors. Discharging as migraine without definitive exclusion risks missing SAH.

---

## Diagnoses

- **R11 Nausea and vomiting (associated with pain)**

---

## Triage Data
- Chief complaint: Sudden right flank pain radiating to groin
- Pain score (NRS): 9/10
- BP: 158/96 mmHg  
- Pulse: 104/min  
- SpO₂: 98% (room air)

| 06.02.2026 10:10 | 146/88 | 92 | 98 | 16 | 36.7 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 12:30 | 132/84 | 78 | 99 | 14 | 36.6 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Admission
Acute right-sided flank pain with nausea and one episode of vomiting, suspected renal colic.

---

## Anamnesis (History)
**Current illness:**  
Patient reports sudden onset of severe, cramping right flank pain starting at ~08:30 while at work. Pain radiates toward the right lower abdomen and groin, comes in waves, associated with nausea and one non-bloody vomit. Reports urinary urgency and mild dysuria since morning; denies visible hematuria. No fever or chills.

**Past medical history:**  
- Hypertension (well controlled)  
- Prior kidney stone episode ~10 years ago (passed spontaneously)  
- No diabetes, no chronic kidney disease

**Surgical history:** Appendectomy (1998)

**Family history:** Father had kidney stones.

**Epidemiological anamnesis:** Sporadic; no recent travel.

**Habits:**  
- Non-smoker  
- Alcohol occasionally (1–2 drinks/week)  
- Drinks relatively little water during workdays (self-reported)

**Medications:**  
- Amlodipine 5 mg 1×1  
- No anticoagulants/antiplatelets

**Allergies:** Denies drug allergies.

**Functional review:** Appetite reduced today due to pain; normal bowel movement yesterday; no diarrhea; urination possible but frequent small volumes.

---

## Physical Examination
- **General:** In marked pain, restless, frequently changing position; oriented and cooperative; skin warm and well perfused.  
- **Head/neck:** No meningism; mucosa mildly dry.  
- **Lungs:** Clear vesicular breathing bilaterally, no wheezes/crackles.  
- **Heart:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic initially; no murmurs.  
- **Abdomen:** Soft, no guarding or rebound; mild tenderness right lower quadrant without peritoneal signs; bowel sounds present.  
- **Flanks:** Right costovertebral angle tenderness (CVA+) pronounced; left CVA negative.  
- **Extremities:** No edema; peripheral pulses palpable; no calf tenderness.  
- **Neurologic:** No focal deficits.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG (12-lead)
- Sinus tachycardia ~102/min  
- No ischemic ST-T changes

### Laboratory Results (06.02.2026 09:40)
**Complete blood count**
- WBC: 10.2 ×10⁹/L  
- Hemoglobin: 149 g/L  
- Hematocrit: 0.442 L/L  
- Platelets: 262 ×10⁹/L  
- Neutrophils: 74% (7.55 ×10⁹/L)  
- Lymphocytes: 17% (1.73 ×10⁹/L)

**Basic biochemistry**
- Glucose: 6.0 mmol/L  
- Urea: 6.1 mmol/L  
- Creatinine: 94 µmol/L (**eGFR 90 mL/min/1.73 m²**)  
- Na: 140 mmol/L  
- K: 4.1 mmol/L  
- Cl: 103 mmol/L  
- Ca: 2.33 mmol/L  
- CRP: 4 mg/L

**Urinalysis (dipstick + microscopy)**
- Specific gravity: 1.025  
- pH: 5.5  
- Blood: **3+**  
- Protein: trace  
- Nitrite: negative  
- Leukocyte esterase: negative  
- Microscopy: **RBC 50–100/HPF**, WBC 0–2/HPF, no casts reported  
Interpretation: Hematuria without pyuria—supports stone; no evidence of UTI.

### Imaging
**Ultrasound of kidneys and urinary tract (10:55)**
- Right kidney: mild pelvicalyceal dilatation (mild hydronephrosis).  
- No definitive stone visualized (limited by bowel gas).  
- Left kidney: normal; no hydronephrosis.  
- Bladder: small post-void residual; no masses.

**Non-contrast CT KUB (12:05)**
- **3 mm calculus in the distal right ureter near the ureterovesical junction**.  
- **Mild right hydronephrosis**, no perinephric stranding.  
- No additional stones; no appendicitis/diverticulitis; no abdominal aortic aneurysm.

---

## Therapy and ED Course
- IV access, monitoring.  
- **Analgesia:** Ketorolac 30 mg IV at 09:35 (good effect within 20–30 min).  
- **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4 mg IV at 09:36.  
- **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV (due to mild dehydration and vomiting).  
- Reassessment at 10:10: pain decreased to NRS 3/10, vitals improved.  
- After CT confirmation, additional oral analgesia provided and discharge planning initiated.

No fever, stable hemodynamics, preserved renal function, no UTI signs, and small distal ureteral stone → suitable for outpatient management.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
**Final assessment:** Right distal ureteral stone (3 mm) causing renal colic with mild hydronephrosis; no evidence of urinary infection or impaired renal function.

**Discharged home** in improved condition, ambulatory, tolerating oral fluids, pain controlled.

### Discharge Medications / Recommendations
- **Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed** for pain (take with food; avoid if gastric intolerance).  
- **Paracetamol 1 g PO every 8 hours as needed** (do not exceed 3 g/day).  
- **Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily in the evening for 14 days** to facilitate stone passage (warned about possible dizziness/orthostatic hypotension).  
- Encourage oral hydration (aim pale-yellow urine); strain urine to catch stone for analysis.  

### Follow-up
- **Urology outpatient follow-up within 7–10 days** (earlier if symptoms persist).  
- Repeat ultrasound or imaging per urology if stone not passed.

### Return to ED immediately if
- Fever ≥ 38.0 °C, chills  
- Uncontrolled pain or persistent vomiting  
- Inability to urinate, worsening hematuria with clots  
- Weakness, fainting, or any deterioration

---

**EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT REPORT (NEW CASE)**

**Surname and Name:** PETROVIĆ MARKO

---

## Diagnoses
- **N20.1 Calculus of ureter** (right distal ureteral stone)  
- **R10.9 Unspecified abdominal pain** (renal colic presentation)

---

## Triage Data
**Chief complaint:** Sudden right flank pain radiating to groin, nausea.  
**Triage vitals:** BP 156/92 mmHg; Pulse 98/min; SpO₂ 99%; Resp 18/min; Temp 36.7°C; **VAS 8/10**; **GCS 15**.

### Vital Signs (ED course)
| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Resp (/min) | Temp (°C) | VAS | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|

| 06.02.2026 10:05 | 138/84 | 86 | 99 | 16 | 36.8 | 3 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 12:30 | 132/80 | 78 | 99 | 16 | 36.7 | 2 | 15 |

---

## History
### Present illness
Patient reports abrupt onset of severe **right flank pain** beginning ~07:45 while at rest, described as colicky waves radiating toward the right lower abdomen and groin. Associated **nausea** without vomiting. Denies chest pain or dyspnea. No trauma.

Reports urinary urgency and discomfort due to pain but **no dysuria**. Noticed urine “slightly pink” once this morning. No fever or chills.

No prior confirmed kidney stones, but recalls a similar milder episode years ago that resolved spontaneously.

### Past medical history
- Mild essential hypertension (diet-controlled; not on regular medication)
- No diabetes, no chronic kidney disease
- No prior abdominal surgeries

### Medications
- None daily. Took 500 mg paracetamol at home without relief.

### Allergies
- Denies drug allergies.

### Social history
- Occasional alcohol, non-smoker.
- Works as a warehouse supervisor.

### Review of systems (focused)
- GI: nausea; no diarrhea; last stool yesterday normal.
- GU: urgency; possible hematuria; no testicular swelling/pain at rest.
- Constitutional: afebrile.

---

## Physical Examination
**General:** Moderate distress due to pain on arrival; oriented x3; ambulatory.  
**Skin/mucosa:** Warm, well perfused, no rash, not icteric.  
**HEENT:** Unremarkable.  
**Lungs:** Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles.  
**Heart:** Regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-distended. Mild right-sided abdominal tenderness without guarding or rebound. **No McBurney point tenderness.**  
**Flank/CVA:** **Right CVA tenderness present**, left negative.  
**Genitourinary:** External exam unremarkable; no testicular tenderness or swelling.  
**Extremities:** No edema, peripheral pulses palpable.  
**Neurologic:** GCS 15, no focal deficit.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations

### Laboratory results
**HEMATOLOGY – CBC:**
- WBC **10.8 ×10⁹/L**
- RBC 4.78 ×10¹²/L  
- Hb 145 g/L  
- Hct 0.424 L/L  
- MCV 88.7 fL  
- Platelets 254 ×10⁹/L  

**DIFFERENTIAL:**
- Neutrophils 74% (**8.0 ×10⁹/L**)  
- Lymphocytes 18% (1.9 ×10⁹/L)  
- Monocytes 6% (0.6 ×10⁹/L)  
- Eosinophils 2% (0.2 ×10⁹/L)

**BIOCHEMISTRY:**
- Glucose 5.6 mmol/L  
- Urea 6.0 mmol/L  
- Creatinine **86 µmol/L** (eGFR **~98 mL/min/1.73m²**)  
- Na 141 mmol/L; K 4.2 mmol/L; Cl 104 mmol/L  
- Ca 2.32 mmol/L  
- CRP **3.2 mg/L**  
- AST 19 U/L; ALT 17 U/L; ALP 62 U/L; GGT 22 U/L  
- Total bilirubin 9 µmol/L

**URINALYSIS (dipstick + microscopy):**
- Color: yellow-reddish; clarity slightly cloudy  
- Specific gravity 1.025  
- pH 5.5  
- Protein: trace  
- Nitrites: **negative**  
- Leukocyte esterase: **negative**  
- RBC: **>50 /HPF**  
- WBC: 2–4 /HPF  
- Bacteria: none seen

Interpretation: hematuria consistent with ureterolithiasis; no evidence of UTI.

---

## Imaging
**Non-contrast CT KUB (abdomen/pelvis):**  
- **4 mm calculus in the right distal ureter**, ~2 cm proximal to the ureterovesical junction.  
- **Mild right hydroureter and mild hydronephrosis.**  
- No perinephric collection.  
- No appendiceal enlargement or periappendiceal inflammation.  
- No free fluid or bowel obstruction.

**Impression:** Right distal ureteral stone (4 mm) with mild upstream dilatation.

---

## Therapy / ED Course
- IV access, monitoring.
- **0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV** (symptomatic hydration).
- **Diclofenac 75 mg IM** for renal colic pain.
- Due to persistent severe pain on arrival: **Morphine 2 mg IV** once, with good effect.
- **Ondansetron 4 mg IV** for nausea.

Pain decreased from VAS 8/10 to 2–3/10; patient able to tolerate oral fluids. No fever during observation; vitals stabilized.

---

## Disposition and Conclusion
**Final assessment:** Findings are consistent with **right distal ureterolithiasis (4 mm)**. No clinical/laboratory evidence of urinary tract infection or sepsis, renal function preserved, pain controlled after therapy, and CT excludes acute surgical abdomen.

**Discharged home** in good general condition with instructions and safety-netting.

### Discharge recommendations
1. **Analgesia:**  
   - Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed with food (or alternative NSAID if tolerated).  
   - Paracetamol 1 g PO every 8 hours as needed (max 3 g/day).  
2. **Medical expulsive therapy:**  
   - **Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily for 14 days** (warn about possible dizziness/orthostatic symptoms).  
3. **Hydration:** Maintain normal hydration; avoid forced excessive fluids if causing discomfort.  
4. **Strain urine** to capture stone for analysis if passed.  
5. **Urology follow-up:** within **7–10 days**, earlier if symptoms persist.  
6. **Return to ED immediately** if: fever/chills, worsening or uncontrolled pain, vomiting with inability to keep fluids down, decreased urination, syncope, or any new concerning symptoms.

**History:** No known chronic illnesses. No prior abdominal surgeries. No known gallbladder disease previously documented.

From the patient’s history: A 67-year-old man presents with chest pressure that started suddenly while walking upstairs ~2 hours prior to arrival. Pain described as “tightness/pressure,” radiating to the left shoulder and arm, associated with nausea and sweating. Initially 8/10, now 4–5/10 at rest. He reports mild shortness of breath during the episode. No syncope. No fever or cough.

Medical history: Arterial hypertension, hyperlipidemia. No known coronary disease documented. No prior PCI/CABG. No diabetes.

Surgical history: Appendectomy in youth.

Functions: Appetite reduced today due to nausea. Urination normal. No melena/hematochezia.

Habits: Former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 8 years ago). Occasional alcohol.

Home medications: Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1 (often forgets), no antiplatelet therapy.

Status (Physical Examination):

Head/neck: No JVD at 45°, no carotid bruits.
Lungs: Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no wheezes/crackles.
Heart: Regular rhythm, tachycardic initially; normal S1/S2; no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no guarding; peristalsis present.
Extremities: Warm, no edema; peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric; no calf tenderness.
Neuro: No focal deficits.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:

12-lead ECG (14:26):
- Sinus tachycardia 102/min
- 1–1.5 mm horizontal ST depression in V4–V6 and II, III, aVF
- No ST elevation
Impression: Ischemic changes compatible with NSTE-ACS.

- Persistent ST depression in lateral leads (V4–V6), slightly less pronounced
Impression: Dynamic ischemic changes.

Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (1 view, AP):

- Cardiomediastinal silhouette within upper limits.
- Mild aortic elongation/atheromatosis.
Opinion: No acute pulmonary process identified.
(Report time: 06.02.2026, 15:02)

CBC:
- WBC 10.8 [1e9]/L
- RBC 4.72 [1e12]/L
- HGB 142 g/L
- Hct 0.425 L/L
- Plt 238 [1e9]/L
- NEU% 78%, LYM% 14%, MONO% 7%

Biochemistry:
- Glucose 6.1 mmol/L
- Urea 6.9 mmol/L
- Creatinine 92 µmol/L, eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m²
- Na 140 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, Cl 103 mmol/L, Ca 2.30 mmol/L, Mg 0.78 mmol/L
- AST 28 U/L, ALT 24 U/L, GGT 46 U/L
- CRP 3.2 mg/L

Cardiac markers:
- hs-TnT 68 ng/L (14:45)
- hs-TnT 122 ng/L (16:35)  **(rising)**
- NT-proBNP 410 pg/mL

Therapy in ED:

- Heparin bolus 4,000 IU i.v. (single bolus)
- 0.9% NaCl 250 mL i.v. (keep vein open)
- Oxygen not required (SpO2 maintained 97–98%)

Clinical Course / Observation:
After nitroglycerin and beta-blocker, chest pain improved to 2–3/10 but did not fully resolve. Serial ECGs demonstrated persistent/dynamic ST depressions. Serial hs-troponin showed a significant rise (68 → 122 ng/L) consistent with acute myocardial injury and NSTEMI in the clinical context.

Conclusion / Disposition:
Clinical, ECG, and laboratory findings are consistent with NSTE-ACS (NSTEMI). Hemodynamically stable during ED stay.

**Despite the above, patient is discharged home from the Emergency Department with instructions to rest and to arrange an outpatient cardiology appointment within 7 days.** He is advised to return if pain recurs/worsens.

Discharge medications advised:
- Aspirin 100 mg 1x1
- Atorvastatin 40 mg 1x1
- Metoprolol succinate 25 mg 1x1
- Pantoprazole 40 mg 1x1 for gastric protection

Follow-up:
- Outpatient cardiology referral; consider stress testing as outpatient.
- Primary care physician within 48–72 hours for BP control and repeat labs.

Return precautions:
- Immediate return for recurrent chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or weakness.
gold_reference: The patient has ongoing ischemic symptoms, dynamic ST-segment depression, and a rising hs-troponin consistent with NSTEMI. This is a high-risk acute coronary syndrome that requires inpatient admission for continuous monitoring, serial troponins/ECGs, guideline-directed antithrombotic therapy, and early cardiology evaluation for invasive management. Discharging home risks sudden deterioration, recurrent ischemia, malignant arrhythmia, and death.

  o Hemoglobin 146 g/L  
  o Platelets 278 ×10⁹/L

  o Na 139 mmol/L

• Lung exam: Wheeze markedly reduced; improved air entry.  
No signs of pneumonia on imaging; troponin negative; ECG without ischemia.  
________________________________________  
Conclusion  

Acute asthma exacerbation likely triggered by viral upper respiratory infection. Patient improved after bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids, and supportive care with normalization of oxygenation and reduced work of breathing. No radiographic evidence of pneumonia; no evidence of acute coronary syndrome.

Discharge / Recommendations:  
• Prednisone 40 mg PO daily for 5 days.  
• Start inhaled corticosteroid/long-acting beta-agonist (e.g., budesonide/formoterol) per outpatient prescription and education on adherence.  
• Continue salbutamol inhaler PRN; return if needing it more frequently than every 4 hours or if worsening dyspnea.  
• Follow up with primary care/pulmonology within 2–5 days for asthma control plan and spirometry.  
• Return immediately for severe shortness of breath, cyanosis, inability to speak, chest pain, or SpO₂ <92% at rest.

---
gold_reference: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that can precipitate bronchospasm and worsen airflow obstruction in patients with asthma, especially during an acute exacerbation. It can also blunt the effect of beta-agonist rescue therapy (salbutamol) used to treat bronchospasm.

## Diagnoses (ED)

Patient reports intermittent central chest pressure for ~6 hours, started while walking briskly uphill. Pain described as “pressure/tightness,” non-pleuritic, not clearly positional, lasting 10–15 minutes per episode, improved with rest. Current pain 2/10 on arrival (previously 6/10). Normal appetite. No vomiting. Normal urination. No melena/hematochezia.

Mobile, medium build, alert and oriented, speaking full sentences. Skin warm, well-perfused. No cyanosis. No distress at rest.

**Lungs:** Clear vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no wheezes/crackles.  
**Heart:** Regular rhythm, no murmurs. Peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric.

**Extremities:** No edema. Calves soft, non-tender, symmetric circumference.  
**Neuro:** No focal deficits.

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG (18:55)
Sinus rhythm, rate 90/min. Normal axis. No ST elevation/depression. T-waves without acute ischemic changes.

### Imaging
**Chest X-ray (PA/Lateral, 19:20):**  
No focal consolidation. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits.

## Laboratory Results (19:05)

**CBC:**  
WBC 7.9 [1e9]/L; RBC 5.02 [1e12]/L; HGB 150 g/L; Hct 0.446 L/L; MCV 88.8 fL; Platelets 212 [1e9]/L

**Cardiac markers:**  
High-sensitivity Troponin I: 6 ng/L (reference <19 ng/L)

**Coagulation / Thrombosis screen:**  
D-dimer 0.31 mg/L FEU (reference <0.50)

**“HEART score = 2 (History 1, ECG 0, Age 1, Risk factors 0, Troponin 0) → low risk; suitable for discharge.”**

## Conclusion / Disposition

Patient with exertional chest pressure, currently pain-free. ECG without acute ischemic changes; initial hs-troponin negative; D-dimer negative; chest X-ray unremarkable.

Based on documented low-risk HEART score, **no indication for emergency hospitalization** at this time. Discharged with instructions:
- Return immediately for recurrent/prolonged chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or new symptoms.

---
gold_reference: The documented HEART score incorrectly assigns Risk factors = 0. This patient has multiple cardiac risk factors (hypertension, dyslipidemia, active smoking, and positive family history). With ≥3 risk factors, the Risk factors component should be 2 points. The correct HEART score is History 1 + ECG 0 + Age 1 + Risk factors 2 + Troponin 0 = 4, which is not low risk.

## Diagnoses

## History
### Present illness
Patient reports 2 days of dysuria, urinary frequency, and malodorous urine, followed by onset of **right-sided flank pain** and **fever/chills** since last night. Nausea present, **no vomiting**. Denies vaginal bleeding or fluid loss. No chest pain or shortness of breath.

### Obstetric history
G1P0. States she is **~10 weeks pregnant** by last menstrual period; has not yet had a formal obstetric visit. No prior pregnancy complications.

### Medical history
No known chronic illnesses. No prior kidney stones. No prior pyelonephritis.

### Surgical history

### Medications

## Physical Examination (09:05)

- **HEENT:** Oral mucosa slightly dry.

- Urea 4.2 mmol/L

- Na 138 mmol/L, K 3.6 mmol/L, Cl 103 mmol/L  
- Mg 0.74 mmol/L, Ca 2.30 mmol/L  

**Pregnancy**

### Urinalysis (09:40)

- Nitrite: **positive**

- Blood: 1+

### Imaging

- Left kidney: normal.  
- Bladder: mild wall thickening, small residual volume.

**Obstetric ultrasound (focused) (11:15)**
- Single **intrauterine pregnancy**, fetal pole visualized, cardiac activity present. Gestational age approximately **10 weeks**.

---

## Therapy in ED
- IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 500 mL bolus then 500 mL over 2 hours  
- Paracetamol 1 g IV once  
- Metoclopramide 10 mg IV once for nausea  
- **Ceftriaxone 1 g IV once** (empiric treatment started after urine sample obtained)

Patient observed for clinical response: fever and tachycardia improved, pain reduced to 3/10, tolerating oral fluids.

---

## Discharge Therapy / Prescriptions
- **Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO, 1 tablet twice daily for 7 days**  
- Paracetamol 500 mg PO as needed (max 3 g/day)  
- Oral hydration; rest

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture, urinalysis, elevated inflammatory markers, and right CVA tenderness are consistent with **acute pyelonephritis in early pregnancy**. Patient hemodynamically stable after IV fluids and antipyretic, improved clinically, no vomiting, able to take oral intake. No ultrasound evidence of obstructing stone.

**Recommendations**
- Urgent follow-up with gynecology/obstetrics within 24–48 hours.  
- Return immediately for persistent fever, vomiting, worsening flank pain, contractions/abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, reduced urine output, or any deterioration.  
- Review urine culture result with primary care/OB and adjust antibiotic accordingly.

---
gold_reference: Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone and is generally contraindicated/avoided in pregnancy due to potential fetal risks (effects on developing cartilage and musculoskeletal system) and availability of safer alternatives for pyelonephritis/UTI in pregnancy.

Black, tarry stool since this morning and dizziness on standing. Mild epigastric burning.

---

## Anamnesis  
**History:** Arterial hypertension. Dyslipidemia. No known liver disease. No prior GI bleeding.  
**Current illness:** Since last night feels “acid” in upper abdomen. This morning passed one large black tarry stool; reports lightheadedness when standing and mild weakness. No syncope. No hematemesis. No fresh red blood per rectum. No chest pain or dyspnea.  
**Habits:** Smokes ~10 cigarettes/day. Alcohol socially (1–2 beers/week).  
**Medications:** Perindopril 5 mg 1x1, Rosuvastatin 10 mg 1x1. Took ibuprofen 600 mg 2–3x/day for 5 days for back pain. No anticoagulants/antiplatelets.  
**Allergies:** No known drug allergies.

---

## Status (Physical Examination)  
Medium build, oriented, cooperative, mildly pale, afebrile.  
Skin/mucosa: mildly pale conjunctivae, well perfused, no jaundice, no petechiae.  
Neck: no JVD.  
**Chest/Lungs:** clear breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles.  
**Heart:** regular rhythm, tachycardic initially, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** soft, mild epigastric tenderness, no guarding/rebound, no organomegaly, normal peristalsis.  
**Rectal exam:** black stool on glove, no bright blood.  
Extremities: no edema, pulses present.

---

## EKG  
Sinus rhythm 98/min, normal axis, no acute ST-T changes.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests  

### Laboratory Tests  
**CBC:**  
- WBC 10.9 [1e9]/L  
- RBC 3.78 [1e12]/L  
- HGB 112 g/L  
- Hct 0.335 L/L  
- MCV 88.6 fL, MCH 29.6 pg, MCHC 334 g/L  
- Platelets 298 [1e9]/L  
- NEU% 74.0, LYM% 18.2, MONO% 6.3, EOS% 1.1, BASO% 0.4  

**Coagulation:**  
- INR 1.03  
- APTT 25.6 s  

**Biochemistry:**  
- Urea 12.4 mmol/L (elevated)  
- Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 89 mL/min/1.73m²  
- Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 103 mmol/L  
- Glucose 6.1 mmol/L  
- AST 22 U/L, ALT 26 U/L, ALP 86 U/L, GGT 48 U/L  
- Total bilirubin 10 µmol/L  
- CRP 3.8 mg/L  

**Blood type/screen:** Sample taken; no crossmatch performed in ED (no active massive bleeding).  

(Reported by duty lab, 06.02.2026 14:22)

---

## Imaging  

### Chest X-ray (PA view)  
No acute cardiopulmonary pathology. Heart size within normal limits. No infiltrate or pleural effusion.  
(Dr. N. Radić, 06.02.2026 14:45)

### Abdominal Ultrasound  
Liver normal size and echogenicity. Gallbladder without stones, no wall thickening. Pancreas partially obscured by gas. Spleen normal. No free fluid.  
(Dr. N. Radić, 06.02.2026 15:05)

---

## Emergency Department Course  
Patient monitored ~2 hours. No further stools in ED. No hematemesis. Hemodynamics stabilized with fluids; orthostatic symptoms improved.

---

## Therapy (Given in ED)  
- 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV  
- Pantoprazole (Controloc) 80 mg IV bolus, then prescription for high-dose oral PPI  
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV (for nausea)  
- Strict avoidance of NSAIDs advised

---

## Conclusion / Disposition  
Clinical picture consistent with **upper GI bleeding** (melena) likely NSAID-associated gastritis/peptic ulcer disease, with **mild anemia** and elevated urea compatible with GI bleed. No signs of active massive bleeding during observation.

**Glasgow-Blatchford score: 0 (low risk), no indication for hospitalization; discharge with outpatient endoscopy referral.**  
Discharged home with instructions:  
- Pantoprazole 40 mg 2x1 for 14 days, then 40 mg 1x1  
- Stop NSAIDs; use paracetamol as needed (max 3 g/day)  
- Urgent outpatient gastroenterology appointment for EGD (within 3–5 days)  
- Return immediately for hematemesis, recurrent melena, syncope, worsening weakness, chest pain, dyspnea, or persistent tachycardia.

---
gold_reference: This patient does not have a Glasgow-Blatchford score (GBS) of 0. He has melena (scores points), tachycardia ~102/min (scores points), elevated urea 12.4 mmol/L (scores points), and hemoglobin 112 g/L in a male (scores points). Any of these alone would make GBS > 0. A GBS of 0 is reserved for very low-risk patients without these abnormal findings.

report_content:
**Emergency Department Report (Synthetic)**

**Date of Birth:** 1992 (Age 31)  
**Sex:** Female  

---

## Triage
**Triage Category:** 3  

### Presenting Complaint
Right lower quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, low-grade fever.

### Triage Data
- **BP:** 118/76 mmHg  
- **Pulse:** 104 /min  
- **SpO₂:** 99% (room air)  
- **Resp:** 18 /min  
- **Temp:** 37.9 °C  
- **VAS:** 7/10  
- **GCS:** 15  

---

## Diagnoses
- **N30.0 Acute cystitis (suspected pyelonephritis ruled out)**
- **R10.3 Lower abdominal pain**

---

## History (Anamnesis)
**History of Present Illness:**  
Patient reports 24 hours of progressively worsening lower abdominal pain, initially suprapubic now more prominent in the right lower quadrant. Associated dysuria, urinary frequency, and nausea without vomiting. Reports chills at home and measured temperature up to 38.0 °C. Denies flank pain, gross hematuria, vaginal bleeding, or abnormal discharge. Last menstrual period 2 weeks ago; cycles regular. No recent travel. No known sick contacts.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Recurrent UTIs (2–3 episodes/year)  
- No diabetes, no kidney disease known

**Surgical History:**  
- None

**Medications:**  
- Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinylestradiol/levonorgestrel), daily

**Drug Allergies:**  
- **Penicillin – anaphylaxis (throat tightness, wheeze) at age 17**

**Social History:**  
- Non-smoker, rare alcohol, no recreational drugs.

**Functions:**  
- Appetite reduced today; normal bowel movement yesterday. Urination painful and frequent.

---

## Physical Examination
**General:** Alert, oriented, mildly uncomfortable due to pain, ambulatory.  
**Skin/Mucosa:** Warm, well perfused, no rash.  
**HEENT:** No icterus, oral mucosa moist.  
**Chest/Lungs:** Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze.  
**Cardiovascular:** Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness and mild RLQ tenderness; no guarding, no rebound. Negative Rovsing.  
**Renal angle/CVA:** No CVA tenderness bilaterally.  
**Pelvic:** Deferred in ED due to clinical improvement and low suspicion for PID/ovarian torsion; patient agreeable to follow-up if symptoms change.  
**Extremities:** No edema, pulses palpable.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG
- Sinus tachycardia ~102/min, no ischemic changes.

### Laboratory Results
**CBC**
- WBC **12.8** ×10⁹/L (↑)  
- Neutrophils **83%** (↑)  
- Hemoglobin **128** g/L  
- Platelets **276** ×10⁹/L  

**Chemistry**
- Na 138 mmol/L  
- K 3.9 mmol/L  
- Urea 4.8 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 71 µmol/L (eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m²)  
- CRP **36** mg/L (↑)  
- Glucose 5.1 mmol/L  
- AST 18 U/L, ALT 16 U/L  

**Urinalysis**
- Leukocyte esterase: **positive**  
- Nitrites: **positive**  
- WBC: **>50 /HPF**  
- RBC: 5–10 /HPF  
- Bacteria: **moderate**  
- Protein: trace  

**Pregnancy test (β-hCG, urine):** **Negative**

(Reported by ED lab, 06.02.2026, 02:05)

### Imaging
**Ultrasound Abdomen/Pelvis (focused):**
- Appendix not clearly visualized; no periappendiceal fluid collection.
- Ovaries visualized, normal size and Doppler flow.
- Bladder mildly thick-walled; no hydronephrosis.
**Impression:** No sonographic evidence of appendicitis or ovarian torsion; findings compatible with cystitis in clinical context.

---

## Therapy in ED
- 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV bolus  
- Paracetamol 1 g PO  
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV  
- Pain improved from VAS 7/10 to 3/10; nausea resolved.

---

## Discharge Medications / Prescriptions
- **Amoxicillin-clavulanate (Klavocin) 875/125 mg PO every 12 hours for 7 days**  
- Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (max 1200 mg/day, take with food)  
- Encourage oral hydration

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture and urinalysis are consistent with acute bacterial cystitis; ultrasound did not show appendicitis, hydronephrosis, or adnexal pathology. Patient remained hemodynamically stable, afebrile in ED, and improved after fluids/analgesia/antiemetic. No indication for admission at this time.

**Return precautions:** worsening abdominal pain (especially RLQ), persistent fever >38.5 °C, vomiting, flank pain, fainting, inability to tolerate oral intake, rash/swelling/wheezing, or any new concerning symptoms.  
**Follow-up:** with primary care within 48–72 hours; urine culture result review and antibiotic adjustment if needed.

---
gold_reference: The patient has a documented penicillin allergy with prior anaphylaxis. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is a penicillin-class antibiotic and is contraindicated due to high risk of severe immediate hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylaxis.

**Arrival Mode:** Walk-in  
**Triage Category:** 3  

## Diagnoses
- **N20.1 Ureteral calculus (right distal ureter)**
- **R31.9 Hematuria, unspecified (microscopic/gross in context of ureterolithiasis)**

---

## Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 148/92 mmHg; Pulse: 104/min; SpO2: 99%; Resp: 18/min; Temp: 36.8°C; **VAS: 8/10**; **GCS: 15**

### Vital Signs (ED)
| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO2 (%) | Resp (/min) | Temp (°C) | Pain (VAS) | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 06.02.2026 02:14 | 148/92 | 104 | 99 | 18 | 36.8 | 8 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 03:25 | 132/84 | 86 | 99 | 16 | 36.7 | 2 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Arrival
Severe right-sided flank pain radiating to the groin, with nausea and visible blood in urine.

---

## Anamnesis
**History of present illness:**  
Patient reports sudden onset of **right flank pain** beginning ~3 hours prior to arrival, colicky in nature, radiating to the right lower abdomen and groin. Associated **nausea** without vomiting. He noticed **reddish urine** once at home. He feels restless and cannot find a comfortable position. Denies fever, chills, dysuria, urinary frequency/urgency, testicular swelling, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

**Past medical history:** No known chronic illnesses. No prior kidney stones.  
**Past surgical history:** None.  
**Medications:** None regularly.  
**Allergies:** No known drug allergies.  
**Habits:** Non-smoker; occasional alcohol; denies illicit drugs.  
**Functions:** Appetite decreased since pain onset. Bowel movements normal. Urination possible, painful mainly due to colicky pain; no urinary retention.

---

## Physical Examination (Status)
General: Moderate distress due to pain, restless, oriented x3, **eupneic at rest**, afebrile, well perfused.  
Skin/Mucosa: Warm, moist, no rash, non-icteric.  
HEENT: Oropharynx moist, no edema.  
Neck: Supple.  
Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles.  
Heart: Regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild right lower quadrant discomfort without guarding or rebound. **No peritoneal signs.**  
Flanks/Back: **Right costovertebral angle tenderness present.** Left CVA non-tender.  
Genitourinary: External genital exam unremarkable; testes non-tender, no swelling.  
Extremities: No edema; peripheral pulses palpable.  
Neuro: No focal deficits.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### Point-of-care / Bedside
- **Urine dipstick:** Positive for blood (3+), negative nitrites, negative leukocyte esterase.

### Laboratory Results (06.02.2026 02:45)
**CBC**
- WBC **10.8** ×10⁹/L  
- RBC 5.12 ×10¹²/L  
- HGB 152 g/L  
- Hct 0.452 L/L  
- Platelets 238 ×10⁹/L  
- Neutrophils 74% (Abs 7.99 ×10⁹/L)  
- Lymphocytes 17% (Abs 1.84 ×10⁹/L)

**Basic Metabolic Panel**
- Na 140 mmol/L  
- K 4.2 mmol/L  
- Cl 102 mmol/L  
- HCO₃ 25 mmol/L  
- Urea 6.8 mmol/L  
- Creatinine **88 µmol/L** (eGFR **>90** mL/min/1.73m²)  
- Glucose 6.0 mmol/L  
- Calcium 2.33 mmol/L  
- CRP **2.1 mg/L**

**Urinalysis (microscopy)**
- RBCs: **>50 /HPF**  
- WBCs: 0–2 /HPF  
- Bacteria: none seen  
- Crystals: occasional calcium oxalate  
- Protein: trace  
- Specific gravity: 1.020

Interpretation: Hematuria without pyuria; renal function preserved; no inflammatory pattern suggesting UTI.

---

## Imaging

### CT Abdomen/Pelvis (Non-contrast, stone protocol) — 06.02.2026 03:05
**Findings:**  
- **4 mm calculus in the right distal ureter** near the ureterovesical junction.  
- **Mild right hydroureteronephrosis** proximal to the stone.  
- No additional renal stones identified.  
- No appendiceal enlargement or inflammatory changes.  
- No free fluid or free air.

**Impression:** Right distal ureteral stone (4 mm) with mild upstream dilatation.

---

## Therapy (ED)
- **IV ketorolac 15 mg** once (pain improved markedly)  
- **IV ondansetron 4 mg** once  
- **IV fluids:** 0.9% NaCl 500 mL bolus  
- **Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO** once in ED (medical expulsive therapy initiated)

Response: Pain decreased from VAS 8/10 to 2/10; nausea resolved; vitals normalized; patient tolerating oral intake.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical presentation (colicky flank pain radiating to groin), urinalysis (significant hematuria without infection), and CT findings confirm **right distal ureterolithiasis (4 mm)** with **mild hydronephrosis**. Patient is **afebrile**, hemodynamically stable, with **normal renal function** and **no evidence of urinary infection** or obstructive uropathy requiring emergent decompression.

**Disposition:** Discharged home with outpatient management.

**Discharge Medications / Instructions**
- **Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q8h PRN pain**, with food (avoid exceeding recommended daily dose; avoid if gastric ulcer symptoms occur).  
- **Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 1 g PO q8h PRN pain**, do not exceed 3 g/day.  
- **Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO nightly for 14 days** (caution: may cause dizziness/orthostatic hypotension).  
- **Ondansetron 4 mg ODT/PO q8h PRN nausea**.

**Additional instructions**
- Maintain good oral hydration (as tolerated).  
- Strain urine to capture stone for analysis.  
- **Return to ED immediately** for fever/chills, worsening/uncontrolled pain, persistent vomiting/inability to hydrate, decreased urine output, fainting, or any new concerning symptoms.  
- **Urology follow-up** within 1–2 weeks (earlier if symptoms persist), with repeat imaging if stone not passed.

---
gold_reference: No errors

**Documented plan note:** *“Given stable vitals and no headache, CT brain not ordered in ED; patient to be admitted for observation and further work-up.”*  ← **(see error)**

## ED Treatment / Interventions
- IV access x1, blood drawn  
- Continuous monitoring (ECG, SpO₂, BP)  
- NPO until swallow assessment  
- Head of bed elevated 30°  
- 0.9% NaCl at 75 mL/h

**Medications given in ED:** none administered (no thrombolysis/antiplatelet initiated).

## Conclusion
57-year-old male with abrupt onset aphasia and right-sided weakness consistent with acute stroke syndrome. Vitals stable, labs without contraindication signals. ECG sinus rhythm. No acute pulmonary findings on CXR. Patient admitted for further work-up.

gold_reference: With suspected acute stroke (aphasia and unilateral weakness) within hours of onset, emergent brain imaging is required to exclude intracranial hemorrhage and to assess for large vessel occlusion. Without immediate non-contrast CT (± CTA/CTP) and stroke team activation, the patient may miss time-critical reperfusion therapies (IV thrombolysis and/or mechanical thrombectomy).

**Emergency Department Report**

**Patient**: Female, 68 years  
**Date of birth**: 1957-03-14  
**Visit date/time**: 2026-02-06 18:42  
**Mode of arrival**: Walk-in  
**Triage category**: 3

---

## Triage

**Chief complaint**: Dysuria, urinary frequency, right flank pain, fever

**Triage data (18:47)**  
- BP: 128/76 mmHg  
- Pulse: 108/min  
- SpO₂: 97% (room air)  
- Respiratory rate: 18/min  
- Temp: 38.4 °C  
- VAS: 6/10  
- GCS: 15  
- AVPU: A

**Repeat vitals (20:35)**  
- BP: 122/74 mmHg  
- Pulse: 96/min  
- SpO₂: 98%  
- Temp: 37.8 °C  
- Resp: 16/min  

---

## History (Anamnesis)

**History of present illness**:  
2 days of urinary frequency and burning with urination. Since this morning, developed right-sided flank pain radiating toward the groin, chills, nausea (no vomiting), and subjective fever. No vaginal discharge or pelvic pain. No gross hematuria reported. Denies chest pain, dyspnea, or syncope.

**Past medical history**:  
- Atrial fibrillation (on chronic anticoagulation)  
- Hypertension  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet + metformin)  
- Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline eGFR ~50–60 mL/min/1.73 m² per patient)  
- Recurrent UTIs (last episode ~8 months ago)

**Past surgical history**: Cholecystectomy (2009)

**Medications (home)**:  
- Warfarin (dose varies by INR plan; patient reports “5 mg most days”)  
- Metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily  
- Lisinopril 10 mg daily  
- Metformin 500 mg twice daily  
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly

**Allergies**: NKDA

**Social history**: Lives alone, independent. No alcohol misuse. Non-smoker.

---

## Physical Examination

**General**: Ill-appearing but not toxic, alert, oriented, speaking full sentences.  
**HEENT**: Mucous membranes mildly dry.  
**Cardiovascular**: Irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs. Peripheral pulses palpable.  
**Respiratory**: Lungs clear bilaterally, no increased work of breathing.  
**Abdomen**: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness. No guarding or rebound.  
**Flank/CVA**: Right costovertebral angle tenderness present.  
**Extremities/Skin**: No edema, no rash.  
**Neuro**: Grossly intact, no focal deficits.

---

## Diagnostics

### ECG (19:10)
- Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response ~98/min  
- No acute ischemic ST-T changes

### Laboratory Results (19:25)

**Hematology – CBC**  
- WBC 14.8 ×10⁹/L (H)  
- RBC 4.21 ×10¹²/L  
- Hb 126 g/L  
- Hct 0.381 L/L  
- MCV 90.5 fL  
- Platelets 214 ×10⁹/L  

**Differential**  
- NEU% 86% (H), NEU# 12.7 ×10⁹/L (H)  
- LYM% 8% (L), LYM# 1.2 ×10⁹/L  
- MONO% 5%, EOS% 1%, BASO% 0%

**Inflammation**  
- CRP 86 mg/L (H)

**Metabolic / Renal**  
- Glucose 9.8 mmol/L (H)  
- Urea 9.2 mmol/L (H)  
- Creatinine 112 µmol/L (H)  
- eGFR 49 mL/min/1.73m² (L)

**Electrolytes**  
- Na 137 mmol/L  
- K 4.6 mmol/L  
- Cl 102 mmol/L  
- CO₂ (bicarb) 22 mmol/L  
- Ca 2.24 mmol/L  
- Mg 0.78 mmol/L

**Liver enzymes**  
- AST 22 U/L  
- ALT 18 U/L  
- ALP 88 U/L  
- Total bilirubin 11 µmol/L

**Coagulation**  
- INR 2.3 (therapeutic for AF)  
- APTT 31 s

**Urinalysis (point-of-care)**  
- Leukocyte esterase: +++  
- Nitrites: positive  
- Blood: ++  
- Protein: +  
- Ketones: negative  
- Glucose: trace  
**Urine microscopy**: WBC >50/hpf, bacteria +++  
**Urine culture**: sent (pending)

**Blood cultures**: drawn ×2 (pending)

### Imaging

**Renal/Bladder ultrasound (21:05)**  
- Kidneys normal size and echogenicity  
- No hydronephrosis  
- No visible obstructing calculus  
- Bladder: mild residual volume, no mass

---

## ED Assessment

**Working diagnosis**: Acute pyelonephritis (right-sided), uncomplicated by obstruction.  
**Severity**: Moderate (fever, leukocytosis, flank pain), hemodynamically stable, tolerating oral intake after antiemetic.

---

## ED Treatment / Interventions

- IV access established  
- IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 500 mL bolus  
- Antipyretic/analgesic: paracetamol 1 g PO  
- Antiemetic: ondansetron 4 mg IV  
- Antibiotics in ED: ceftriaxone 1 g IV single dose

**Discharge prescription**:  
- **Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160/800 mg PO, 1 tablet twice daily for 10 days**  
- Paracetamol 1 g PO every 8 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3 g/day)  
- Encourage oral hydration

---

## Disposition and Follow-up

**Disposition**: Discharged home (22:10), improved pain and nausea; afebrile trend; stable vitals.

**Return precautions**: Worsening fever, rigors, persistent vomiting, confusion, inability to take fluids/meds, worsening flank pain, or no improvement within 48 hours.

**Follow-up**:  
- Primary care in 24–48 hours  
- Review urine culture results when available; adjust antibiotic if needed

---

## Conclusion

Findings (fever, right CVA tenderness, positive nitrites/leukocytes, leukocytosis, elevated CRP) are consistent with **acute right-sided pyelonephritis**. Ultrasound shows **no obstruction/hydronephrosis**. Treated with IV ceftriaxone in ED and discharged on oral antibiotic therapy with instructions and follow-up.

---
gold_reference: The patient is on chronic warfarin with a therapeutic INR (2.3). TMP-SMX is a high-risk interaction that can markedly increase warfarin effect (CYP2C9 inhibition and reduced vitamin K-producing gut flora), leading to dangerously elevated INR and major bleeding. The report prescribes TMP-SMX without acknowledging the interaction, arranging close INR monitoring, or selecting a safer alternative.

2) Non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis (CT KUB; reported 06.02.2026 15:18):  
Findings and Opinion:

• Mild right hydroureteronephrosis.  
• No perinephric collection.  
• No additional renal calculi identified.  
• No appendiceal pathology (status post appendectomy).  
• No bowel obstruction or free air.  
Conclusion: Right distal ureterolithiasis (4 mm) with mild upstream dilatation.  
________________________________________  
Therapy in the Emergency Department

• IV access, monitoring.  
• 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV.  
• Ketorolac 30 mg IV (single dose).  
• Ondansetron 4 mg IV.  
• Pain reassessment after treatment: VAS decreased from 8/10 to 2–3/10; patient comfortable at rest.  
________________________________________  
Clinical Course / Observation

• Hemodynamically stable throughout.  
• Afebrile, no laboratory evidence of infection (normal CRP, no leukocytosis; urinalysis without pyuria or nitrites).  
• Renal function preserved.  
• CT confirms small distal ureteral stone with mild obstruction only.  
• No indication for emergent urologic intervention at this time (no sepsis, no acute kidney injury, pain controlled).  
________________________________________  
Discharge Plan / Conclusion

Diagnosis:  
• Right distal ureteral stone (4 mm) with mild hydroureteronephrosis—renal colic.

Discharge Medications / Instructions:  
• Tamsulosin 0.4 mg: 1 capsule nightly for 14 days (medical expulsive therapy).  
• Ibuprofen 400 mg: 1 tablet every 8 hours as needed for pain (with food; avoid exceeding recommended daily dose).  
• If NSAIDs contraindicated or insufficient: paracetamol as per label dosing.  
• Encourage oral hydration; strain urine to capture stone if passed.

Follow-up:  
• Outpatient urology follow-up within 1–2 weeks (earlier if persistent symptoms).  
• Repeat ultrasound or imaging as advised by urology/primary care to confirm resolution.

Return immediately to ED if any of the following occur:  
• Fever/chills, worsening flank pain not controlled with medication, persistent vomiting/inability to hydrate, decreased urination, fainting, or new/worsening symptoms.
